questions.hint: This can be solved with symmetry. How do you compare the probability that your number is strictly higher than the dealer's with the probability that the dealer's number is strictly higher than yours? What is the probability that the two numbers are the same?
hints: By symmetry P(you higher)=P(dealer higher); the rest is a tie.
P(tie in rank)=3/51=1/17.
questions.genSolution: By symmetry $P(\text{you higher})=P(\text{dealer higher})$, and the remaining probability is a rank tie. Given your card, $3$ of the other $51$ share its rank, so $P(\text{tie})=\tfrac3{51}=\tfrac1{17}$. Hence $P(\text{win})=\tfrac{1-1/17}{2}=\tfrac{8}{17}.$

questions.title: Crazy Covariance
questions.question: Let $X \sim \text{Exp}(1)$ and $Y \mid X = x \sim \text{LogNorm}(0,x)$. Find Cov$(X,Y)$. Note that we say a random variable $R \sim \text{LogNorm}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ if $\log(R) \sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)$.
questions.hint: Cov$(X,Y) = \mathbb{E}[XY] - \mathbb{E}[X]\mathbb{E}[Y]$. Use LOTUS to compute this afterwards.
hints: E[Y|X=x]=e^{x/2} (lognormal mean). Use Cov=E[XY]−E[X]E[Y] with the tower rule.
E[Y]=E[e^{X/2}]=2; E[XY]=E[X e^{X/2}]=4 (Exp(1) integrals).
questions.genSolution: Since $Y\mid X=x\sim\mathrm{LogNorm}(0,x)$, $\mathbb{E}[Y\mid X]=e^{X/2}$. With $X\sim\mathrm{Exp}(1)$: $\mathbb{E}[Y]=\mathbb{E}[e^{X/2}]=\int_0^\infty e^{x/2}e^{-x}dx=2$ and $\mathbb{E}[XY]=\mathbb{E}[Xe^{X/2}]=\int_0^\infty x\,e^{-x/2}dx=\tfrac{1}{(1/2)^2}=4$. Thus $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y)=4-1\cdot2=2.$

questions.title: Non-Consecutive Sequence
questions.question: Suppose that you flip a coin $n$ times and it turns out that no consecutive heads appear in the sequence. Let $E(n)$ be the expected number of heads that appear given this information. Compute $\displaystyle \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} \dfrac{E(n)}{n}$. The answer is in the form $\dfrac{a}{b + \sqrt{c}}$ for integers $a,b,$ and $c$ with $c$ minimal. Find $abc$.
questions.hint: Break it up into steps. First, compute the conditional probability the sequence starts with $H$. Then, derive a second-order recurrence for $E(n)$ with $A(n)$ as coefficients. Then, suppose $E(n) \sim cn$ and compute $c$.
hints: Among strings with no 'HH', use the transfer matrix [[1,1],[1,0]] (states T,H).
The limiting frequency of H equals u_H v_H/(u·v) with the Perron eigenvectors: 1/(λ²+1).
questions.genSolution: Strings with no consecutive heads form the golden-mean shift with transfer matrix $M=\begin{psmallmatrix}1&1\\1&0\end{psmallmatrix}$ (states $T,H$) and Perron eigenvalue $\lambda=\tfrac{1+\sqrt5}2$. Both eigenvectors are $(\lambda,1)$, so the limiting fraction of $H$ positions is $\dfrac{u_H v_H}{u\cdot v}=\dfrac{1}{\lambda^2+1}$. Since $\lambda^2+1=\tfrac{5+\sqrt5}2$, the limit is $\dfrac{2}{5+\sqrt5}$. Hence $a=2,b=5,c=5$ and $abc=50.$

questions.title: Maximize Head Ratio II
questions.question: Say that you are flipping a fair coin where you can stop flipping whenever you want. Your goal is to maximize the ratio between the number of heads you get with the total number of flips. The ratios are in the form $a:1$. What is the expectation of $a$ when you follow the strategy of stopping when you have more heads than tails? Round your answer to the nearest thousandths.
questions.hint: When trying to find the probability of outcomes, use Dyck words. The Taylor Series for $\arcsin(x) = \displaystyle \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{4^n(2n+1)}\binom{2n}{n}x^{2n+1}$
hints: Stopping the first time heads exceed tails relates to Dyck-path/ballot counts.
Summing the resulting ratio over stopping times uses the arcsin Taylor series at x=1.
The expected ratio works out to π/4 ≈ 0.785.
questions.genSolution: Stopping the first time heads exceed tails, the stopping-length probabilities are Catalan/Dyck-type terms. Summing the head-ratio against these weights collapses (via the given $\arcsin$ series evaluated toward $x=1$) to $\mathbb{E}[a]=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\approx0.785.$

questions.title: Double Aces
questions.question: What is the probability of taking out $13$ cards from a well shuffled standard $52$ card deck and getting exactly $2$ Aces? Round your answer to the nearest hundredths.
questions.hint: The number of aces drawn in this question follows a Hypergeometric distribution.
hints: Number of aces in 13 cards is hypergeometric.
questions.genSolution:
The number of aces among $13$ drawn is hypergeometric:
$$P=\frac{\binom{4}{2}\binom{48}{11}}{\binom{52}{13}}=6\cdot\frac{(13\cdot12)(39\cdot38)}{52\cdot51\cdot50\cdot49}\approx0.21.$$

questions.title: DeMorgan's Birthday
questions.question: Augustus DeMorgan (yes, from DeMorgan's Laws) died in $1871$. He used to brag that he was $x$ years old in the year $x^2$. Assuming a lifespan of no longer than $120$ years, what is $x$?
questions.hint: DeMorgan must have been born somewhere between $1751$ and $1871$. What possible squares are there?
hints: He was x in year x², so born in x²−x, between 1751 and 1871.
Need x²≤1871 ⇒ x≤43; check x=43.
questions.genSolution: Being $x$ years old in year $x^2$ means he was born in $x^2-x$, which must lie between $1751$ and $1871$. Since $x^2\le1871$, $x\le43$; $x=43$ gives $43^2=1849$ and birth year $1806$, consistent. So $x=43.$

questions.title: Contracts and Pricing III
questions.question: After being caught in an embarrassing high-profile data fabrication scandal, Calambya University decides to stop sharing data with the national college ranking organization, effective in 2023. Rival school Mahogany University is overjoyed and offers each student a tuition refund worth $X$ thousand dollars, where $X = \text{Calambya's 2023 Ranking} - \text{Mahogany's 2023 Ranking}$. One Mahogany student, Justin, offers to sell the following contract to his friend Kevin for $0.70$ thousand dollars, before $X$ is known: Justin will give Kevin the right but not the obligation to purchase Justin's tuition refund for only $0.5$ thousand dollars. Suppose $X \sim \text{Exp}(\beta)$, where $\beta$ is the scale. Compute $\beta$ to the nearest tenth under the assumption that the contract is priced fairly.
questions.hint: Condition on the value of $X$ when computing the expected gain/loss.
hints: Kevin holds a call on X with strike 0.5; fair price 0.70 = E[max(X−0.5,0)].
For X~Exp(scale β), E[max(X−K,0)] = β e^{−K/β}.
questions.genSolution: Kevin's contract pays $\max(X-0.5,0)$ (a call struck at $0.5$). For $X\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\text{scale }\beta)$, $\mathbb{E}[\max(X-K,0)]=\beta e^{-K/\beta}$. Fair pricing requires $0.70=\beta e^{-0.5/\beta}$; solving gives $\beta\approx1.1.$

questions.title: Snowman Surface
questions.question: A snowman is created by placing three spherical snowballs of radii $8,11,$ and $14$ inches on top of one another in decending size. Assume that every single spot of each sphere is visible on the snowman, you uniformly pick a  random point on the surface of the snowman. Find the probability that this point is at most $39$ inches above the ground.
questions.hint: Think about the total surface area of the snowman (Use the formula for surface area of a sphere), then ask yourself, what proportion of it is at most 39 inches from the ground.
hints: A uniform surface point: probabilities are area ratios; use 4πr² and Archimedes' zone law.
Bottom sphere (r=14, 0–28 ft) all ≤39; middle (r=11, center at 39) contributes its lower half; top all >39.
questions.genSolution:
The bottom sphere ($r=14$) spans heights $0$–$28$ (all $\le39$): area $4\pi\cdot196$. The middle sphere ($r=11$) is centered at $39$, so exactly its lower half is $\le39$: area $\tfrac12\cdot4\pi\cdot121=2\pi\cdot121$. The top sphere lies above $50$: nothing. Total area $=4\pi(196+121+64)$. So
$$P=\frac{4\pi\cdot196+2\pi\cdot121}{4\pi(381)}=\frac{1026}{1524}=\frac{171}{254}.$$

questions.title: Non-Disjoint Subsets
questions.question: Let $A$ and $B$ be uniformly at random selected subsets of $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$. Find the probability that $A$ and $B$ are not disjoint.
questions.hint: There are $2^5 = 32$ ways to select each of $A$ and $B$, as there are $2^5$ subsets of a set of size $5$. Note that each of the $5$ elements are equally-likely to belong to $A\cap B, A\cap B^c, A^c\cap B,$ and $A^c \cap B^c$.
hints: Disjoint means no element is in both A and B.
Per element, 4 equally likely (in A, in B, both, neither); not-both has prob 3/4.
questions.genSolution: For each of the $5$ elements, its membership in $(A,B)$ is one of $4$ equally likely cases; disjointness needs it not to be in both, probability $\tfrac34$. By independence $P(\text{disjoint})=(\tfrac34)^5=\tfrac{243}{1024}$, so $P(\text{not disjoint})=1-\tfrac{243}{1024}=\tfrac{781}{1024}.$

questions.title: Perfect Correlation II
questions.question: Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ are perfectly correlated i.e. $\rho(X,Y) = 1$ with $\sigma_Y < \sigma_X$. Find the correlation of $X+Y$ and $X-Y$.
questions.hint: Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ have respective variances $\sigma_X^2$ and $\sigma_Y^2$. Compute $\text{Cov}(X+Y,X-Y)$ and their respective variances using the common formulae.
hints: Same setup as perfect-correlation-I but now σ_X>σ_Y.
ρ(X+Y,X−Y)=(σ_X²−σ_Y²)/[(σ_X+σ_Y)|σ_X−σ_Y|].
With σ_X>σ_Y the sign is +1.
questions.genSolution: With $\rho=1$, $\mathrm{Cov}(X+Y,X-Y)=\sigma_X^2-\sigma_Y^2$, $\mathrm{Var}(X+Y)=(\sigma_X+\sigma_Y)^2$, $\mathrm{Var}(X-Y)=(\sigma_X-\sigma_Y)^2$. So $\rho(X+Y,X-Y)=\dfrac{\sigma_X^2-\sigma_Y^2}{(\sigma_X+\sigma_Y)(\sigma_X-\sigma_Y)}=1$ (since $\sigma_X>\sigma_Y$).

questions.title: Brownian Difference
questions.question: Let $B_t$ be a standard Brownian Motion. Fix $t > 0$, and define $\Delta_{m,n} = B_{tm2^{-n}} - B_{t(m-1)2^{-n}}$. Evaluate $\mathbb{E}\left[\left(\displaystyle \sum_{m=1}^{2^n}\Delta_{m,n}^2 - t\right)^2\right]$ as a function of $n$ and $t$. Evaluate this function with $t = 1$ and $n = 5$.
questions.hint: By expanding out the interior, $\mathbb{E}\left[\left(\displaystyle \sum_{m=1}^{2^n}\Delta_{m,n}^2 - t\right)^2\right] = \displaystyle \mathbb{E}\left[\left(\displaystyle \sum_{m=1}^{2^n}\Delta_{m,n}^2\right)^2 - 2t \sum_{m=1}^{2^n} \Delta_{m,n}^2 + t^2\right]$. Apply linearity of expectation. Use properties of Brownian motion here to evaluate each of the interiors.
hints: Σ Δ² has mean t, so the expression is Var(Σ Δ²).
Each Δ ~ N(0, δ) with δ=t2^{−n}; Var(Δ²)=2δ². Sum over 2^n terms.
questions.genSolution: $\sum_m\Delta_{m,n}^2$ has mean $t$, so the quantity is $\mathrm{Var}\big(\sum_m\Delta_{m,n}^2\big)$. Each increment is $N(0,\delta)$ with $\delta=t2^{-n}$, and $\mathrm{Var}(\Delta^2)=2\delta^2$. Summing the $2^n$ independent terms: $2^n\cdot2(t2^{-n})^2=2t^2 2^{-n}$. At $t=1,n=5$: $2\cdot2^{-5}=\tfrac1{16}.$

questions.title: Mixed Set II
questions.question: How many subsets of $\{1,2,\dots, 10\}$ contain exactly $2$ elements from $\{1,2,3\}$ AND only have odd elements?
questions.hint: Consider breaking the selection process up into two parts.
hints: 'Only odd elements' restricts to {1,3,5,7,9}; the only odd members of {1,2,3} are 1,3.
Exactly 2 from {1,2,3} forces including both 1 and 3.
Free choices: any subset of {5,7,9} ⇒ 2³=8.
questions.genSolution: Only odd elements are allowed, so the subset lies in $\{1,3,5,7,9\}$. The odd elements of $\{1,2,3\}$ are just $1,3$, so 'exactly two from $\{1,2,3\}$' forces both $1$ and $3$ in. The remaining elements are any subset of $\{5,7,9\}$: $2^3=8.$

questions.title: Laser Game
questions.question: Mordecai has a large target of radius $10$ and a laser pointer. He is clumsy, so he points the laser at a uniformly random point on the target. Let $D$ represent the random distance from the laser to the center of the target. He offers you to play the following game: Mordecai points the laser at the target one time. Before doing so, you fix a value $0 \leq r \leq 10$. If your laser is within distance $r$ away from the center, you must pay Mordecai $\$(10-r)$. Otherwise, Mordecai pays you $\$r$. Find the value of $r$ that maximizes your expected winnings from this game.
questions.hint: Calculate the probability the dart is within or outside a distance $r$ and optimize the quadratic.
hints: P(D≤r)=r²/100 (area ratio). Write your expected winnings.
W(r)=−(10−r)(r²/100)+r(1−r²/100) simplifies to r − r²/10.
Maximize: W'(r)=1−r/5=0 ⇒ r=5.
questions.genSolution:
Since the point is uniform on the disk, $P(D\le r)=\tfrac{r^2}{100}$. Your expected winnings are
$$W(r)=-(10-r)\tfrac{r^2}{100}+r\Big(1-\tfrac{r^2}{100}\Big)=r-\tfrac{r^2}{10}.$$
Then $W'(r)=1-\tfrac r5=0\Rightarrow r=5.$

questions.title: Smallest Factorizaiton
questions.question: The number $1234567890$ is not prime, so it can be written in the form $ab$ for two positive integers $a$ and $b$. Find $a^* + b^*$, where $a^*$ and $b^*$ satisfy $a^*b^* = 1234567890$ and for any other pair $(a,b)$ such that $ab = 1234567890$, $|a-b| \geq |a^* - b^*|$. It may be helpful to consider the prime factorization.
questions.hint: With a little bit of work, one can show that the prime factorization of $1234567890 = 2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 5 \cdot 3607 \cdot 3803$.
hints: Factorize: 1234567890 = 2·3²·5·3607·3803; the closest factor pair splits the two big primes.
Put 3607 and 3803 on opposite sides and balance with the small factors (product 90).
questions.genSolution: With $1234567890=2\cdot3^2\cdot5\cdot3607\cdot3803$, a balanced factorization keeps the two large primes apart and splits the small factors (product $90$) to equalize. Setting $a=3607\cdot10=36070$ and $b=3803\cdot9=34227$ gives the smallest gap, so $a^*+b^*=36070+34227=70297.$

questions.title: Delta Decay II
questions.question:
You have a European straddle (not necessary at-the-money) at strike $K = 25$ and with underlying price $S_0 = 27$.

The call option has $\Delta = 0.72$ and the put option has $\Delta = -0.28$.  You are delta-hedged by buying (or selling) the underlying such that the overall porfolio $\Delta$ is $0$ and the straddle expires in exactly $1$ hour.  $$$$Let's say $30$ minutes pass and the underlying remains at the same price, how many units of the underlying do you need to buy/sell to remain delta-hedged? Enter $-x$ if your answer is to sell $x$ units (fractional quantities are allowed)
questions.hint: Assume the delta will decay equally within the last hour
hints: Straddle delta = call Δ + put Δ = 0.72 − 0.28 = 0.44; you're short 0.44 shares.
ITM call → 1 and OTM put → 0 by expiry; decay linearly over the hour. At 30 min: call 0.86, put −0.14.
New straddle delta 0.72; you must sell 0.72−0.44 = 0.28 more ⇒ −0.28.
questions.genSolution: The straddle's delta is $0.72+(-0.28)=0.44$, hedged by shorting $0.44$ shares. As expiry nears the ITM call's delta $\to1$ and the OTM put's $\to0$; decaying linearly over the hour, after $30$ minutes the call is $0.86$ and the put $-0.14$, giving straddle delta $0.72$. To re-hedge you short an extra $0.72-0.44=0.28$ shares, i.e. $-0.28.$

questions.title: Primitive Preference
questions.question: On Earth II, every couple prefers to have a baby boy over a baby girl. Assume that there is an equally likely chance of having a boy or girl, and that the genders of children are mutually independent. If each couple continues on having more children until they have a boy, in which they will stop having more children, what does the percentage of boys in this society approach over time?
questions.hint: The percentage of boys in this society is dependent on nature, not on couples' preferences.
hints: Each birth is independently 50/50 regardless of any stopping rule.
Stopping rules can't change the sex ratio of births.
The proportion of boys → 50% (0.5).
questions.genSolution: Each birth is an independent fair coin flip; a family's decision to stop after a boy doesn't change the odds of any individual birth. So across all births boys and girls remain equally likely, and the percentage of boys approaches $50\%$ ($0.5$).

questions.title: 12 Left
questions.question: Three players, say $A,B,$ and $C$, are going to draw $5$ chips each (without replacement) out of a bag of $15$ chips numbers $1-15$. The order of selection is $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$. Given that player $A$ only selected even-numbered chips, find the probability that player $B$ selected Chip $12$.
questions.hint: There are $7$ even values from $1-15$. For $B$ to be able to select Chip $12$, $A$ must have not selected it. What is the probability that $A$ selected $12$ given they only selected from the even values.
hints: A drew 5 of the 7 even chips; chip 12 is even. First, did A take 12?
P(A took 12)=5/7, so chip 12 is still available with prob 2/7.
If available, B draws 5 of the remaining 10: P=1/2. Total = (2/7)(1/2)=1/7.
questions.genSolution: There are $7$ even chips; A took $5$ of them, so chip $12$ was taken by A with probability $\tfrac57$, leaving it available with probability $\tfrac27$. If available, it's among the $10$ remaining chips and B draws $5$, so $P(\text{B gets it})=\tfrac{5}{10}=\tfrac12$. Thus $P=\tfrac27\cdot\tfrac12=\tfrac17.$

questions.title: Russian Roulette I
questions.question: You and your friend are bored one day and decide to play Russian Roulette. The six-chambered revolver is loaded with one bullet. To start the game the cylinder is spun to randomize the order of the chambers. The two of you take turns pulling the trigger until the person who fires the gun loses. Considering the cylinder is not re-spun after each turn, what is the probability that you win if your friend goes first?
questions.hint: The cylinder is only spun once, at most there are 6 possible turns, consider in which turns you win.
hints: One spin fixes the bullet's chamber uniformly among 6; friend fires chambers 1,3,5 and you 2,4,6.
You win if the bullet is in one of the friend's chambers.
questions.genSolution: After the single spin the bullet is equally likely in any of the $6$ chambers. The friend (first) pulls chambers $1,3,5$ and you pull $2,4,6$. You win iff the bullet sits in a friend's chamber, probability $\tfrac{3}{6}=\tfrac12.$

questions.title: Deriving Put-Call Parity II
questions.question:
You have access to the underlying $S$, which has an initial price $S_0 = 8$, bonds that pay $1$ at time-$T$, where $T=1$. The interest rates are $0.02$, continuously compounded. Finally, you have access to three different put options of varying strikes. The puts are given in the format of $(\text{Strike } K, \text{Price } C_0)$


 $$ 
\begin{align*}
(5, 0.4) \\ 
(10, 3.2) \\ 
(15, 5.6) \\ 
\end{align*}
 $$ 


Find the time-$0$ price of a call option with strike $K = 10$. Round to two decimal points.
questions.hint: What is the payoff of a call?
hints: Put–call parity: C − P = S₀ − K·e^{−rT}.

questions.title: Breakeven Price I
questions.question: Given you buy a call option for $\$3$ at a strike price of $\$4$, what is the breakeven price?
questions.hint: Think about the payoff of a call option and how much you would pay for it
hints: A long call profits once the stock clears the strike by the premium paid.
Breakeven = strike + premium.
questions.genSolution: A long call breaks even when the payoff $S_T-K$ equals the premium, i.e. $S_T=K+\text{premium}=4+3=7.$

questions.title: Integral Variance IV
questions.question: Let $W_t$ be a standard Brownian Motion. Find the variance of $X = \displaystyle \int_0^2 \sqrt{t} e^{\frac{W_t^2}{8}}dW_t$.
questions.hint: Since the integrand is "nice enough" here, we can see that the mean of $X$ is $0$. Use Ito's Isometry for the second moment.
hints: Mean is 0; by Itô isometry Var=E[∫_0^2 t e^{W_t²/4} dt].
E[e^{W_t²/4}]=1/√(1−t/2) (Gaussian quadratic exponential).
questions.genSolution:
The Itô integral has mean $0$, so by Itô's isometry $\mathrm{Var}(X)=\mathbb{E}\!\int_0^2 t\,e^{W_t^2/4}dt=\int_0^2 t\,\mathbb{E}[e^{W_t^2/4}]dt$. Since $W_t\sim N(0,t)$, $\mathbb{E}[e^{W_t^2/4}]=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-t/2}}$. Substituting $u=1-t/2$,
$$\int_0^2\frac{t}{\sqrt{1-t/2}}dt=4\int_0^1\frac{1-u}{\sqrt u}du=4\Big(2-\tfrac23\Big)=\frac{16}{3}.$$

questions.title: Positive Brownian II
questions.question: Let $B_t$ be a standard Brownian Motion. Find $\mathbb{P}[B_2 > 0, B_8 > 0]$.
questions.hint: Start off with a similar trick to Positive Brownian I. Attempt converting the resulting integral to polar coordinates.
hints: (B₂,B₈) is bivariate normal with correlation ρ=Cov/√(2·8)=2/4=1/2.
For mean-zero bivariate normal, P(both>0)=1/4 + arcsin(ρ)/(2π).
questions.genSolution: $(B_2,B_8)$ is jointly normal with $\mathrm{Cov}(B_2,B_8)=2$, so correlation $\rho=\tfrac{2}{\sqrt{2\cdot8}}=\tfrac12$. For a mean-zero bivariate normal, $P(B_2>0,B_8>0)=\tfrac14+\tfrac{\arcsin\rho}{2\pi}=\tfrac14+\tfrac{\pi/6}{2\pi}=\tfrac14+\tfrac1{12}=\tfrac13.$

questions.title: Bakery Boxes
questions.question: A bakery manager uniformly at random selects an integer (name it $k$) between 1 and 4, inclusive. He then chooses from $k$ distinct desert types at his shop to create a gift basket consisting of $k$ items. Find the expected number of ways that the manager can create the gift basket.
questions.hint: Condition on the number that the bakery manager selects. Then, you can use the Stars and Bars method to compute the number of baskets.
hints: For a given k, baskets of k items from k types (repetition allowed) = C(2k−1, k).
Average over k=1..4 uniformly.
questions.genSolution: For a chosen $k$, the number of size-$k$ multisets from $k$ types is $\binom{2k-1}{k}$. These are $1,3,10,35$ for $k=1,2,3,4$. Averaging uniformly, $\mathbb{E}=\tfrac14(1+3+10+35)=\tfrac{49}{4}.$

questions.title: 4 Die Sum
questions.question: Calculate the probability that when we roll $4$ fair $6-$sided dice, the sum of their upfaces is 20.
questions.hint: Count the different permutations that sum of $20$. Alternatively, condition on the sum of the first $2$ dice.
hints: By symmetry sum=20 has the same count as sum=8 (reflect each die x→7−x).
Count x₁+…+x₄=8 with 1≤xᵢ≤6: stars and bars gives C(7,3)=35 (no value exceeds 6).
questions.genSolution: By the reflection $x_i\mapsto7-x_i$, the number of ways to total $20$ equals the number to total $8$. For $x_1+\dots+x_4=8$ with each $\ge1$, stars and bars gives $\binom{7}{3}=35$ (and no die exceeds $6$). So $P=\dfrac{35}{1296}.$

questions.title: Big Bubble II
questions.question: A spherical bubble has a radius increasing at a rate of $\dfrac{9}{4\pi}$ inches per second. At the moment when the volume of the bubble is increasing at $36$ cubic inches per second, what rate is the surface area increasing at (in square inches per second)?
questions.hint: We know that $V(r) = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ and $S(r) = 4\pi r^2$ represent the volume and the surface area of the bubble as a function of radius. Take the derivative of the volume function to solve for the radius first.
hints: dV/dt = 4πr²·dr/dt; solve for r when dV/dt=36 with dr/dt=9/(4π).
questions.genSolution: $\frac{dV}{dt}=4\pi r^2\frac{dr}{dt}$. With $\frac{dr}{dt}=\frac{9}{4\pi}$ and $\frac{dV}{dt}=36$: $9r^2=36\Rightarrow r=2$. Then $\frac{dS}{dt}=8\pi r\frac{dr}{dt}=8\pi\cdot2\cdot\frac{9}{4\pi}=36$ square inches per second.

questions.title: Digit Halving
questions.question: Which two digit integer has the unique property that the product of its digits is equal to half of the integer?
questions.hint: Let $x=\text{a}\text{b}$ be the integer. Then $x = 10a + b$. In addition, you know that $ab = \dfrac{x}{2}$.
hints: Let the number be 10a+b with digit product ab = (10a+b)/2.
Rearrange: b(2a−1)=10a ⇒ b=10a/(2a−1).
questions.genSolution: Let the number be $10a+b$ with $ab=\tfrac{10a+b}{2}$. Then $2ab=10a+b\Rightarrow b(2a-1)=10a\Rightarrow b=\tfrac{10a}{2a-1}$. Testing digits, $a=3$ gives $b=6$, so the number is $36$ (and $3\cdot6=18=36/2$).

questions.title: Statistical Test Review I
questions.question: We want to test whether or not a coin is balanced based on the number of heads, $X$, that appear after $36$ tosses of the coin. Let's say we use the rejection region $|x - 18| \geq 4$. What is the value of $\alpha$ to the nearest thousandth?
questions.hint: Recall, by definition, that $\alpha$ is the probability that the test statistic, $X$, is within the rejection region when $H_0$ is true.
hints: Under H₀, X~Binom(36,½): mean 18, sd 3. Reject if x≤14 or x≥22.
α=P(|X−18|≥4); approximate with continuity correction.
questions.genSolution: Under $H_0$, $X\sim\mathrm{Binom}(36,\tfrac12)$ with mean $18$, sd $3$. The rejection region $|x-18|\ge4$ is $x\le14$ or $x\ge22$. With a normal approximation and continuity correction, $\alpha=2\,P(X\le14)\approx2\,\Phi\!\big(\tfrac{14.5-18}{3}\big)=2\,\Phi(-1.167)\approx0.243.$

questions.title: Boys with Girls
questions.question: There are $15$ boys and $10$ girls in a class. They line up in a row in random order. What is the expected amount of times a boy and a girl are standing next to each other? Ex: $BGBBGGGBGGBBBBG$ has $7$ occurrences where a boy and girl are next to each other.
questions.hint: Try to find the probability of a boy and girl standing next to each other for each space they can.
hints: There are 24 adjacent pairs in a row of 25; use an indicator per pair.
P(a given pair is one boy + one girl)=2·(15/25)(10/24)=1/2.
questions.genSolution: In a row of $25$ there are $24$ adjacent pairs. A given pair is a boy–girl pair with probability $2\cdot\tfrac{15}{25}\cdot\tfrac{10}{24}=\tfrac12$. By linearity, $\mathbb{E}=24\cdot\tfrac12=12.$

questions.title: Uniform Order I
questions.question: Let $X_1,X_2,\dots,X_{20}$ be IID Unif$(0,1)$ random variables. Compute $\mathbb{P}[X_1 > \text{max}\{X_2,X_{10},X_{15}\}]$
questions.hint: Can you write another probability statement in the form of $X_1 = \text{(something)}$?
hints: The event is that X₁ is the largest of the 4 IID values X₁,X₂,X₁₀,X₁₅.
By symmetry each is equally likely to be the max.
questions.genSolution: $X_1>\max\{X_2,X_{10},X_{15}\}$ means $X_1$ is the largest of those $4$ IID uniforms. By symmetry each is equally likely to be the maximum, so the probability is $\tfrac14.$

questions.title: Close Couples
questions.question: $ n$ married couples ($n$ husbands and $n$ wives) sit at a round table of $2n$ seats at random. The seating scheme is such that the seats must alternate between men and women. Find the number of couples that will sit together $\mathbb{E}[N]$ on average. Assume $n>2$.
questions.hint: Let $I_i$ be the indicator that couple $i$ sits together. How can you use the Linearity of Expectation to solve this problem?
hints: Seats alternate sexes; fix a husband — his two neighbours are women's seats.
His wife occupies one of the n women's seats; P(she's adjacent)=2/n.
questions.genSolution: With seats alternating by sex, each husband has exactly two adjacent women's seats out of $n$. His wife is equally likely in any of the $n$ women's seats, so $P(\text{couple }i\text{ together})=\tfrac2n$. By linearity, $\mathbb{E}[N]=n\cdot\tfrac2n=2.$

questions.title: Hitting MGF
questions.question: Let $B_t$ be a standard Brownian Motion and define $T_a = \text{inf}\{t > 0: |W_t| > a\}$ for $a > 0$. Find $\mathbb{E}[e^{-\lambda T_a}]$ as a function of $\lambda$ and $a$ and evaulate with: ($\lambda = 4$ and $a = \ln(2)$)
questions.hint: Consider the exponential martingale $Z_t = e^{\theta B_t - \theta^2t/2}$.
hints: Use the exponential martingale Z_t=e^{θB_t−θ²t/2} and optional stopping at T_a, where B_{T_a}=±a.
Symmetry gives cosh(θa)·E[e^{−θ²T/2}]=1, so with λ=θ²/2: E[e^{−λT_a}]=1/cosh(a√(2λ)).

questions.title: Circular Birthdays
questions.question: $7$ people sit around a circular table uniformly at random. All of them have a distinct age. Find the probability that they sit down at the table in age order. Note that the ages can be increasing in either the clockwise or counter-clockwise directions.
questions.hint: There are $(7-1)! = 6! = 720$ distinct ways for the people to sit at the table with no restrictions. How many arrangements are there of the people in age order?
hints: Circular seatings of 7 people: (7−1)!=720.
Age order occurs in exactly 2 ways (clockwise or counterclockwise).
questions.genSolution: There are $(7-1)!=720$ distinct circular arrangements. Age order around the table occurs in exactly $2$ of them (increasing clockwise or counterclockwise), so $P=\tfrac{2}{720}=\tfrac1{360}.$

questions.title: Find the Triangle
questions.question: The sides and height of a triangle are four consecutive whole numbers. What is the area of the triangle?
questions.hint: Utilize the Heron's formula to calculate the height of a triangle
hints: Four consecutive integers are three sides plus one height.
Try sides 13,14,15 (a classic Heronian triangle) with the height to side 14.
Area=84 (Heron), and height to base 14 is 12 — so {12,13,14,15} are consecutive. Area=84.
questions.genSolution: Take sides $13,14,15$. By Heron's formula with $s=21$, the area is $\sqrt{21\cdot8\cdot7\cdot6}=\sqrt{7056}=84$. The height to the base $14$ is $\frac{2\cdot84}{14}=12$. So the four consecutive whole numbers are $12,13,14,15$ (height $12$, sides $13,14,15$), and the area is $84.$

questions.title: Terminating Sum
questions.question: Evaluate $\displaystyle \sum_{k \in S} \dfrac{1}{k^3}$, where $S$ is the set of all positive integers such that $\dfrac{1}{k}$ has a terminating decimal expansion. For example, $2 \in S$, as $\dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$ has a finite expansion. However, $7 \notin S$, as $\dfrac{1}{7} = 0.\overline{142857}$ does not terminate.
questions.hint: The prime factorization of $100$ is $100 = 2^2 \cdot 5^2$. Therefore, whatever denominator we have must be in the form $k = 2^a5^b$.
hints: 1/k terminates iff k=2^a·5^b (a,b≥0).
Σ 1/k³ factors as a product of two geometric series.
questions.genSolution:
$1/k$ terminates iff $k=2^a5^b$ with $a,b\ge0$. Then
$$\sum_{k\in S}\frac1{k^3}=\Big(\sum_{a\ge0}\frac1{8^a}\Big)\Big(\sum_{b\ge0}\frac1{125^b}\Big)=\frac{8}{7}\cdot\frac{125}{124}=\frac{1000}{868}=\frac{250}{217}.$$

questions.title: Thank You, Quant!
questions.question: Two quant firms have client inflow that is well-modeled by independent Poisson processes. The respective intensity parameters are $6$ and $10$. Clients give outstanding reviews about the service with respective probabilities $\dfrac{1}{6}$ and $\dfrac{1}{5}$ for the two firms, independent of one another. The clients that give outstanding reviews are sent a thank you card. Find the expected time between clients that receive thank you cards.
questions.hint: Use Poisson thinning to note that the inflow of clients sent thank you cards from either firm is also a Poisson process. What are the parameters for each? How can you relate that to the rate of clients being sent thank you cards?
hints: Poisson thinning: thank-you cards from each firm are Poisson with rate λ·p.
Rates: 6·(1/6)=1 and 10·(1/5)=2; combined rate 3.
Expected inter-arrival time = 1/3.
questions.genSolution: By Poisson thinning, thank-you cards arrive as Poisson processes of rates $6\cdot\tfrac16=1$ and $10\cdot\tfrac15=2$. The superposition has rate $1+2=3$, so the expected time between carded clients is $\tfrac13.$

questions.title: Cylindrical Intersection
questions.question: Two cylinders of radius $1$ intersect at right angles to one another. Furthermore, their central axes also intersect. Find the volume of the enclosed region.
questions.hint: We can model this as two cylinders $x^2 + y^2 = 1$ and $y^2 + z^2 = 1$. Suppose that we fix some $y$. What is the point set $(x,z)$ of interest? What is the shape of it?
hints: This is the Steinmetz solid of two perpendicular cylinders.
Each horizontal cross-section (fixed y) is a square of side 2√(1−y²).
questions.genSolution:
Model the cylinders as $x^2+y^2\le1$ and $y^2+z^2\le1$. At fixed $y$, the cross-section is a square $|x|,|z|\le\sqrt{1-y^2}$ with area $(2\sqrt{1-y^2})^2=4(1-y^2)$. So
$$V=\int_{-1}^{1}4(1-y^2)\,dy=4\Big(2-\tfrac23\Big)=\frac{16}{3}.$$

questions.title: Bolt Variance I
questions.question: A manufacturer produces nuts and bolts that have a diameter variance no larger than .0002 centimeters. A random sample of twelve bolts returned a sample variance of .0003. What is the value of the appropriate test statistic to test, at the 5$\%$ level, $H_0: \sigma ^2 = .0002$ against $H_a : \sigma ^2 > .0002$? Assume independence, variance homogeneity, and that diameter variance is approximately normally distributed.
questions.hint: Note that we are testing a hypothesis concerning variance. What is the appropriate test statistic for this?
hints: Testing a variance uses the chi-square statistic.
questions.genSolution: For a variance test the statistic is $\chi^2=\dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2}=\dfrac{11\cdot0.0003}{0.0002}=11\cdot1.5=16.5.$

questions.title: Pick Your Opponent
questions.question: You are playing in a tennis tournament against Alice and Bob. In the tournament you will play $3$ games, where if you win $2$ consecutive games you win the tournament. The probability you beat Bob is better than beating Alice, let $A$ represents Alice and $B$ represents Bob. Answer $1$ if you would prefer your three games to be $ABA$ and $2$ if you'd like your 3 games to be $BAB$.
questions.hint: Which one of the three games must you win to win $2$ consecutive games?
hints: To win 2 in a row over 3 games, which single game must you always win?
Both winning pairs (1–2 and 2–3) include the middle game, so you must win game 2.
Put the easier opponent (Bob) in the middle ⇒ choose ABA ⇒ answer 1.
questions.genSolution: To win two consecutive games out of three you must win the middle game (it's in both possible consecutive pairs). So you want your strongest matchup in the middle. Since you beat Bob more often, you'd want Bob... but the middle of $ABA$ is Bob, giving you the easy game where it matters most. Hence choose $ABA$: answer $1.$

questions.title: Questionable Values
questions.question: Sean is creating questions for QuantEssential. Each easy question is worth $\$3$, while each medium question is worth $\$6$. He has a question storage saved up of easy and medium questions. If Sean picks a question at random, the probability it is a medium is $1/4$. The total value of Sean's question bank is a perfect square. What is the smallest possible value of his question bank (in dollars)?
questions.hint: There must be exact three times as many easy questions as medium so that the probability of a medium is $1/4$.
hints: P(medium)=1/4 means easy:medium = 3:1.
With m mediums and 3m easys, total value = 9m+6m = 15m.
Smallest perfect-square multiple of 15 is 15·15=225.
questions.genSolution: Since $P(\text{medium})=\tfrac14$, there are $3$ easy per medium: with $m$ mediums the value is $3(3m)+6m=15m$. We need $15m$ to be a perfect square; as $15=3\cdot5$, the smallest is $m=15$, giving $15\cdot15=225.$

questions.title: Colosseum Fight II
questions.question:
Alice and Bob are in Roman times and have $4$ gladiators each. The strengths of each of Alice's gladiators are $1-4$, while Bob's gladiators have strengths $4,5,9,$ and $12$. The tournament is going to consist of Alice and Bob picking gladiators to fight against one another one-at-a-time. Then, the two gladiators fight to the death with no ties. If the two gladiators are of strengths $x$ and $y$, respectively, then the probability that the gladiator with strength $x$ wins is $\dfrac{x}{x+y}$. The winning gladiator will maintain the same strength after the round is over.


 $$ 
 $$ 


Alice is going to pick first for each fight among her remaining gladiators. Afterwards, Bob can select his gladiator (assuming he has one) to go against the one Alice selected. The winner of the tournament is the person who has at least one gladiator left at the end. Does Bob have an optimal strategy $S^*$ such that the probability Bob wins the tournament with strategy $S^*$ is strictly larger than with any other strategy $S \neq S^*$? If so, answer $1$. If not, answer $0$.
questions.hint: In this game, instead of giving the strength $x$ gladiator $x$ balls, give him a light bulb whose lifetime is $\text{Exp}(1/x)$ distributed. How do you define fights and winners now? What is the condition for Bob/Alice to win in terms of the lightbulbs? Why is this equivalent to the original game?
hints: Replace strength-x gladiators with Exp(1/x) lightbulbs; a fight is which bulb burns out first.
By memorylessness the surviving bulb's remaining life is fresh, so the win probability doesn't depend on the order of matchups.
All strategies give the same win probability ⇒ no strictly-better unique strategy ⇒ 0.
questions.genSolution: Model a strength-$x$ gladiator by an $\mathrm{Exp}(1/x)$ lightbulb; a fight is which of two bulbs dies first, with the survivor keeping its (memoryless) remaining life — exactly the $\frac{x}{x+y}$ rule. Because the exponential is memoryless, the eventual winner depends only on the total bulb lifetimes, not on the order Bob chooses. So every strategy yields the same winning probability, and there is no strictly optimal unique strategy: answer $0.$

questions.title: Last Lightbulb
questions.question: Let $X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4 \sim \text{Exp}(1)$ IID represent the lifetimes of $4$ lightbulbs before burning out. Find the expected time until the last lightbulb burns out.
questions.hint: If $X_1,X_2,\dots,X_n \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ IID, then $\text{min}\{X_1,\dots, X_n\} \sim \text{Exp}(n\lambda)$.
hints: The last burnout is the max of 4 Exp(1) lifetimes.
E[max of n Exp(1)] = Σ_{k=1}^n 1/k (harmonic).
questions.genSolution: The last burnout is $\max(X_1,\dots,X_4)$. Using the memoryless decomposition, $\mathbb{E}[\max]=\sum_{k=1}^{4}\tfrac1k=1+\tfrac12+\tfrac13+\tfrac14=\tfrac{25}{12}.$

questions.title: 2 Coin More
questions.question: Tim has $20$ fair coins and Jeff has $22$ fair coins. They both flip their coins. What is the probability that Jeff has more heads than Tim? Round to the nearest thousandth.
questions.hint: Let's consider the first $N=20$ coins. There are 3 possible events: Jeff has more heads, Jeff has equal heads, or Jeff has less heads. For Jeff to win in the next $2$ coins, we need the following to occur. If Jeff has more heads in the first $N$ coins, then Jeff will win with probability $1$. If Jeff has equal heads, then Jeff needs to get at least 1 head in the next $2$ coins.
hints: Compare Jeff's first 20 coins with Tim's 20: a=P(Jeff more), b=P(tie), c=P(Jeff one fewer).
Jeff wins if ahead after 20 (×1), tied then ≥1 head in 2 (×3/4), or one behind then 2 heads (×1/4).
questions.genSolution:
Compare Jeff's first $20$ coins to Tim's $20$. Let $a=P(\text{Jeff ahead})$, $b=P(\text{tie})$, $c=P(\text{Jeff exactly one behind})$; by symmetry $a=\tfrac{1-b}2$. Jeff wins overall if ahead (always), tied then gets $\ge1$ head in his last two ($\tfrac34$), or one behind then $2$ heads ($\tfrac14$):
$$P=a+\tfrac34 b+\tfrac14 c.$$
With $b=\binom{40}{20}/4^{20}\approx0.1254$ and $c=\tfrac{20}{21}b\approx0.1194$, $a\approx0.4373$, giving $P\approx0.561.$

questions.title: Expected Chord Length
questions.question: You uniformly select two random points from the circumference of the unit circle. Find the expected length of the chord (line segment) between the two points, with the answer is in the form $\dfrac{x}{\pi}$ for some rational number $x$. Find $x$.
questions.hint: Let $\theta$ and $\phi$ be IID $\text{Unif}(0,2\pi)$. Picking the two points uniform on the circumference is equivalent to picking these two angles. The measure of the angle between $\theta$ and $\phi$ is $(\theta - \phi)$. To get the length of the line segment connecting the two points, we can take a shortcut: the length here only depends on the distance between our points we select in terms of angle. Use this to fix one of the points at $(1,0)$ and you can do the rest.
hints: Fix one point; the other is at angle θ~U(0,2π). Chord length = 2 sin(θ/2).
questions.genSolution:
By rotational symmetry fix one point and let the angular gap $\theta\sim U(0,2\pi)$; the chord length is $2\sin(\theta/2)$. Then
$$\mathbb{E}=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}2\sin(\theta/2)\,d\theta=\frac1\pi\big[-2\cos(\theta/2)\big]_0^{2\pi}=\frac{4}{\pi},$$
so $x=4.$

questions.title: Consecutive Pairs
questions.question: Consider the set of 10 consecutive integers $\{1, 2, \ldots, 10\}$. How many subsets contain exactly 1 pair of consecutive integers? For example, $\{3, 5, 6, 9\}$ contains exactly 1 pair of consecutive integers.
questions.hint: Suppose we have all 10 integers written out in order on paper. Our goal is to cover some of the integers with tiles such that only two tiles are side-by-side; covered integers belong in a subset of interest. Perhaps we can invent our own special tiles of different shapes that prevent some integers from being next to each other by design; then, we can simply find the number of arrangements of the tiles.
hints: Pick the unique consecutive pair {i,i+1}; remaining elements avoid i−1,i,i+1,i+2 and have no consecutive pair.
Left part {1..i−2} and right {i+3..10} each contribute Fibonacci-many independent sets: F(i)·F(10−i).
questions.genSolution:
Choose the single consecutive pair $\{i,i+1\}$. The other elements must avoid $i-1,i,i+1,i+2$ (so the run isn't extended or touched) and themselves contain no consecutive pair. The left segment $\{1,\dots,i-2\}$ and right segment $\{i+3,\dots,10\}$ independently contribute $F(i)$ and $F(10-i)$ such subsets (Fibonacci counts of non-adjacent subsets). Summing,
$$\sum_{i=1}^{9}F(i)F(10-i)=34+21+26+24+25+24+26+21+34=235.$$

questions.title: Bowl of Cherries I
questions.question: Amy has a bowl of 5 red cherries and 8 purple cherries. She takes out cherries one at a time until there are no red cherries left. What is the probability that the bowl is empty?
questions.hint: The only way for Amy to have no cherries left is if the last cherry drawn from the bowl is red. We're therefore left with a simple ordering problem: how many ways can Amy order $m$ indistinguishable red cherries and $n$ indistinguishable purple cherries such that the last cherry is red?
hints: The bowl empties iff the very last cherry removed is red.
By symmetry each of the 13 cherries is equally likely to be last.
P(last is red)=5/13.
questions.genSolution: She keeps drawing until the reds are gone, so the bowl ends empty exactly when the last cherry overall is red. By symmetry each of the $13$ cherries is equally likely to be last, so the probability is $\tfrac{5}{13}.$

questions.title: Hit Or Miss
questions.question: A particle starts at (4, 4). Each turn, it moves either 1 unit in the $-x$ direction, 1 unit in the $-y$ direction, or 1 unit in each of the $-x$ and $-y$ directions. During a turn, the particle decides how it will move randomly such that the probability of each option is $\frac{1}{3}$. The particle repeatedly takes turns until it hits the $x$ or $y$ axes for the first time. Compute the probability that the particle hits the origin.
questions.hint: In order to hit the origin, the particle must arrive at $(1, 1)$. To do so, the particle can (1) move 3 left and 3 down, (2) move 2 left, 2 down, and 1 diagonal, (3) move 1 left, 1 down, and 2 diagonal, or (4) move down 3 consecutive diagonals.
hints: To hit the origin the particle must reach (1,1) and then move diagonally.
Reaching (1,1) from (4,4) uses d diagonals and 3−d of each axis move: multinomial (6−d)!/((3−d)!²d!)·(1/3)^{6−d}.
Sum over d=0..3 gives 245/729; times 1/3 for the final diagonal ⇒ 245/2187.
questions.genSolution:
To finish at the origin the particle must reach $(1,1)$ then take a diagonal step. Going $(4,4)\to(1,1)$ with $d$ diagonal moves needs $3-d$ left and $3-d$ down moves, in $\binom{6-d}{(3-d),(3-d),d}$ orders, each with probability $(1/3)^{6-d}$:
$$P(\text{reach }(1,1))=\sum_{d=0}^{3}\frac{(6-d)!}{(3-d)!(3-d)!\,d!}\Big(\tfrac13\Big)^{6-d}=\frac{20+90+108+27}{729}=\frac{245}{729}.$$
Multiplying by $\tfrac13$ for the final diagonal step gives $\dfrac{245}{2187}.$

questions.title: Clock Angle II
questions.question: What is the angle between the hands of a clock at $\text{3:15}$ PM (in degrees)?
questions.hint: Its not $0$!
hints: Minute hand at :15 is at 90°. The hour hand has moved past 3.
Difference = 97.5 − 90 = 7.5°.
questions.genSolution: At $15$ minutes the minute hand is at $90^\circ$. The hour hand is at $3.25\times30^\circ=97.5^\circ$. The angle between them is $97.5-90=7.5^\circ.$

questions.title: Sum Over Min Die
questions.question:
You have been tasked to find the expected value of a die game in which you are rolling $2$ dice at a time. Your first roll of the dice will all be summed up and will be your starting score. Your next, and final, roll of the $2$ dice will be your divisor, in which you are going to pick the optimal die out of the two to divide your total by, which will be your final score.

 $$ 
 $$ 

Calculate the expected value of playing this game optimally.
questions.hint: Split this into two parts, first EV of the sum of two dice, then EV of the min of two dice.
hints: Final score = (sum of first roll)/(chosen die of second roll); dividing by the smaller maximizes it.
Independent rolls: EV = E[sum]·E[1/min] = 7·E[1/min].
questions.genSolution: To maximize the score you divide by the smaller of the two second dice, so the final score is $\frac{S}{m}$ with $S$ the first-roll sum (mean $7$) and $m=\min$ of the second roll, independent. Using $P(\min=k)=\frac{(7-k)^2-(6-k)^2}{36}$, $\mathbb{E}[1/m]=\frac1{36}\big(11+\tfrac92\cdot\tfrac1{?}\dots\big)=\frac{1191}{2160}$. Hence $\mathbb{E}=7\cdot\frac{1191}{2160}=\frac{2779}{720}.$

questions.title: Base Exponent Square
questions.question: How many integers $100 \leq k \leq 400$ are such that $k^k$ is a perfect square?
questions.hint: We know that $\sqrt{k^k} = k^{\frac{k}{2}}$ by basic algebra. When is this guaranteed to be an integer?
hints: k^k is a perfect square automatically when k is even (k^{k/2})².
When k is odd, k^k is square iff k itself is a perfect square.
questions.genSolution: If $k$ is even, $k^k=(k^{k/2})^2$ is always a perfect square. If $k$ is odd, $k^k$ (odd exponent) is a square iff $k$ is itself a perfect square. In $[100,400]$ there are $151$ even values, plus the odd squares $121,169,225,289,361$ ($5$ of them). Total $=151+5=156.$

questions.title: Squid Game I
questions.question: 10 contestants are arranged into a line on a bridge and in front of them lay ten left tiles and ten right tiles side by side. In order to cross the bridge, the contestants must cross 10 tiles, and at each step, the person in front must pick either the left or right tile to step on. However, for each left & right tile pair, there is exactly one sturdy tile and one faulty tile, but the contestants cannot tell them apart. The contestants cross the bridge in their assigned order with the first person picking either the left or right tile, and continuing to lead unless either a faulty tile is picked (resulting in elimination) or person one reaches the other side. If the first person is eliminated before reaching the other side, the person second in line assumes the lead picking until he/she is eliminated (or reaches the other side), and so on. The winner of the game is the $\textit{first}$ person to reach the other side. Which position in line (1-indexed) should a contestant choose if they are playing optimally to win?
questions.hint: What is the expected number of tiles a person will get right until they fail?
hints: Each newly-reached tile: the first person to try it guesses wrong (and is eliminated) with prob 1/2, revealing it for the next.
Expected eliminations = sum over 10 tiles of 1/2 = 5.
So ~5 people fall before someone crosses ⇒ choose position 6.
questions.genSolution: Each of the $10$ tiles, when first reached, is guessed wrong by the current leader with probability $\tfrac12$ — an elimination that reveals the safe tile for the next person. By linearity the expected number of eliminations is $10\cdot\tfrac12=5$. So on average $5$ contestants fall before one crosses, making position $6$ the optimal choice.

questions.title: Bull Call Spread II
questions.question: Consider the following asset $S$, with initial price $S_0 = 7$. Now, let's look at a bull call spread with $K_1 = 5$ and $K_2 = 10$. We want to price the bull call spread, but we do not have access to a computer. The only information we know is that there exists a discount factor of $0.9$ and that the initial price is $7$. Give the best upper-bound for the price of this bull call spread.
questions.hint: Similar to pricing via replication, find a function that superreplicates the payoff.
hints: The bull call spread's payoff is capped at K₂−K₁=5.
A position of 5 bonds super-replicates the payoff (always ≥ it).
Best bond-based upper bound = 5·0.9 = 4.5.
questions.genSolution: A bull call spread $(S-5)^+-(S-10)^+$ pays at most $K_2-K_1=5$ (when $S\ge10$). Holding $5$ zero-coupon bonds super-replicates this payoff, so the price is bounded by the discounted cap: $5\times0.9=4.5.$

questions.title: Lock and Key Pair
questions.question: You have $5$ unique locks in front of you with $5$ unique keys that unlock exactly one of the locks. Assuming  an optimal strategy, what is the maximum number of times you need to try the locks to identify which key unlocks each lock?
questions.hint: Try $4$ of the keys on the first lock.
hints: For each lock, try keys until matched; the last key is deducible.
Lock 1 needs at most 4 tries, lock 2 at most 3, ….
questions.genSolution: For the first lock, try keys one by one; after $4$ failures the $5$th is known, so $\le4$ tries. The next lock needs $\le3$, then $2,1,0$. Worst case $4+3+2+1+0=10$ tries.

questions.title: Car Question
questions.question:
Suppose that we have $2$ cars parked in a line occupying spaces $1$ and $2$ of a parking lot. Spaces $3$ and $4$ are initially empty. Every minute, a car is considered eligible to move forward one space if a) the space in front of them is empty prior to any potential moves and b) label of the space they are on is less than $4$. Suppose that every minute, if a car is eligible to move, it moves forward one space with probability $1/2$. What is the expected amount of minutes before the cars occupy spaces $3$ to $4$? 


 $$ 
 $$ 


$\textbf{Note:}$ The space in front must be empty before any moves. For example, on the first turn, only the car in space $2$ is eligible to move.
questions.hint: Let $E_{ij}$ represent the expected number of minutes before the cars are in spaces $3$ and $4$ when the cars are in spaces $i < j$. Clearly $E_{34} = 0$, as we already have reached our goal. We want $E_{12}$. The number of turns needed for a car to move forward to the next spot is $N \sim \text{Geom}(1/2)$. The first step is that we need the car in space $2$ to move to space $3$. This takes $2$ minutes on average, so $E_{12} = 2 + E_{13}$. Use the result of First Flip to say $\mathbb{E}[X \mid X < Y] = \mathbb{E}[X \mid X = Y] = \dfrac{4}{3}$, where $X,Y \sim \text{Geom}(1/2)$ IID.
hints: From (1,2) only the front car can move (3 empty): E_{12}=2+E_{13}.
From (1,3) both move in parallel; the back car can only advance to 3 after the front leaves it. So E_{13}=E[max(T₁,T₂)]+2.
questions.genSolution: From $(1,2)$ only the front car (at $2$) can move into $3$, taking $\mathrm{Geom}(\tfrac12)$ time (mean $2$): $E_{12}=2+E_{13}$. From $(1,3)$ both cars advance independently — the back car $1\to2$ and front $3\to4$ — but the back car can only continue $2\to3$ once the front has vacated $3$. So $E_{13}=\mathbb{E}[\max(T_1,T_2)]+2$ where $T_1,T_2\sim\mathrm{Geom}(\tfrac12)$. Since $\mathbb{E}[\max]=2\cdot2-\tfrac43=\tfrac83$, $E_{13}=\tfrac83+2=\tfrac{14}{3}$ and $E_{12}=2+\tfrac{14}{3}=\tfrac{20}{3}.$

questions.title: Shootout
questions.question: $n$ pirates all stole some gold! However, all of them are greedy, so they each are loaded with $2$ cannons. Each of the $n$ pirates has $2$ cannons that they point at $2$ distinct pirates among the other $n-1$. What is the minimum value of $n$ such that it is possible to have a situation where no two pirates are mutually pointing cannons at one another?
questions.hint: With $n = 2$, clearly the pirates can only point at one another. With $n = 3$, there are also only $2$ options for the pirates to point at. Think about the Pigeonhole Principle.
hints: Each pirate sends 2 arrows, so there are 2n directed arrows among C(n,2) unordered pairs.
No mutual pair means each pair is used at most once: need 2n ≤ C(n,2).
2n≤n(n−1)/2 ⇒ n≥5; n=5 works (a regular tournament).
questions.genSolution: There are $2n$ directed arrows and $\binom{n}{2}$ unordered pairs; avoiding mutual pointing means each pair carries at most one arrow, so $2n\le\binom n2=\tfrac{n(n-1)}2$, i.e. $n\ge5$. For $n=4$, $2n=8>6=\binom42$, forcing a mutual pair. A regular tournament on $5$ vertices (each out-degree $2$) achieves it, so the minimum is $n=5.$

questions.title: Dominated Turtle
questions.question:
Two turtles, Tort and Bort, are going to perform independent simple symmetric random walks on the integers starting at positions $0$ and $4$, respectively. Compute the probability after $10$ steps, Tort and Bort are back at their initial positions and that Tort was strictly behind Bort at all $10$ steps. The answer will be in the form 
 $$ 
\dfrac{\displaystyle \binom{a}{b}^p - \binom{c}{d}^p}{2^r}
 $$ 
 where $b \leq \dfrac{a}{2}$ and $d \leq \dfrac{c}{2}$. Find $a^2 + b^2 + c^2 + d^2 + p^2 + r^2$.
questions.hint: If $X_n$ is the position of Tort and $Y_n$ is the position of Bort (both at time $n$), imagine plotting $(X_n,Y_n)$ in the plane. Think about complementation and some type of reflection principle.
hints: Each turtle returns to start: C(10,5) paths each; the constraint is Tort always strictly below Bort (gap starts at 4).
By the reflection principle, the touching pairs correspond to Tort 0→4 and Bort 4→0: C(10,3)² of them.
questions.genSolution: Each turtle takes $10$ steps back to its start, so each has $\binom{10}{5}$ equally likely paths; the joint count is over $2^{20}$. Requiring Tort strictly below Bort throughout, the reflection principle maps the 'touching' pairs to Tort going $0\to4$ and Bort $4\to0$, numbering $\binom{10}{3}^2$. So the probability is $\dfrac{\binom{10}{5}^2-\binom{10}{3}^2}{2^{20}}$, giving $a=10,b=5,c=10,d=3,p=2,r=20$ and $a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2+p^2+r^2=100+25+100+9+4+400=638.$

questions.title: Doubly 5 I
questions.question: Kathy has a fair $6-$sided die with numbers $1-6$ on the sides. She keeps rolling keeping track of the outcomes until she gets both a  $4$ and $6$. Find the probability Kathy observed exactly $2$ $5$s while rolling her die.
questions.hint: Break up the event based on when Kathy stops rolling. What needs to occur for her to stop rolling after exactly $n$ rolls? Think about the negative binomial distribution.
hints: Ignore 1,2,3 (they neither stop the game nor are counted); condition on rolls in {4,5,6}, each 1/3.
Build the generating function of #5s: f(z)=2/((2−z)(3−z)).
questions.genSolution: Rolls of $1,2,3$ neither stop the game nor are counted, so condition on rolls in $\{4,5,6\}$ (each $\tfrac13$). Tracking the number of $5$s with a generating function: from a state needing one target, $f_{h}(z)=\tfrac1{2-z}$, and from the start $f(z)=\tfrac{2}{(2-z)(3-z)}=\tfrac{2}{2-z}-\tfrac{2}{3-z}$. The coefficient of $z^2$ is $2\cdot\tfrac1{2^3}-2\cdot\tfrac1{3^3}=\tfrac14-\tfrac2{27}=\tfrac{19}{108}.$

questions.title: Unit Fraction Representation
questions.question:
Assume, without proof, that every rational number $0 < q < 1$ can be represented as the sum of unit fractions. For example, 
 $$ 
\dfrac{4}{5} = \dfrac{1}{2} + \dfrac{1}{4} + \dfrac{1}{20}
 $$ 
 Find the largest denominator in the unit fraction representation of $\dfrac{179}{720}$ that has the fewest terms and largest possible maximum denominator. In the example above, the answer would be $10$.
questions.hint: We can see that $\dfrac{1}{5} < \dfrac{179}{720} < \dfrac{1}{4}$, so $\dfrac{1}{5}$ is our first fraction in our sum.
hints: Greedy (Fibonacci–Sylvester): subtract the largest unit fraction ≤ the remainder.
questions.genSolution: Apply the greedy (Fibonacci–Sylvester) algorithm. Since $\tfrac15<\tfrac{179}{720}<\tfrac14$, take $\tfrac15$: remainder $\tfrac{179}{720}-\tfrac{144}{720}=\tfrac{35}{720}=\tfrac{7}{144}$. Next $\tfrac1{21}<\tfrac7{144}$, leaving $\tfrac{49}{1008}-\tfrac{48}{1008}=\tfrac1{1008}$. So $\tfrac{179}{720}=\tfrac15+\tfrac1{21}+\tfrac1{1008}$, and the largest denominator is $1008.$

questions.title: Soccer Jerseys
questions.question: Liverpool FC sells player jerseys at random prices. The price of a Mohamed Salah jersey is normally distributed with mean $\$80$ and standard deviation $\$15$. The price of a Sadio Mane jersey is normally distributed with mean $\$70$ and standard deviation $\$20$, independent of Salah jerseys. Gabe wants to be $97.5\%$ sure that he can purchase both jerseys. How much money (in dollars) should Gabe bring with him? For the purposes of this question, take $\Phi^{-1}(0.95) = 1.65$ and $\Phi^{-1}(0.975) = 1.96$
questions.hint: We want to find a value $c$ so that $\mathbb{P}[S + M \leq c] = 0.975$. $M \sim N(70,20^2)$ and $S \sim N(80,15^2)$ represent the prices of Mane and Salah jerseys, respectively. What is the distribution of the sum of independent normal random variables?
hints: Sum of independent normals: S+M ~ N(150, 15²+20²).
Variance 625 ⇒ sd 25. Want the 97.5th percentile.

questions.title: Binary Zeroes
questions.question: In the binary expansion of $142!$, how many trailing zeroes are there?
questions.hint: The number of trailing zeroes is the highest power of $2$ dividing $142!$.
hints: Trailing binary zeros = exponent of 2 in 142! (Legendre's formula).
questions.genSolution: Trailing zeros in binary equal the power of $2$ dividing $142!$. By Legendre's formula, $\sum_{k\ge1}\lfloor142/2^k\rfloor=71+35+17+8+4+2+1=138.$

questions.title: Ranged Max
questions.question: Let $X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4 \sim \text{Unif}(0,4)$ IID. Find the probability that the maximum of these $4$ random variables is in the interval $(2,3]$.
questions.hint: Let $M = \text{max}\{X_1,X_2,X_3,X_4\}$. Can you derive the CDF of $M$ easily?
hints: For U(0,4), P(max≤m)=(m/4)⁴.

questions.title: Random Determinant
questions.question: Let $A$ be a random $2 \times 2$ matrix formed by having each element $ij$ set by $A_{ij} \sim \text{Bernoulli}(0.5)$. Find $\mathbb{E}[\text{det}(A-A^T)]$.
questions.hint: A first observation is that $A$ and $A^T$ have the same elements on the diagonal, so the diagonal elements are $0$.
hints: A−Aᵀ is antisymmetric: [[0, a−b],[b−a, 0]] with a=A₁₂, b=A₂₁.
det = (a−b)², which is 1 iff a≠b.
questions.genSolution: $A-A^T=\begin{psmallmatrix}0&a-b\\b-a&0\end{psmallmatrix}$ with $a=A_{12},b=A_{21}$, so $\det(A-A^T)=-(a-b)(b-a)=(a-b)^2$. This is $1$ when $a\ne b$ and $0$ otherwise, and $P(a\ne b)=\tfrac12$, so $\mathbb{E}[\det(A-A^T)]=\tfrac12.$

questions.title: Covariance Review VI
questions.question:
Suppose that $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ and $Y \sim \chi^2(
u)$ are independent. Define $W = Z / \sqrt{Y}$. Compute $\text{Cov}(Y, W)$.
questions.hint: Use the independence of $Y$ and $Z$ and substitute in the definition of $W$ after using the definition of covariance.
hints: Cov(Y,W)=E[YW]−E[Y]E[W], with W=Z/√Y and Z⟂Y.
E[W]=E[Z]E[1/√Y]=0 and E[YW]=E[Z√Y]=E[Z]E[√Y]=0.
So Cov(Y,W)=0.
questions.genSolution: Since $Z\perp Y$ and $\mathbb{E}[Z]=0$: $\mathbb{E}[W]=\mathbb{E}[Z]\,\mathbb{E}[1/\sqrt Y]=0$ and $\mathbb{E}[YW]=\mathbb{E}[Z\sqrt Y]=\mathbb{E}[Z]\,\mathbb{E}[\sqrt Y]=0$. Hence $\mathrm{Cov}(Y,W)=0-0=0.$

questions.title: Binomial Contract Pricing I
questions.question: We want to price a derivatives contract on the underlying $S$, which has payoff $S_T^2$ at expiry, where $T = 2$. At each step, the underlying will either double or half. Assuming the initial price of the underlying is $S_0 = 5$, what is the time-$0$ price of the contract?
questions.hint: What are the potential final payoffs? Back-propagate.
hints: Find the risk-neutral probability with u=2, d=1/2 (r=0): p=(1−d)/(u−d).
p=1/3; final S_T∈{20,5,5,1.25} with payoff S_T².
questions.genSolution: With $u=2,d=\tfrac12$ and $r=0$, the risk-neutral probability is $p=\dfrac{1-d}{u-d}=\dfrac{0.5}{1.5}=\tfrac13$. After two steps $S_T\in\{20,5,5,1.25\}$ with probabilities $\tfrac19,\tfrac29,\tfrac29,\tfrac49$. The price is $\mathbb{E}_Q[S_T^2]=\dfrac{400+25\cdot2+25\cdot2+1.5625\cdot4}{9}=\dfrac{506.25}{9}=56.25.$

questions.title: Same Heads
questions.question: There is a game where $2$ people flip a fair coin $4$ times each and record their flips. What is the probability that the two people flipped the same number of tails?
questions.hint: Find the probability of just one person getting each number of tails possible.
hints: P(both get k tails)=[C(4,k)/16]²; sum over k.
Σ C(4,k)² = C(8,4) (Vandermonde).
questions.genSolution: $P(\text{same tails})=\sum_{k=0}^4\Big(\tfrac{\binom4k}{16}\Big)^2=\dfrac{\sum_k\binom4k^2}{256}=\dfrac{\binom84}{256}=\dfrac{70}{256}=\dfrac{35}{128}.$

questions.title: Dollar Cent Switch
questions.question: A man went to his local bank to cash a check. By mistake, the cashier gave him the amount in dollars as cents and in cents as dollars. Without checking the money, the man left, spent a nickel on candy, and then realized he had exactly twice the value of the original check. He had no money in his pocket before visiting the bank. How much was the check worth?
questions.hint: Let $y$ represent the number of cents and $x$ the number of dollars on the check. Then, the value of the check is $x + 0.01y$. The amount the man was mistakenly paid is $y + 0.01x$. You can take it from here.
hints: Check = x dollars + y cents = x+0.01y; paid y+0.01x; after −0.05 it's twice the check.
questions.genSolution: Let the check be $x$ dollars and $y$ cents, value $x+0.01y$; he was paid $y+0.01x$. After spending a nickel: $y+0.01x-0.05=2(x+0.01y)$, i.e. $98y-199x=5$. Since $199\equiv3\pmod{98}$, $3x\equiv-5\pmod{98}\Rightarrow x\equiv31$. Then $x=31,\,y=63$ (valid), so the check was $\$31.63$ (check: paid $\$63.31$, minus $0.05$ is $63.26=2\times31.63$).

questions.title: Square Normal
questions.question: Let $X,Y \sim N(0,1)$ IID. Compute $\mathbb{E}[X \mid X^2 + Y^2]$.
questions.hint: $ -X \sim N(0,1)$ as well.
hints: X²+Y² is unchanged if X→−X (and −X is also N(0,1), independent of Y).
So conditional on the radius, X and −X are equally likely.
E[X | X²+Y²]=0.
questions.genSolution: The radius $X^2+Y^2$ is invariant under $X\mapsto-X$, and $-X\sim N(0,1)$ has the same joint law with $Y$. Thus, given $X^2+Y^2$, $X$ and $-X$ are equally likely, so $\mathbb{E}[X\mid X^2+Y^2]=0.$

questions.title: Compound Game
questions.question: You are given a fair $6-$sided die and play the following game: You receive the value of the face-up side on each roll. If you roll an odd number, the game ends. If you roll an even number, you flip a fair coin. If the coin lands on tails, the game ends. If it lands on heads, you roll the die again and repeat the coin flip process.  How much do you expect to make from this game?
questions.hint: Condition on the parity of the first roll.
hints: You collect each roll's value; you roll again only if the roll was even AND the coin is heads.
P(continue)=½·½=¼, and each roll contributes mean 3.5.
questions.genSolution: Each roll you receive its value (mean $3.5$), and you roll again only when the value is even and the coin shows heads — probability $\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac12=\tfrac14$. So $E=3.5+\tfrac14 E\Rightarrow \tfrac34 E=3.5\Rightarrow E=\tfrac{14}{3}.$

questions.title: Covered Calls I
questions.question: You are entering a covered call position on $\$\text{TSLA}$. You buy 100 shares at $\$230$ and sell the Dec call $@10.50$. What is your breakeven for this strategy?
questions.hint: How do we lose money in a covered call and what can offset it?
hints: A covered call = long stock + short call; the premium lowers your effective cost.
Breakeven = stock price − premium received.
questions.genSolution: In a covered call you own the stock and collect the call premium, so your breakeven is the purchase price minus the premium: $230-10.50=219.50.$

questions.title: Sum Exceedance II
questions.question: Let $X_1,X_2,\dots \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)$ IID and $N_2 = \text{min}\{n : X_1 + \dots + X_n > 2\}$. Find $\mathbb{E}[N_2]$. Your answer will be in the form $ae^2 + be$ for integers $a$ and $b$. $e$ here is Euler's constant. Find $a + b$.
questions.hint: Write a delayed differential equation that $f(x) = \mathbb{E}[N_x]$ should satisfy on $(1,2)$. You can obtain this by using Law of Total Expectation on $X_1$ and differentiating the result. What is a reasonable initial condition for $f(1)$? You may also want to recall $f(x) = e^x$ on $(0,1)$.
hints: E[N_x] (uniforms to exceed x) equals e^x on (0,1); extend via the delayed equation / Smirnov formula.
questions.genSolution: Let $f(x)=\mathbb{E}[N_x]$, the expected number of uniforms whose sum first exceeds $x$. It satisfies $f(x)=e^x$ on $(0,1)$ and in general $f(x)=\sum_{k=0}^{\lfloor x\rfloor}(-1)^k\dfrac{(x-k)^k e^{x-k}}{k!}$. At $x=2$ the $k=2$ term vanishes, leaving $f(2)=e^2-e$. So $a=1,b=-1$ and $a+b=0.$

questions.title: Close Dice I
questions.question: On average, how many times does a fair $6-$sided die need to be rolled to obtain two consecutive rolls that differ by exactly $1$?
questions.hint:
Let $E_i$ be the expected time for this to happen given that our current roll is $i$ and $E$ be the expected value of the event we are interested in. Then 
 $$ 
E = 1 + \dfrac{E_1 + E_2 + E_3 + E_4 + E_5 + E_6}{6}
 $$ 
 by Law of Total Expectation. By the symmetry of the fair die, $E_1 = E_6$, $E_2 = E_5$ and $E_3 = E_4$. Note that $E_2 \neq E_3$ because intuitively $E_1$ and $E_6$ should be larger since there is only one possible value we can roll in the next roll that will give a difference of $1$. Set up the rest of the Markov Chain.
hints: E_i = expected rolls to get consecutive faces differing by exactly 1; symmetry E_1=E_6, E_2=E_5, E_3=E_4.
Success counts: 1 for faces 1,6; 2 for faces 2–5. Set up E_i=1+(1/6)·Σ_{non-success}E_j.
questions.genSolution:
Let $E_i$ be the expected additional rolls to get adjacent faces differing by exactly $1$. By symmetry $a=E_1=E_6$, $b=E_2=E_5$, $c=E_3=E_4$. A face $1$ or $6$ has one 'success' neighbour, faces $2$–$5$ have two:
$$a=1+\tfrac16(2a+b+2c),\ b=1+\tfrac16(a+2b+c),\ c=1+\tfrac16(2a+b+c).$$
Solving: $a=\tfrac{70}{17},b=\tfrac{58}{17},c=\tfrac{60}{17}$. Then $E=1+\tfrac{a+b+c}{3}=1+\tfrac{188}{51}=\tfrac{239}{51}.$

questions.title: Basic Die Game VII
questions.question: A player rolls a standard fair $6-$sided die. If the player rolls a $6$, the game ends and the player receives no payout. Otherwise, the player may quit and receive $\$k$, where $k$ is the upface of the previous roll. Otherwise, the player may roll again under the same rules. Assuming optimal play from the player, what is their expected payout?
questions.hint: Given that the player does not roll a $6$, what is the conditional expectation of their die roll? When should the person roll again?
hints: Let V be the game value; you re-roll when V exceeds your current face.
V=3 is consistent (keep 3,4,5; reroll 1,2).
questions.genSolution: Let $V$ be the value of choosing to roll. After a roll, a $6$ (prob $\tfrac16$) busts; otherwise you keep $\max(k,V)$. So $V=\tfrac16\cdot0+\sum_{k=1}^5\tfrac16\max(k,V)$. Testing $V=3$: $\max(k,3)$ over $k=1..5$ is $3,3,3,4,5$ summing to $18$, giving $V=\tfrac{18}{6}=3$ — consistent. So the expected payout is $\$3.$

questions.title: Car and Fly I
questions.question: Two cars, $c_1$ and $c_2$ are on opposite sides of a $400$ mile road. Both cars, $c_1$ and $c_2$, drive towards each other at a constant rate of $120$ miles per hour and $60$ miles per hour respectively.  $$$$ A fly starts on car $c_2$ and flies towards Car  $c_1$ at a rate of $180$ miles per hour. Then, once it hits Car  $c_1$, it immediately starts to fly back to Car $c_2$ at the same rate.  $$$$ How long after the cars start driving will the fly return to Car $c_2$, to the nearest minute?
questions.hint: Imagine the road as a number line with the cars and fly are moving on. Caculate how long it takes the fly to reach car $c_1$ then back to car $c_2$
hints: Leg 1: fly and c₁ close at 180+120=300 over 400 mi ⇒ 4/3 hr.
At that moment c₂ has moved up; fly and c₂ are 160 mi apart, closing at 180+60=240 ⇒ 2/3 hr.
Total 4/3+2/3=2 hr=120 min.
questions.genSolution: Going out, the fly and $c_1$ close at $180+120=300$ mph over $400$ mi: $\tfrac{400}{300}=\tfrac43$ hr. By then the fly is at mile $240$ and $c_2$ at mile $80$, a gap of $160$, closing at $180+60=240$ mph: $\tfrac{160}{240}=\tfrac23$ hr. Total $\tfrac43+\tfrac23=2$ hr $=120$ minutes.

questions.title: Option Dice II
questions.question: Pretend you have a simple options chain on the expected value of the product of two dice rolls. What would the put option at $4$ be priced at in this market?
questions.hint: When does our contract make money and how much?
hints: Underlying X = product of two dice; put pays max(4−X, 0).
Only X=1,2,3 give positive payoff: 3, 2, 2, 1, 1 across outcomes.
Σ=9 over 36 ⇒ price 1/4.
questions.genSolution: The put on $X=$ product pays $\max(4-X,0)$, nonzero only for $X<4$: $X=1$ once ($+3$), $X=2$ twice ($+2$), $X=3$ twice ($+1$). Summing, $\sum(4-X)^+=3+4+2=9$ over $36$ outcomes, so the price is $\tfrac{9}{36}=\tfrac14.$

questions.title: Put Fly I
questions.question:
We have the underlying $S$ with initial price $S_0 = 15$. We have access to the following calls. The calls are given in the format of $(\text{Strike } K, \text{Price } C_0)$


 $$ 
\begin{align*}
(10, 8.1) \\ 
(15, 4.2) \\ 
(20, 1.4) \\ 
\end{align*}
 $$ 
 $$ 
 $$ 
What is the time-$0$ price of a $10/15/20$ put-fly?
questions.hint: Use put-call parity.
hints: A butterfly has the same price whether built from calls or puts (put–call parity makes the linear terms cancel).
Compute the call butterfly: C(10) − 2C(15) + C(20).
questions.genSolution: A $10/15/20$ butterfly costs the same with puts or calls (the stock and bond terms from put–call parity cancel in the $1{:}{-}2{:}1$ combination). Using calls: $C_{10}-2C_{15}+C_{20}=8.1-8.4+1.4=1.1.$

questions.title: Optimal Marbles I
questions.question: Two players, say $A$ and $B$, play the following game: Both players have $100$ marbles and may put anywhere between $1$ and $100$ marbles in the box each. This decision is not revealed to the other player. Then, they draw $2$ marbles with replacement between trials. If the marble belongs to $A$, then assuming that $A$ put $a$ marbles in the box, $A$ is paid $100-a$ monetary units from a third party. Similarly if the marble belongs to $B$, then assuming $B$ but $b$ marbles in the box, $B$ is paid $100-b$ monetary units from a third party. Assume both players play optimally. Find the expected total payout of player $A$.
questions.hint: If the strategy is optimal, then if this game were to be repeated many times, they would not change their strategy. Therefore, the optimal strategy for the game where there are $2$ consecutive marble draws is the same as the optimal strategy for the game with one draw. Then, we just multiply the expected profit by $2$ to represent the two draws. Furthermore, as this game is symmetric for the two players, their optimal strategy will be the same. Set up a function $A(a,b)$ the represents the expected payout if $A$ puts in $a$ marbles and $b$ puts in $b$ marbles. Find the equilibrium.
hints: Per draw, A earns (100−a)·a/(a+b). Find the symmetric Nash equilibrium a=b.
questions.genSolution: Per draw A's expected payout is $A(a,b)=\dfrac{a}{a+b}(100-a)$. Setting $\partial_a A=0$ gives $a^2+2ab-100b=0$; at the symmetric equilibrium $a=b$ this is $3a^2=100a\Rightarrow a=\tfrac{100}{3}$. Each draw then pays $\tfrac12(100-\tfrac{100}3)=\tfrac{100}{3}$, and with two draws A's total is $2\cdot\tfrac{100}{3}=\tfrac{200}{3}\approx67.$

questions.title: Expected Increase
questions.question: Let $X_1,X_2,\dots$ be a sequence of IID random variables with some continuous PDF $f(x)$. Let $N$ be the time at which the sequence stops decreasing i.e. the first value $n$ such that $X_1 \geq X_2 \geq \dots \geq X_{n-1}$ but $X_{n-1} < X_{n}$. Find $\ln(\mathbb{E}[N])$.
questions.hint: Recall for non-negative integer-valued random variables $X$ that $\mathbb{E}[X] = \displaystyle \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \mathbb{P}[X \geq k]$.
hints: N≥k means the first k−1 values are non-increasing.
P(X_1≥…≥X_{k-1})=1/(k−1)! (all orderings equally likely).
questions.genSolution: $N\ge k$ exactly when $X_1\ge X_2\ge\dots\ge X_{k-1}$, which for continuous IID values has probability $\tfrac1{(k-1)!}$. Then $\mathbb{E}[N]=\sum_{k\ge1}P(N\ge k)=\sum_{k\ge1}\tfrac1{(k-1)!}=e$, so $\ln\mathbb{E}[N]=1.$

questions.title: Comparing Flips II
questions.question: You and your friend are playing a game with a fair coin, tossing it and writing down the outcomes. You win if HTH appears before HHT, else your friend wins. What is the probability that your friend wins?
questions.hint: Both sequences contain the subsequence HT, so imagine writing down an infinite number of toss outcomes and scanning for HT subsequences. When will HTH occur before HHT?
hints: Once you see HH, the next T completes HHT (friend) before any HTH is possible.
Track states; let q=P(friend wins) from state 'just saw H'.
questions.genSolution: Friend wins if HHT appears before HTH. From the state 'last flip H': a further H reaches HH, from which the next T gives HHT before any HTH can form (friend wins for sure); a T reaches HT, from which H gives HTH (you win) and T returns to needing an H. Letting $q$ be the friend's win probability from 'saw H': $q=\tfrac12\cdot1+\tfrac12\big(\tfrac12\cdot0+\tfrac12 q\big)$, so $\tfrac34 q=\tfrac12\Rightarrow q=\tfrac23.$

questions.title: Slippery Snail
questions.question: A snail is $30$ feet away from some food. The snail moves at a rate of $4$ feet per hour. However, at the end of each hour, a large gust of wind blows and the snail moves back $2$ feet. The snail eats the food instantaneously once it reaches it. How many hours does it take for the snail to reach the food?
questions.hint: For the first $13$ hours, the snail moves up $4$ feet and then gets blown back $2$ feet, moving at a net rate of $2$ feet per hour.
hints: Net progress is +2 ft/hour, but the snail eats the instant it reaches 30 (before the wind).
After 13 hours it's at 26 ft; in hour 14 it climbs +4 to reach 30.
14 hours.
questions.genSolution: Each hour the snail gains $4$ then loses $2$ (net $+2$), but reaches the food the instant it touches $30$ — before the gust. After $13$ hours it sits at $2\cdot13=26$ ft; in the $14$th hour it climbs $+4$ to $30$ and eats. So it takes $14$ hours.

questions.title: Ship Meeting
questions.question: A pirate ship sets sail from island $A$ to island $B$. At the same time, another pirate ship starts sailing from island $B$ to island $A$. Assume each ship stays along the same path between the two islands. The pirate ships pass other at $1:00$ PM and continue on their paths. One of them arrives at $5:00$ PM, the other at $10:00$ PM. At what time did the pirate ships set sail? Put your answer in military time, excluding the $0$ if the hour is only one digit. For example, if the answer is $4:30$ PM, answer $1630$.
questions.hint: Let's call the two ships $1$ and $2$ in the order presented in the question. WLOG, let ship $1$ arrive at $5:00$ PM and ship $2$ arrive at $10:00$ PM. Clearly ship $2$ is sailing at a slower speed than ship $1$, as it took longer from the meeting point and they left at the same time. Let $v_1$ and $v_2$ be the velocities of the two ships and $d$ be the total distance between the islands. From the meeting point, what is the distance between the two islands in terms of $v_1$ and $v_2$?
hints: Let t be the time before the 1PM meeting; after meeting the ships take 4h and 9h to finish.
A standard relation gives t=√(t₁·t₂)=√(4·9).
t=6h before 1PM ⇒ 7:00 AM ⇒ 700.
questions.genSolution: If $t$ is the travel time to the meeting, the post-meeting times satisfy $t=\sqrt{t_1 t_2}=\sqrt{4\cdot9}=6$ hours (each ship covers the other's pre-meeting distance). So they left $6$ hours before $1{:}00$ PM, i.e. $7{:}00$ AM $=700.$

questions.title: Circular Hop
questions.question: You are in a circle with $100$ points labelled $0-99$ clockwise. You start at $1$. At each turn, you move one unit left or right with equal probability. What is the expected time to hit $0$?
questions.hint: Use the fact that for a (non-circular) symmetric random walk starting at $0$ with boundaries $a$ and $-b$, for $a,b > 0$ being integers, the expected time to hit a boundary is $ab$.
hints: On the circle, point 0 is reached by hitting either 0 or 100 on the unrolled line (100≡0).
Starting at 1 with absorbing boundaries 0 and 100, expected time = position·(100−position).
questions.genSolution: Unroll the circle to a line with absorbing points $0$ and $100$ (which are the same point). Starting at $1$, the expected time for a symmetric walk to hit $\{0,100\}$ is $1\cdot(100-1)=99.$

questions.title: Lead Count
questions.question: Suppose you flip a fair coin $10$ times. We say a certain outcome (heads or tails) is leading after $n$ flips if there are strictly larger than $\dfrac{n}{2}$ flips of that outcome within the first $n$ flips. Find the probability that the outcome of the first flip of the coin is leading after $10$ flips.
questions.hint: Fix the first flip arbitrarily since it is equally likely to be heads or tails. Without loss of generality, say it is heads. Then we would need at least $5$ heads in the next $9$ flips to be leading.
hints: Fix the first flip as heads; it leads iff there are >5 heads total, i.e. ≥5 heads in the remaining 9.
For Binom(9,½), P(X≥5)=P(X≤4)=1/2 by symmetry.
So the probability is 1/2.
questions.genSolution: Say the first flip is heads. It is 'leading' after $10$ flips iff heads strictly exceed $5$, i.e. at least $5$ heads occur in the remaining $9$ flips. For $\mathrm{Binom}(9,\tfrac12)$, $P(X\ge5)=P(X\le4)=\tfrac12$ by symmetry, so the answer is $\tfrac12.$

questions.title: Plane Partition
questions.question: Determine the maximum number of disjoint regions into which the plane can be divided by $10$ non-parallel lines. For instance, a single line divides the plane into $2$ regions.
questions.hint: Let $L_n$ be the maximum number of regions with $n$ lines. Note that $L_1 = 2$ by the above and $L_2 = 4$, as we want to draw two non-parallel lines. The key observation is here that the $(n+1)$st line that is added should intersect each of the first $n$ lines, and this yields $n+1$ distinct regions.
hints: The n-th line crosses the previous n−1, adding n new regions.
questions.genSolution: Each new line meets all previous ones, adding as many regions as it has pieces. This gives $L_n=1+n+\binom n2=1+\tfrac{n(n+1)}2$. For $n=10$: $1+55=56.$

questions.title: Number 50
questions.question: How many 10 digit numbers are there whose digits are prime and whose product of its digits is exactly 100000?
questions.hint: The product of the digits must be a constant $c \cdot 10^5 = c \cdot 2^5 \cdot 5^5$.
hints: Digits are prime {2,3,5,7}; product 100000=2⁵·5⁵ has no factor 3 or 7.
So digits must be only 2s and 5s, with exactly five 2s and five 5s.
Arrangements: 10!/(5!5!)=252.
questions.genSolution: The product $100000=2^5\cdot5^5$ has no factor of $3$ or $7$, so the prime digits used can only be $2$ and $5$. To get $2^5\cdot5^5$ we need exactly five $2$s and five $5$s among the ten digits. The number of arrangements is $\dfrac{10!}{5!\,5!}=252.$

questions.title: Straddle Gamma
questions.question: Let's consider a straddle on underlying $S$ with strike $K$ and expiry $T$. You have a call option on $S$, also with strike $K$ and expiry $T$. This call option has $\Gamma = 0.03$. What is the $\Gamma$ of the straddle? Assume Black-Scholes dynamics.
