questions.question: You're on a game show! The host tells you that $\$200$ is located behind $2$ of $7$ doors. The other $5$ doors have nothing behind them. You have no idea which doors the money is behind. You keep selecting randomly until you find the first door with money behind it. You do not select any doors that you have selected prior. If each door opening costs $\$x$ and the game is fair, what is $x$?
questions.hint: Use a "First Ace" approach, where the aces here are the doors with the money.
hints: Expected doors opened to reach the first of 2 'money' doors among 7 is (7+1)/(2+1) (First-Ace).
= 8/3 openings, each costing x; fair means cost = $200.
questions.genSolution: By the 'First Ace' argument, the $2$ money doors split the $5$ empties into $3$ gaps, so the expected number of doors opened to hit the first money door is $\tfrac{7+1}{2+1}=\tfrac83$. Fairness requires $x\cdot\tfrac83=200$, so $x=75.$

questions.title: Cube Slice
questions.question: A lumberjack is cutting a $3 \times 3 \times 3$ cubic piece of wood into $27$ $1 \times 1 \times 1$ cubes of wood. The lumberjack can reposition the cube and individual pieces after each cut. Find the minimum number of cuts the lumberjack needs to make to form the smaller cubes.
questions.hint: Consider the center cube. To form the $6$ sides of it, at least $6$ cuts need to be made.
hints: Focus on the center sub-cube of the 3×3×3.
Its 6 faces are all interior, each requiring a separate cut (repositioning can't create two opposite faces in one cut).
So at least 6 cuts; 6 suffice.
questions.genSolution: Consider the central $1\times1\times1$ cube. Each of its $6$ faces is an internal surface that must be produced by a distinct cut (no single planar cut can form two opposite faces of it, even with rearranging). Hence at least $6$ cuts are needed, and $6$ (two per axis) suffice.

questions.title: Diverse Distributions
questions.question: Let $X \sim \text{Unif}(0,\lambda)$ and $Y \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$, where $\lambda > 0$ is a constant. For what value of $\lambda$ do $X$ and $Y$ have the same mean? Find $\lambda^2$
questions.hint: The mean of a Unif$(a,b)$ random variable is $\dfrac{a+b}{2}$ and the mean of an Exp$(a)$ random variable is $\dfrac{1}{a}$.
hints: Mean of U(0,λ) is λ/2; mean of Exp(λ) is 1/λ.
Set λ/2 = 1/λ.
λ²=2.
questions.genSolution: Equating means, $\tfrac{\lambda}{2}=\tfrac1\lambda$, so $\lambda^2=2.$

questions.title: OLS Review I
questions.question: Consider the following ordered pairs $(x, y)$: $(-2, 3), (-1, 2), (0, 1), (1, 1), (2, 0.5)$. Compute the sum of the least-squares estimators for the parameters $\beta_0, \beta_1$ in the model $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon$, where $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$.
questions.hint: Recall $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}$, and $\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}$, where $S_{xy} = \sum_{i = 1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})$ and $S_{xx} = \sum_{i = 1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})^2$.
hints: x̄=0, ȳ=1.5. Compute S_xy and S_xx.
questions.genSolution: Here $\bar x=0,\bar y=1.5$. Then $S_{xy}=\sum x_i(y_i-\bar y)=-6$ and $S_{xx}=\sum x_i^2=10$, so $\hat\beta_1=-0.6$ and $\hat\beta_0=\bar y-\hat\beta_1\bar x=1.5$. Their sum is $1.5-0.6=0.9.$

questions.title: Swift Betting
questions.question: Juliana is playing "Fifteen" by Taylor Swift. The length of the song is $234$ seconds. Juliana offers you the following bet: You may guess a value $0 < x < 1$. Juliana will then stop the song at a uniformly random point along its duration. If the point at which she stops the song is at most $234x$ seconds into the song, you must pay Juliana $2x$ monetary units. Otherwise, she pays you $x$ monetary units. Find the value of $x$ that maximizes your expected profit.
questions.hint: Fix $0 < x < 1$. What is the probability Juliana stops before $234x$ seconds in the song. What is the expected profit in each case?
hints: P(stop before 234x)=x; profit is −2x then, +x otherwise.
Expected profit = x(−2x)+(1−x)x = x−3x².
Maximize: 1−6x=0 ⇒ x=1/6.
questions.siteSolution: We need to calculate our expected profit as a function of $x$. If we guess $x$, then the probability Juliana stops before $234x$ is $x$. In this case, we pay Juliana $-2x$. Therefore, with probability $1-x$, we are paid $x$. Therefore, the expected profit when guessing $x$ is $x(-2x) + (1-x)x = x - 3x^2$. Taking the derivative, we have that the maximizer is when $1 - 6x = 0$, so $x = \dfrac{1}{6}$.

questions.title: Modified RNG
questions.question: Jimmy picks a number uniformly at random from $(0,1)$. If Jimmy chooses $x$, then Jon picks a number from $(x,1)$ uniformly at random. If $Y$ represents the number Jon selects, in simplest form, find $\dfrac{\mathbb{E}[Y]}{\text{Var}(Y)}$.
questions.hint: Let $X$ be Jimmy's number. Then $X \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)$ and $Y \mid X = x \sim \text{Unif}(x,1)$. Use Law of Total Expectation and Variance to obtain $\mathbb{E}[Y]$ and Var$(Y)$.
questions.genSolution: With $E[Y\mid X]=\tfrac{X+1}2$ and $\mathrm{Var}(Y\mid X)=\tfrac{(1-X)^2}{12}$: $E[Y]=E[\tfrac{X+1}2]=\tfrac34$, and $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=E\big[\tfrac{(1-X)^2}{12}\big]+\mathrm{Var}\big(\tfrac{X+1}2\big)=\tfrac1{36}+\tfrac1{48}=\tfrac{7}{144}$. Hence $\dfrac{E[Y]}{\mathrm{Var}(Y)}=\dfrac{3/4}{7/144}=\dfrac{108}{7}.$

questions.title: Squid Game II
questions.question: $10$ contestants are arranged into a line on a bridge and in front of them lay ten left tiles and ten right tiles side by side. In order to cross the bridge, the contestants must cross $10$ tiles, and at each step, the person in front must pick either the left or right tile to step on. However, for each left & right tile pair, there is exactly one sturdy tile and one faulty tile, but the contestants cannot tell them apart. The contestants cross the bridge in their assigned order with the first person picking either the left or right tile, and continuing to lead unless either a faulty tile is picked (resulting in elimination) or person one reaches the other side. If the first person is eliminated before reaching the other side, the person second in line assumes the lead picking until he/she is eliminated (or reaches the other side), and so on. The winner of the game is the $\textit{first}$ person to reach the other side. Let $p_i$ be the probability that the $i$th contestant in the line wins. Find ${ \underset i \max} \hspace{3pt} p_i$.
questions.hint: The probability of a person $i$ winning is equal to the probability of everyone before person $i$ failing at some point, and person $i$ getting the remaining ones correct.
hints: Each of the 10 tiles produces an elimination (first attempt wrong) independently w.p. 1/2.
Contestant i wins iff exactly i−1 eliminations occur: p_i = C(10,i−1)/2^10.
questions.genSolution: Each tile causes exactly one elimination if its first guess is wrong (probability $\tfrac12$), independently across the $10$ tiles. Contestant $i$ wins iff there are exactly $i-1$ eliminations, so $p_i=\dfrac{\binom{10}{i-1}}{2^{10}}$. This is largest at $i-1=5$: $\dfrac{\binom{10}{5}}{1024}=\dfrac{252}{1024}=\dfrac{63}{256}.$

questions.title: Show The Face
questions.question: Roll a fair standard $6-$sided die until a $6$ appears. Given that the first $6$ occurs before the first $5$, find the expected number of times the die was rolled.
questions.hint:
An incorrect solution goes as follows: Since we want the first $6$ to appear before the first $5$, we can eliminate $5$ from our sample space and look at the reduced sample space conditional on no roll being a $5$. There are $5$ remaining values in this sample space that are equally probable, so the number of rolls needed to see a $6$ conditional on this information is $\text{Geom}(1/5)$, which has mean $5$. Therefore, the answer is $5$.


 $$ 
 $$ 


Consider finding the conditional distribution of $N$ given this information.
hints: Don't just remove 5 from the sample space — conditioning changes the distribution of N.
questions.genSolution: Let $N$ be the roll of the first $6$. For both $N=n$ and '6 before 5', the first $n-1$ rolls must be in $\{1,2,3,4\}$: $P(N=n,\,6\text{ first})=(\tfrac46)^{n-1}\tfrac16$. Summing gives $P(6\text{ before }5)=\tfrac12$. Then $\mathbb{E}[N\mid 6\text{ before }5]=\dfrac{\sum_n n(\tfrac23)^{n-1}\tfrac16}{1/2}=\tfrac13\cdot\dfrac1{(1-2/3)^2}=3.$

questions.title: Ant in a Hurry
questions.question: An ant sits at one corner of a unit cube, wishing to travel to the corner farthest from it. Let $d$ be the minimal distance the ant must travel to achieve its goal. Find $d^2$. Note that the ant must always be in contact with a surface and cannot fly.
questions.hint: Open up and flatten the cube to a two-dimensional surface to better visualize the optimal path.
hints: Unfold the cube into a flat net so the path becomes a straight line.
The opposite corner unfolds to a point 1 across and 2 along.
questions.genSolution: Unfolding two adjacent faces into a plane, the far corner lies at horizontal distance $2$ and vertical distance $1$ from the start, so the shortest surface path is the straight segment of length $\sqrt{1^2+2^2}=\sqrt5$. Thus $d^2=5.$

questions.title: Complex Circle
questions.question: Suppose that $Z$ is a uniformly random point selected from the boundary of the unit circle in the complex plane $|z| = 1$. Compute $\mathbb{E}[Z^2]$.
questions.hint: Write $Z = X + Yi$, where $X$ and $Y$ are real-valued and satisfy $X^2 + Y^2 = 1$. On the real unit circle, $X$ and $Y$ are uncorrelated and identically distributed.
hints: Write Z=e^{iθ} with θ~U(0,2π).
Z²=e^{2iθ}; average it over the circle.
questions.genSolution: With $Z=e^{i\theta}$, $\theta\sim U(0,2\pi)$, we have $\mathbb{E}[Z^2]=\mathbb{E}[e^{2i\theta}]=\dfrac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}e^{2i\theta}\,d\theta=0.$

questions.title: Beer Barrel I
questions.question: Rem's parents found a $120$-quart beer barrel and, angry about it, decided to throw it out. However, Anna pleads with them to allow her to keep some of the beer. They agree to let her keep some, provided she can measure exactly one quart into both a $7$-quart and a $5$-quart container. A transaction is defined as pouring liquid from one container to another. What is the minimum number of transactions required to achieve this goal? If it is not possible, respond with $-1$.
questions.hint:
We can solve this problem by following these steps in order: 

 $$ 
 $$ 

1. Execute 14 transactions of filling and emptying the 7-quart container, which wastes 98 quarts, leaving 22 quarts in the barrel..

 $$ 
 $$ 

2. Fill the 7-quart container, then pour 5 quarts into the 5-quart container, leaving 2 quarts in the 7-quart container.

 $$ 
 $$ 

3. Empty the 5-quart measure, then transfer 2 quarts from the 7-quart to the 5-quart.

 $$ 
 $$ 

4. Fill the 7-quart measure, then fill up the 5-quart from the 7-quart, leaving 4 quarts in the 7-quart.
hints: You need exactly 1 quart in each of the 7- and 5-quart containers simultaneously.
Repeatedly wasting full 7-quart pours reduces the barrel toward a remainder you can split.
Combine the bulk-wasting phase with the classic 7/5 water-pouring measure steps and count transactions.

questions.title: Family Ties
questions.question: A grandmother has two grandchildren and their ages so conveniently line up that the age of each grandchild is one of the digits of the grandmother's age. Additionally, the sum of all three ages is 98. What is the age of the grandmother?
questions.hint: Let $x$ be the age of the $10$s digit child and $y$ be the age of the $1$s digit child. What is the grandmother's age in terms of $x$ and $y$?
hints: Grandmother's age = 10x+y where x,y are the grandchildren's ages (digits).
x=8 gives y=5, so grandmother is 85.
questions.genSolution: Let the grandmother be $10x+y$ with grandchildren aged $x$ and $y$. Then $(10x+y)+x+y=98\Rightarrow11x+2y=98$. The digit solution is $x=8,y=5$, giving grandmother's age $85$ (and $85+8+5=98$).

questions.title: Bank Arbitrage
questions.question: You have a bond and bank account that pay $5\%$ and $3\%$ compounded annually. You are allowed to buy or borrow the bond (needing to repay it back later). Similarly, you are able to borrow money from the bank (needing to pay the principal and interest at the end) or deposit money into the bank. There is an arbitrage opportunity. How much profit will you receive at the end of $1$ year if you have $\$200$ total? Round to the nearest cent.
questions.hint: Try to borrow and lend with equal amounts of your money.
hints: Borrow at the lower rate (3%) and lend at the higher rate (5%) — a rate arbitrage.
Split your $200 so you both borrow and lend, then compare the year-end cash flows.
The locked-in profit is the spread applied to the committed amount.

questions.title: Statistical Test Review VII
questions.question: Andy claims that $20\%$ of the public loves QuantEssential. Aaron dislikes Andy and wants to prove him wrong—specifically, he thinks Andy's estimate is too large. He takes a random sample of 100 people to test his claim. It turns out that 15 of the 100 people love QuantEssential, so Aaron argues that $15\%$ of the public loves QuantEssential. Compute, to the nearest thousandth, the value of $\\beta$ for Aaron's alternative hypothesis under a $0.05$-level test (note that $z_{0.05} = -1.645$).
questions.genSolution: The rejection region is $\hat p\le0.2-1.645\sqrt{\tfrac{0.2\cdot0.8}{100}}=0.1342$. Under the alternative $p=0.15$, $\hat p\approx N(0.15,\tfrac{0.15\cdot0.85}{100})$ with sd $0.0357$. Then $\beta=P(\hat p>0.1342)=P\!\big(Z>\tfrac{0.1342-0.15}{0.0357}\big)=\Phi(0.4425)\approx0.671.$

questions.title: Extrinsic Value I
questions.question:
We have an underlying $S$ with price $S_0 = 5$. You have a European call option on $S$ with strike $K = 2$, currently with value $C_0 = 4.7$. What is the intrinsic (I) and extrinsic (E) value of the option?


 $$ $$ 


Give the answer in the format of $I^2 + E^2$.
questions.hint: How much would you gain if you could exercise at time-$0$?
hints: Intrinsic value of a call = max(S−K,0).
questions.genSolution: The call is in-the-money: intrinsic $I=\max(5-2,0)=3$, extrinsic $E=4.7-3=1.7$. So $I^2+E^2=9+2.89=11.89.$

questions.title: Magic Doors
questions.question: Jeff is trapped in a room with $5$ indistinguishable doors. Behind $2$ of the doors are paths to freedom; one path is $2$ miles long, and the other path is $5$ miles long. Behind $3$ of the doors are paths that magically lead back to the room; one path is $1$ miles long, another path is $3$ miles long, and the final path is $4$ mile long.  $$$$ If Jeff returns to the room, the order of the doors are scrambled and the $5$ doors are once again indistinguishable. What is the expected number of miles Jeff will travel before reaching freedom?
questions.hint: The probability of Jeff selecting any one of the $5$ doors is $\frac{1}{5}$. If Jeff selects one of the doors that leads back to the room, he has the same probability of picking any one of the $5$ doors. Write an equation that models this?
hints: Each try is uniform over 5 doors; freedom doors end, loop doors add distance and restart.
questions.genSolution: Each attempt picks a uniform door. Summing distances: $E=\tfrac15(2+5)+\tfrac15(1+E)+\tfrac15(3+E)+\tfrac15(4+E)=\tfrac{15}5+\tfrac35E=3+\tfrac35E$, so $\tfrac25E=3\Rightarrow E=\tfrac{15}{2}.$

questions.title: Prepped Offer?
questions.question: $ 45\%$ of students don’t use QuantEssential, $25\%$ are heavy prep users and $30\%$ are light prep users. If the heavy prep users are twice as likely to get an offer as light prep users, and light prep users are twice as likely to get an offer as those that don’t use the platform, then what is the probability that if someone got an offer that they were a heavy prep user of QuantEssential?
questions.hint: We are looking to update the probability of something given new information. What theorem can help us out here?
hints: Let light users get an offer with rate r; heavy 2r, non-users r/2.
questions.genSolution: Let a light user get an offer with probability $r$; then heavy $=2r$, non-user $=r/2$. $P(\text{offer})=0.25(2r)+0.30(r)+0.45(\tfrac r2)=1.025r$ and $P(\text{heavy}\cap\text{offer})=0.25(2r)=0.5r$. So $P(\text{heavy}\mid\text{offer})=\tfrac{0.5}{1.025}=\tfrac{20}{41}.$

questions.title: Existent MGF
questions.question: Let $X$ be a random variable with MGF $M(\theta)$ such that $M(1) = 7$. Find the smallest possible value for $M(4)$.
questions.hint: We know that $M(\theta) = \mathbb{E}[e^{\theta X}]$ for all $\theta$ such that this expectation exists. Thus, $M(1) = \mathbb{E}[e^X] = 7$. Consider the random variables $Y = e^X$ and $Z = e^{2X}$
hints: M(4)=E[e^{4X}]=E[(e^X)^4]; apply Jensen with the convex function t↦t⁴.
= 7⁴ = 2401, attained when X is constant.
questions.genSolution: Since $t\mapsto t^4$ is convex, Jensen gives $M(4)=\mathbb{E}[(e^X)^4]\ge(\mathbb{E}[e^X])^4=7^4=2401$, with equality when $X$ is constant. So the smallest possible value is $2401.$

questions.title: Rebuy Gambler
questions.question: Alice and Bob start with $\$15$ and $\$10$, respectively. They bet $\$1$ at a time on opposite outcomes of a fair coin. If Alice bankrupts, she has the ability to buy in again with $\$15$, while Bob has the stack he won. If she bankrupts again, she loses the game. Find the probability Bob loses the game.
questions.hint: The easier quantity to compute is the probability that Alice loses the game, which is the complement here. For Alice to lose the game, she first needs to go bankrupt in the initially, and then bankrupt a second time.
hints: Bob loses unless Alice goes bankrupt twice; compute P(Alice bankrupt) each phase by gambler's ruin.
P(Alice loses)=(2/5)(5/8)=1/4 ⇒ P(Bob loses)=3/4.
questions.genSolution: Bob loses unless Alice is bankrupted twice. First phase (Alice $15$ of $25$ total): $P(\text{ruin})=1-\tfrac{15}{25}=\tfrac25$. After rebuy (Alice $15$ of $40$): $P(\text{ruin})=1-\tfrac{15}{40}=\tfrac58$. So $P(\text{Alice loses})=\tfrac25\cdot\tfrac58=\tfrac14$, giving $P(\text{Bob loses})=1-\tfrac14=\tfrac34.$

questions.title: Distinct Date II
questions.question: Find the most recent date where all of the digits, when expressed in the form $MM/DD/YYYY$ are distinct. For example, $01/23/4567$ would be a valid date. Express your answer in the form $MMDDYYYY$.
questions.hint: Our goal now is the maximize the year to make it as close as possible to our present date. The biggest thing to first note is that the year must start with a $1$. Why?
hints: Maximize the year first; years 2000s fail because the month needs a leading 0 or 1 that clashes.
The latest distinct-digit year that admits a valid MM/DD is 1987 (1988,1989 repeat digits).
Remaining digits {0,2,3,4,5,6}: max month 06, max day ≤30 from {2,3,4,5} is 25 ⇒ 06/25/1987.
questions.genSolution: For a $20XY$ year the digits $2,0$ are used, leaving no way to write a valid month (which needs a leading $0$ or $1$), so we look in the $1900$s. The latest year with all-distinct digits that still allows a valid date is $1987$ ($1988,1989$ repeat). With $\{0,2,3,4,5,6\}$ left, the largest month is $06$ and then the largest day $\le30$ from $\{2,3,4,5\}$ is $25$. So the date is $06/25/1987$.

questions.title: Discrete Walker
questions.question: Your on a random walk along a discrete line consisting of $11$ points, ranging from $0$ to $10$. You have an equal probability of moving either up or down with each step. Once you reach either $10$ or $0$, you must halt your movement. If you begin at point $6$, what is the probability of reaching $10$ before reaching $0$?
questions.hint: symmetric random walk problem.
hints: Symmetric walk with absorbing barriers 0 and 10.
P(reach 10 first) = start/total.
questions.genSolution: For a symmetric walk absorbed at $0$ and $10$, the probability of hitting $10$ first from position $k$ is $k/10$. From $6$ that's $\tfrac{6}{10}=\tfrac35.$

questions.title: Valid Expressions
questions.question: How many valid arithmetic expressions using only $*$ and $+$ are there that include the values $123456789$ in order? Some examples include $1 + 2 + 3456 + 7 * 89, 123 + 456 * 78 + 9,$ and $123456789$.
questions.hint: Consider all sequences in the form $1-2-3-4-5-6-7-8-9$. What can fill each dash?
hints: Between consecutive digits there are 8 gaps.
Each gap is one of: +, *, or nothing (concatenate).
questions.genSolution: There are $8$ gaps between the digits $1$–$9$, and each can hold $+$, $*$, or nothing (joining digits). So there are $3^8=6561$ expressions.

questions.title: Fancy Factorial
questions.question: $\dfrac{10!}{6!} = n!$ for some integer $n$. What is $n$?
questions.hint: Intuitively, $1 < n < 10$. Consider simplifying the fraction into $1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot ...$ until you cannot get any higher.
hints: Cancel 6! from 10!.

questions.title: 2D Paths III
questions.question: You are playing a 2D game where your character is trapped in a $6 \times 6$ grid. Your character starts at $(0,0)$ and can only move up and right. There are two power-ups located at $(2,3)$ and $(4,6)$. How many possible paths can your character take to get to $(6,6)$ such that it can collect at least one power-up?
questions.hint: Let $P_1$ and $P_2$ be the events that your character passes through the power-ups at $(2,3)$ and $(4,6)$, respectively. You want $|P_1 \cup P_2|$. Use Inclusion-Exclusion since these are not disjoint events.
hints: Count paths through (2,3) and through (4,6) separately, then subtract those through both.
questions.genSolution: Paths through $(2,3)$: $\binom52\binom74=350$. Through $(4,6)$: $\binom{10}{4}\binom22=210$. Through both: $\binom52\binom52\binom22=100$. By inclusion–exclusion, $350+210-100=460.$

questions.title: Consecutive 1s
questions.question: You roll a die $5$ times and record the outcomes. What is the probability of rolling at least $3$ consecutive $1$s in those 5 rolls?
questions.hint: Use simple statistics such as combinations and proabilities of fair dice to solve.
hints: Count sequences containing '111' (three consecutive 1s) among 6⁵.
Let B_i be 1s at positions i,i+1,i+2 (i=1,2,3); inclusion–exclusion.
questions.genSolution: Count length-$5$ die sequences with three consecutive $1$s. Let $B_i$ force $1$s at positions $i,i+1,i+2$: $|B_i|=6^2=36$. Overlaps: $|B_1\cap B_2|=|B_2\cap B_3|=6$, $|B_1\cap B_3|=1$, triple $=1$. Inclusion–exclusion gives $108-13+1=96$, so $P=\tfrac{96}{6^5}=\tfrac{96}{7776}=\tfrac1{81}.$

questions.title: Square Shade
questions.question: Consider a $2023 \times 2023$ grid. The grid squares from the middle row are shaded in (this is the 1012th row). What is the probability that a randomly selected rectangle contains at least one shaded square?
questions.hint: The $2023 \times 2023$ grid is split into two $1011 \times 2023$ mini grids by the shaded row. How many rectangles can be made from each split, and how many rectangles can be made in total? Consider the complementary event.
hints: A rectangle is chosen by 2 of 2024 horizontal lines and 2 of 2024 vertical lines; it misses the shaded full row iff its row-span avoids row 1012.
Avoiding pairs: both horizontal lines in {0..1011} or both in {1012..2023}: 2·C(1012,2).
questions.genSolution: Choosing a rectangle uses $2$ of $2024$ horizontal lines (and any verticals). It avoids the shaded row $1012$ iff both horizontal lines lie in $\{0,\dots,1011\}$ or both in $\{1012,\dots,2023\}$ ($1012$ lines each): $2\binom{1012}{2}$ ways out of $\binom{2024}{2}$. That ratio is $\dfrac{1012\cdot1011}{1012\cdot2023}=\dfrac{1011}{2023}$, so $P(\text{contains shaded})=1-\dfrac{1011}{2023}=\dfrac{1012}{2023}.$

questions.title: Gamma Review I
questions.question: Compute $\dfrac{\Gamma\left(\dfrac{9}{4}\right)}{\Gamma\left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)}$.
questions.hint: Use the reduction property $\Gamma(a+1) = a\Gamma(a)$.
hints: Use Γ(a+1)=aΓ(a) to step Γ(9/4) down toward Γ(1/4).
questions.genSolution: By $\Gamma(a+1)=a\Gamma(a)$: $\Gamma(\tfrac94)=\tfrac54\Gamma(\tfrac54)=\tfrac54\cdot\tfrac14\Gamma(\tfrac14)$. So $\dfrac{\Gamma(9/4)}{\Gamma(1/4)}=\tfrac54\cdot\tfrac14=\tfrac5{16}.$

questions.title: Statistical Test Review V
questions.question: Rishab wants to use a Z test to test a hypothesis regarding the population mean. Which of the following conditions need to be satisfied for Rishab to proceed with the Z test? $$$$ (1) The population variance must be known, $$$$ (2) the sample must be a random sample from the population, $$$$ (3) the distribution of the sample mean must be approximately normal, and $$$$(4) the sample size must be at least 30. $$$$ Please provide the conditions in increasing order as a concatenated number. For example, if conditions 2 and 4 must be met, respond with 24.
questions.hint: Consider the formula for the test statistic.
hints: The Z statistic uses the known population variance and a normal sampling distribution of the mean.
Conditions 1 (variance known), 2 (random sample), 3 (mean ~ normal) are required; n≥30 is not strictly necessary if normality holds.
Answer: 123.
questions.genSolution: The $Z$ statistic $\dfrac{\bar X-\mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt n}$ requires a known population variance (1), a random sample (2), and an approximately normal sampling distribution of the mean (3). A size of at least $30$ (4) is not strictly required when normality already holds. So the answer is $123.$

questions.title: 10 Die Sum
questions.question: Find the probability of obtaining a sum of $10$ when rolling three fair $6-$sided dice.
questions.hint:
The possibilities of values (not ordered in any way currently) that result in a sum of $10$ are 
 $$ 
(1,6,3), (1,5,4), (2,6,2), (2,5,3), (2,4,4), (3,4,3)
 $$
hints: Count ordered triples (1–6 each) summing to 10.
There are 27 such ordered outcomes.
questions.genSolution: The number of ordered triples of dice summing to $10$ is $27$ (e.g. from the unordered partitions $(1,3,6),(1,4,5),(2,2,6),(2,3,5),(2,4,4),(3,3,4)$, weighted by their arrangements). So $P=\tfrac{27}{216}=\tfrac18.$

questions.title: Same Side
questions.question: What is the probability of flipping the same side of a coin $4$ times a row?
questions.hint: The first flip will dictate what side the rest of the flips must be.
hints: The first flip sets the target side; the next three must match.
Each match has probability 1/2.
questions.genSolution: The first flip is free; the remaining three must match it, each with probability $\tfrac12$. So $P=(\tfrac12)^3=\tfrac18.$

questions.title: Prime Pair
questions.question: You roll two fair $6-$sided dice. Each of the two dice have the first $6$ prime numbers on the sides. Find the probability that the sum of the two upfaces is also prime?
questions.hint: The numbers on the sides would be $2,3,5,7,11,$ and $13$. Therefore, the sum of the two upfaces has to be between $4$ and $26$, inclusive. What must one of the dice show for the sum to be prime?
hints: Faces are 2,3,5,7,11,13; a prime sum >2 must be odd, so exactly one die shows the only even face, 2.
Then need 2+p prime: works for p=3,5,11 (sum 5,7,13) but not 7,13.
Favorable ordered outcomes: 2·3=6 of 36 ⇒ 1/6.
questions.genSolution: The faces are $\{2,3,5,7,11,13\}$. A prime sum greater than $2$ is odd, so exactly one die must show the only even face $2$, and then $2+p$ must be prime: this holds for $p=3,5,11$ (giving $5,7,13$) but not $7,13$. That's $2\times3=6$ ordered outcomes of $36$, so $P=\tfrac16.$

questions.title: Carded Pair
questions.question: Cards are dealt one-by-one from a standard deck of $52$ cards without replacement. The card dealing ends when either $2$ kings appear OR at least $1$ king and $1$ ace appear, whichever comes first. Find the expected number of cards that are dealt in the game.
questions.hint: Use a "first ace" approach and condition on whether or not the first card between king and ace to appear is king vs. ace. What needs to happen afterwards in each scenario?
hints: Only the 8 'key' cards (4 kings, 4 aces) matter; the m-th key card sits at expected deck position m·53/9.
Stop index T among keys: if the first key is a king, T=2; if it's an ace, T = index of the first king.
questions.genSolution: Among the $8$ key cards ($4$K, $4$A), the $m$-th key lands at expected deck position $m\cdot\tfrac{53}{9}$ (the $8$ keys split $44$ others into $9$ gaps). Let $T$ be the stopping index among keys: if the first key is a king ($\tfrac12$), the next key ends it ($T=2$); if it's an ace ($\tfrac12$), we stop at the first king, whose index is $1+\tfrac{8}{5}=\tfrac{13}{5}$. So $\mathbb{E}[T]=\tfrac12\cdot2+\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac{13}{5}=\tfrac{23}{10}$, and $\mathbb{E}[\text{cards}]=\tfrac{53}{9}\cdot\tfrac{23}{10}=\tfrac{1219}{90}.$

questions.title: Mossel's Dice
questions.question: You roll a fair $6-$sided die until you get a $6$. What is the expected number of rolls performed (including the roll that gives $6$) conditioned on the event all rolls show even numbers?
questions.hint: Let $X$ denote the expected number of rolls until we roll a number other than 2 or 4, and $f$, which takes on the value of the final roll. We want $\mathbb{E}[X \mid f=6]$.
hints: Conditioning on the whole sequence being even changes the distribution (don't just say Geom(1/3)).
P(N=n, all even)=(2/6)^{n−1}(1/6); P(all even)=1/4.
questions.genSolution: Let $N$ be the roll of the first $6$. For 'all rolls even', the first $n-1$ must be in $\{2,4\}$: $P(N=n,\text{even})=(\tfrac26)^{n-1}\tfrac16$, and $P(\text{all even})=\tfrac{1/6}{1-1/3}=\tfrac14$. Then $\mathbb{E}[N\mid\text{even}]=\dfrac{\sum_n n(\tfrac13)^{n-1}\tfrac16}{1/4}=\tfrac23\cdot\dfrac1{(1-1/3)^2}=\tfrac32.$

questions.title: Three-Way Tile
questions.question: How many ways can you tile a $3 \times 8$ grid with $1 \times 2$ and $2 \times 1$ tiles?
questions.hint: Let $t(n)$ be the number of ways of tiling a $3 \times n$ board with these tiles. Consider tiling the first spots all the way at the left. Then, you get a different shape, so let $s(n)$ be the ways to tile that different shape. Condition on the tiling in first column.
hints: Let t(n) be tilings of 3×n; set up a coupled recursion with the 'bumpy' shape s(n).
questions.genSolution: With the standard coupled recursion for $3\times n$ domino tilings, $t(n)=4\,t(n-2)-t(n-4)$ with $t(0)=1,t(2)=3$. This yields $3,11,41,153$, so $t(8)=153.$

questions.title: Annie's Coin
questions.question: Annie and Bob are playing a coin flipping game where they each have biased coins. Annie's coin lands on heads with probability $\frac{1}{3}$. Bob's coin lands on heads with probability $\frac{2}{5}$. Annie and Bob take turns tossing coins; Annie goes first. Given the first person to get a heads wins what is the probability that Annie wins?
questions.hint: Think about how this game repeats, Annie has a $\frac{1}{3}$ chance of winning on her first turn. If she doesn't win on her first turn, then she has a $\frac{3}{5}$ chance to have another shot at flipping.
hints: Annie wins on turn 1 with prob 1/3, or both miss and the game resets.
Solve: p = 5/9.
questions.siteSolution: Let $p$ represent the probabilty of Annie winning the game. She has a $\frac{1}{3}$ chance of winning on her first turn. If she doesn't win on her first turn, then she has a $\frac{3}{5}$ chance to survive Bob's turn and flip again. If Annie survives Bob's turn, we can consider this as the game resetting. Thus,\[\begin{aligned}   p &= \frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{5} \cdot p \\    p &= \frac{5}{9}\end{aligned}\]

questions.title: The Perfect Hedge I
questions.question:
You have two assets. We will call them asset $1$ and asset $2$. Asset $1$ has an expected return of $4\%$ and a variance of $15\%$. Asset $2$ has an expected return of $2\%$ and a variance of $4\%$. They have a correlation $\rho = -1$. 
 $$ 
 $$ 
We want to create a risk-free portfolio using assets $1$ and $2$. We will denote $w_1$ and $w_2$ as the weights of asset $1$ and $2$ in the portfolio respectively. Assume that $w_1 + w_2 = 1$. What is $w_1$? Give the answer to $2$ decimal points.
questions.hint: Construct a portfolio of asset $1$ with $w_1 = w$ and asset $2$ with $w_2 = 1 - w$. What is the variance of $w X_1 + (1 - w)X_2$, where $X_1$ and $X_2$ are assets $1$ and $2$ respectively?
hints: With ρ=−1, the portfolio sd is |w₁σ₁ − w₂σ₂|; set it to 0.
w₁σ₁ = (1−w₁)σ₂ with σ₁=√0.15, σ₂=0.2.
questions.genSolution: For $\rho=-1$ the portfolio standard deviation is $|w_1\sigma_1-w_2\sigma_2|$, zero when $w_1\sigma_1=(1-w_1)\sigma_2$. With $\sigma_1=\sqrt{0.15}\approx0.387$ and $\sigma_2=\sqrt{0.04}=0.2$, $w_1=\dfrac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1+\sigma_2}=\dfrac{0.2}{0.587}\approx0.34.$

questions.title: Building Blocks
questions.question: Max is building a tower from three distinct colors of building blocks (red, blue, and yellow). He has $7$ blocks of each color and randomly picks a block out at a time to build his tower of $5$ blocks in height. What is the probability the tower has composed of exactly two different colors?
questions.hint: What are the ways to select the colors, total combinations of blocks, and combinations of blocks that fit our criteria?
hints: Choose 5 of 21 blocks; count selections using exactly 2 colors.
Pick the 2 colors (C(3,2)=3); from their 14 blocks choose 5 using both: C(14,5)−2C(7,5).
questions.genSolution: There are $\binom{21}{5}=20349$ equally likely selections. For exactly two colors: choose the pair ($\binom32=3$) and pick $5$ of their $14$ blocks using both colors, $\binom{14}5-2\binom75=2002-42=1960$. So favorable $=3\cdot1960=5880$ and $P=\dfrac{5880}{20349}.$

questions.title: Jellybean Jar II
questions.question: A child has a pack of $6$ green and $10$ yellow jellybeans. The child wants to eat $4$ jellybeans, so they grab $4$ jellybeans one-by-one uniformly at random without replacement from the pack. Find the probability that the first two and last two jellybeans agree in colors (not necessarily in order). For example, $GYYG$ and $GGGG$ are both valid, but $GGYG$ is not.
questions.hint: Consider the possible cases for where colors can occur in the first 2 spots.
hints: Condition needs the first two and last two pairs to have the same colour multiset: GG/GG, YY/YY, or {G,Y}/{G,Y}.
questions.genSolution: The first two and last two must share the same colour multiset. Counting ordered draws from $6$G,$10$Y: all green $6{\cdot}5{\cdot}4{\cdot}3=360$; all yellow $5040$; and the mixed case (each pair one G one Y) $4\cdot(6{\cdot}5)(10{\cdot}9)=10800$. Total $16200$ over $16{\cdot}15{\cdot}14{\cdot}13=43680$, giving $\tfrac{135}{364}.$

questions.title: Coin on Chess Board
questions.question: A chess board consists of 2 inch by 2 inch squares. We toss a coin (diameter of 1 inch) that lands somewhere on the board randomly. What is the probability that the coin is completely within one of the 2 inch by 2 inch squares (not on more than 1 square)?
questions.hint: Instead of analyzing the whole chess board, think about the coin only landing inside one square.
hints: The coin (radius 1/2) stays inside a 2×2 square iff its center is ≥1/2 from each edge.
That center region is a 1×1 square inside the 2×2.
questions.genSolution: The coin lies entirely within a square iff its center is at least $\tfrac12$ inch from every edge, i.e. inside a concentric $1\times1$ region of the $2\times2$ square. So $P=\dfrac{1^2}{2^2}=\dfrac14.$

questions.title: Circular Charlie
questions.question: There are $14$ boys standing around in a circle, of which one of them is Charlie. $10$ girls walk up and join the circle at random to form a larger circle. Each girl must be between $2$ boys. If the girls arrange such that all ways of forming a larger circle are equally likely, find the probability Charlie is still standing between two boys.
questions.hint: The $14$ boys make $14$ adjacencies for the girls to fill in. There are $\displaystyle \binom{14}{10}$ ways for the girls to arrange since each girl must be between two boys.
hints: The 14 boys create 14 gaps; the girls fill 10 of them (C(14,10) ways).
Charlie stays between two boys iff both of his adjacent gaps are empty.
questions.genSolution: The girls occupy $10$ of the $14$ gaps between boys, $\binom{14}{10}$ ways. Charlie remains between two boys iff his two adjacent gaps are both empty, i.e. all $10$ girls go in the other $12$ gaps: $\dfrac{\binom{12}{10}}{\binom{14}{10}}=\dfrac{66}{1001}=\dfrac{6}{91}.$

questions.title: A Low Median
questions.question: Suppose we draw 3 numbers from a $\text{Unif}[0,1]$ distribution independently. We then sort the numbers in ascending order. What is the probability that the median is less than $2/3$?
questions.hint: Break up into cases of exactly one larger and none larger.
hints: The median of 3 is < 2/3 iff at least 2 of the 3 values are < 2/3.
Each value < 2/3 with probability 2/3.
questions.genSolution: The median of three is below $\tfrac23$ iff at least two values are below $\tfrac23$ (each with probability $\tfrac23$): $\binom32(\tfrac23)^2(\tfrac13)+(\tfrac23)^3=\tfrac{12}{27}+\tfrac{8}{27}=\tfrac{20}{27}.$

questions.title: Clockwise Murder
questions.question: There are $N$ people standing in a circle labeled 1 through $N$, such that the smallest power of two less than or equal to $N$ is $X$. Starting with the person 1, they take a sword and kill the person to their left. Then, they pass the sword to the living person on their left, and the process continues until one person is left. The survivor's number can be expressed as $aN + bX + c$. Compute $a + 2b + 3c$.
questions.hint: Notice that person 1 will always win if there are a total of $2^k$ people. What if it is not a power of $2$?
hints: This is the Josephus problem with step 2; person 1 always wins when N is a power of 2.
Writing N=X+L with X the largest power of 2 ≤N, the survivor is 2L+1=2N−2X+1.
questions.genSolution: In the Josephus problem (every second person killed), if $N=2^k$ then person $1$ survives. Writing $N=X+L$ with $X=2^{\lfloor\log_2 N\rfloor}$, the survivor is $2L+1=2(N-X)+1=2N-2X+1$. Matching $aN+bX+c$: $a=2,b=-2,c=1$, so $a+2b+3c=2-4+3=1.$

questions.title: Light Switch
questions.question: A stoplight displays the colors red (stop), yellow (slow down), and green (go). The colors go in the sequence green $\rightarrow$ yellow $\rightarrow$ red. The light stays green for $40$ seconds until it switches to yellow for $4$ seconds. Afterwards, it turns red for $40$ seconds before turning green again and repeating the cycle. Find the probability that the color of the stoplight switches in a $4$ second interval.
questions.hint: The key here is to condition on the color of the light at the start of the interval.
hints: Cycle length = 40+4+40 = 84 s with 3 switch instants.
A 4-s interval catches a switch if its start falls in the 4 s before a switch: three length-4 windows.
Total favorable 12 s of 84 ⇒ 12/84 = 1/7.
questions.genSolution: The cycle is $40+4+40=84$ seconds with three switch instants. A $4$-second interval contains a switch iff its start lies in the $4$ seconds before one of them; these three length-$4$ windows are disjoint, totaling $12$ seconds. So $P=\dfrac{12}{84}=\dfrac17.$

questions.title: Specific Card Pull I
questions.question: A deck of cards is shuffled well. The cards are dealt one-by-one, until the two of hearts appears. Find the probability that no kings, queens or jacks appear before the two of hearts.
questions.hint: How many cards are actually of interest in this scenario?
hints: Only the 2♥ and the 12 face cards (K,Q,J) matter — 13 cards.
The condition is that the 2♥ comes before all 12 face cards.
By symmetry the 2♥ is first among these 13 with probability 1/13.
questions.genSolution: The only relevant cards are the $2\heartsuit$ and the $12$ face cards. We need the $2\heartsuit$ to precede all of them, i.e. be first among these $13$ equally likely orderings: probability $\tfrac1{13}.$

questions.title: Price an Option II
questions.question:
You have access to the European call options at the following strikes and $T_0$ prices, the underlying $S$ which has initial price $S_0 = 7$, and a bond that has initial price $0.9$, paying $1$ at time $T$. The calls are given in the format of $(\text{Strike } K, \text{Price } C_0)$


 $$ 
\begin{align*}
(5, 4.2) \\ 
(10, 1.4) \\ 
(15, 0.7) \\ 
(20, 0.1)
\end{align*}
 $$ 


Find the time-$0$ price of a contract that pays $\max({3S_T, 15})$ at time $T$.
questions.hint: Create a replicating portfolio
hints: Rewrite max(3S,15)=15+3·max(S−5,0).
That's 15 bonds plus 3 calls at K=5.
questions.genSolution: $\max(3S_T,15)=15+3\max(S_T-5,0)$, replicated by $15$ bonds and $3$ calls struck at $5$. Its price is $15(0.9)+3(4.2)=13.5+12.6=26.1.$

questions.title: Poker Hands IV
questions.question: A poker hand consists of five cards from a fair deck of 52 cards. What is the probability that you have a single pair (two cards of the same value and the other three cards of different unique values)?
questions.hint: Find all possible values and suits of the pair and then find the possibilities for the other three cards.
hints: Pick the pair's rank & suits, then 3 distinct other ranks each with one suit.
questions.genSolution: Choose the pair's rank ($13$) and $2$ suits ($\binom42=6$); then $3$ distinct other ranks ($\binom{12}3$) each with one of $4$ suits. So $P=\dfrac{13\cdot6\cdot\binom{12}3\cdot4^3}{\binom{52}5}=\dfrac{1098240}{2598960}=\dfrac{352}{833}.$

questions.title: Single Double Sum
questions.question:
A $6-$sided die with values $1-6$ is weighed such that the probability of rolling the values $2,3,4,5,$ and $6$ are the same whether we roll this die once or if we roll the die twice and take the sum of the two outcomes. Let $p_1$ be the probability that the value $1$ is obtained on this die. $p_1$ is the solitary positive solution to the polynomial equation 
 $$ 
\displaystyle \sum_{k=1}^6 c_kp_1^k = 1
 $$ 
 for some real constants $c_1,\dots, c_6$. Find $\displaystyle \sum_{k=1}^6 c_k$.
questions.hint: Let $p_i$ be the probability of rolling $i$ on this die. We know that to get sum of $2$, we must roll $1$ twice. Therefore, we must have that $p_2 = p_1^2$.
hints: Match P(value=v) for one roll with P(sum=v) for two rolls, for v=2..6.
This forces p_2=p_1², p_3=2p_1³, p_4=5p_1⁴, p_5=14p_1⁵, p_6=42p_1⁶ (Catalan numbers).
Normalization Σp=1 gives coefficients 1,1,2,5,14,42 summing to 65.
questions.genSolution: Equating one-roll and two-roll probabilities for $2,\dots,6$ gives $p_2=p_1^2$, $p_3=2p_1^3$, $p_4=5p_1^4$, $p_5=14p_1^5$, $p_6=42p_1^6$ — the Catalan numbers. The normalization $\sum p_i=1$ reads $\sum_{k=1}^6 c_k p_1^k=1$ with $(c_k)=(1,1,2,5,14,42)$, whose sum is $65.$

questions.title: 1900 Age
questions.question: A man's age at death was $1/29$th the year of his birth. The man lived to see his birthday in $1900$ but died before the end of World War II in $1945$. How old did he turn on this birthday?
questions.hint: Let $x$ be the age at which he died. Then $29x$ was the year he was born. The year he died then was thus $29x+ x = 30x$.
hints: If x is the age at death, birth year = 29x and death year = 30x.
Need death year <1945 and birth year in the 1800s: x=64 ⇒ born 1856, died 1920.
His 1900 birthday age = 1900−1856 = 44.
questions.genSolution: If $x$ is his age at death, the birth year is $29x$ and the death year $30x$. Requiring a death before $1945$ and a 19th-century birth gives $x=64$: born $1856$, died $1920$. On his $1900$ birthday he turned $1900-1856=44.$

questions.title: Triangular Change
questions.question: You have a triangle, $ABC$, that is changing with respect to time. $$$$ The length of side $AC$ is increasing at $3$ units per second, the length of side $AB$ is decreasing at $2$ units per second, and the length of side $BC$ is decreasing at $2$ units per second. At time $t=t_{1}$, sides \(A C, A B, B C\) have side lengths $12,14,$ and $16$ respectively.  $$$$ What is the rate of change of the perimeter of $ABC$ at time $t_1$ in units per second?
questions.hint: To get the rate of change of the permieter, make a function for the permieter and take the derivative in terms of time.
hints: Perimeter P = AC+AB+BC; differentiate.
= −1 units/sec (lengths irrelevant).
questions.genSolution: The perimeter's rate is the sum of the side rates: $\dfrac{dP}{dt}=3+(-2)+(-2)=-1$ units per second (the current lengths don't matter).

questions.title: Competitive Sampling
questions.question: You and your opponent each draw a number from $U(0, 1)$ without revealing it. You each have the option to redraw and keep that value instead, and the other person doesn't know which choice they made. The person with the higher number wins. The optimal strategy is of the form reroll if the number is less than $k$. Find $k$ to $3$ decimal places.
questions.hint: Remember that the goal is not to maximize the expected value of your draw but the probability of winning.
hints: This is symmetric; both reroll below a threshold k, aiming to win not to maximize value.
Indifference at the threshold yields k²+k−1=0.
questions.genSolution: By symmetry both players reroll when below a threshold $k$. The optimal $k$ is where you're indifferent between keeping $k$ and rerolling against the opponent's same strategy, which reduces to $k^2+k-1=0$. The positive root is $k=\dfrac{\sqrt5-1}{2}\approx0.618.$

questions.title: Too Many Primes to Count
questions.question: We are playing a game with two players. The first player rolls a $6-$sided die $10000$ times and the second player $10001$ times. What are the odds of the second player getting more prime numbers than the first one?
questions.hint: There is a $\frac{1}{2}$ probability that either person will win with an equal amount of throws. What kind of edge does a single extra throw give to player 2?
hints: Each roll is 'prime' (2,3,5) with probability 1/2.
With equal rolls each would lead with probability 1/2; the extra roll breaks ties symmetrically.
P(player 2 leads) = 1/2.
questions.genSolution: Each roll is prime with probability $\tfrac12$. With the same number of rolls, by symmetry each player leads with equal probability and ties split evenly; giving player 2 one extra roll exactly balances this, so $P(\text{player 2 has more})=\tfrac12.$

questions.title: Overlapping Subsets
questions.question: Consider the set $\Omega = \{1,2,\dots,20\}$. Two subsets $A$ and $B$ of $\Omega$ are uniformly at random selected from the power set of $\Omega$ independently. It is possible that $A = B$. Define $N = |A \cap B|$. Compute $\dfrac{\text{Var}(N)}{\mathbb{E}[N]}$.
questions.hint: For an element $x \in A \cap B$, we know that $x \in A$ and $x \in B$. Since $A$ and $B$ are independently selected, the events $\{x \in A\}$ and $\{x \in B\}$ are independent as well.
hints: Each element is in A∩B with probability 1/4 (independently).
questions.genSolution: Each of the $20$ elements is in $A\cap B$ independently with probability $\tfrac12\cdot\tfrac12=\tfrac14$, so $N\sim\mathrm{Binom}(20,\tfrac14)$ with $\mathbb{E}[N]=5$ and $\mathrm{Var}(N)=20\cdot\tfrac14\cdot\tfrac34=\tfrac{15}4$. Thus $\dfrac{\mathrm{Var}(N)}{\mathbb{E}[N]}=\dfrac{15/4}{5}=\dfrac34.$

questions.title: Big Smalls
questions.question: Alice and Bob play the following game: Alice generates a uniformly random integer between $1$ and $100$, inclusive of both. Bob can pick any number that he wants between $1$ and $100$, inclusive of both. Whoever has the larger number must pays out the amount of the smaller number to the person with the smaller number. For example, if Alice selects $70$ and Bob selects $50$, Bob will receive $50$ from Alice. If they select the same number, nobody pays out anything. Assuming optimal play by Bob, what is his expected payout?
questions.hint:
Let $Z_x$ be Bob's gain if he selects the value $x$. Furthermore, let $Y$ be the randomly generated number. We can ignore the case where $Y = x$, as the payout there is $0$ for both people. By Law of Total Expectation, we have

 $$ 

\mathbb{E}[Z_x]=\mathbb{E}[Z_x \mid x>Y] \mathbb{P}[x>Y]+\mathbb{E}[Z_x \mid x<Y] \mathbb{P}[x<Y]


 $$ 


Maximize this function.
hints: Bob's gain picking x: lose Y if x>Y, gain x if x<Y.
Maximize: x=33 or 34 give 3366/200 = 16.83.
questions.genSolution: If Bob picks $x$, he loses $Y$ when $x>Y$ and gains $x$ when $x<Y$: $\mathbb{E}[Z_x]=-\dfrac{(x-1)x}{200}+\dfrac{x(100-x)}{100}=\dfrac{x(201-3x)}{200}$. Maximizing over integers gives $x=33$ or $34$, both yielding $\dfrac{3366}{200}=16.83.$

questions.title: Combinatorial Electrician
questions.question: An electrician sets up $12$ light bulbs on a circle. Each pair of light bulbs has a wire connecting them. Each individual wire is active with probability $\dfrac{1}{2}$, independent of all other wires. A collection of $2 \leq k \leq 12$ light bulbs is said to form a complete $k-$circuit if between any 2 light bulbs in the circuit, we have an active wire. A lightbulb can be part of multiple circuits. Find the expected number of $4-$circuits in our electrician's arrangement of light bulbs.
questions.hint: How many $4-$circuits are there? Set up an indicator random variable for each one. How many wires need to be active in a $4-$circuit to be complete?
hints: There are C(12,4) candidate 4-circuits; use an indicator for each.
A 4-circuit needs all C(4,2)=6 wires active: probability (1/2)⁶.
questions.genSolution: There are $\binom{12}{4}=495$ sets of $4$ bulbs, each forming a complete circuit iff all $\binom42=6$ connecting wires are active, probability $(\tfrac12)^6=\tfrac1{64}$. By linearity, $\mathbb{E}=\dfrac{495}{64}.$

questions.title: Unknown Starter
questions.question: You have $3$ cards, each labeled $n$, $n+1$, $n+2$, and you don't know $n$. All cards start face down. You flip one card and observe the value. If you say "stay", your payout is equal to that card's value. If you don't "stay", then you flip another card. Again, choose to "stay" (and receive payout equal to the 2nd card's value) or flip the final card and receive payout equal to the final card's value. Design the optimal strategy. The expected out payout of this strategy is $n + c$ for some constant $c$. Find $c$.
questions.hint: For simplicity, we can assume the values of the cards are $1,2,$ and $3$, as $n$ is arbitrary and is just a shifting factor. If you make a decision at the first or third card, you have no choice on what the card will be, so the expected value will be $2$ in each of those cases. Consider the possible differences between the first two cards.
hints: WLOG cards are 1,2,3; the strategy can only use the difference of the first two cards (n is unknown).
After flipping: on difference +2 keep (it's the max); on a −1 difference take the third; otherwise stay/decide to maximize.
Averaging the six orderings gives 14/6=7/3=n+4/3, so c=4/3.
questions.genSolution: Take the cards as $1,2,3$ (shift by $n-1$). Since $n$ is unknown, decisions can only use the observed difference of the first two cards. Flipping the first card (staying yields only $2$), then for each ordered pair choosing optimally on the difference: outcomes are $2,3,3,1,3,2$ for $(1,2),(2,3),(2,1),(3,2),(1,3),(3,1)$, averaging $\tfrac{14}{6}=\tfrac73$. So the expected payout is $n+\tfrac43$, giving $c=\tfrac43.$

questions.title: Mean Difference
questions.question: Suppose that $A_1, \dots, A_{10}$ are independent random variables such that $\mu_i = \mathbb{E}[A_i] = 2i-1$ and $\text{Var}(A_i) = 100$ meaning there is a common variance $100$ but not a common mean. Define $\overline{\mu} = \dfrac{1}{10}\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{10} \mu_i$. Compute $\mathbb{E}[(A_1 - \overline{A})^2]$.
questions.hint: Consider $\text{Var}(A_1 - \overline{})$ and using bilinearity of covariance.
hints: E[(A₁−Ā)²]=Var(A₁−Ā)+(E[A₁−Ā])².
questions.genSolution: $\mathbb{E}[(A_1-\bar A)^2]=\mathrm{Var}(A_1-\bar A)+(\mathbb{E}[A_1-\bar A])^2$. Here $\mathbb{E}[A_1]=1$, $\bar\mu=\tfrac1{10}\sum(2i-1)=10$, so the mean term is $(1-10)^2=81$. Writing $A_1-\bar A=\tfrac9{10}A_1-\tfrac1{10}\sum_{i=2}^{10}A_i$ with independent variance-$100$ terms: $\mathrm{Var}=\tfrac{81}{100}(100)+\tfrac{9}{100}(100)=90$. Total $=90+81=171.$

questions.title: Water Measurement
questions.question: A maid was sent to the brook with two vessels that exactly measured $7$ pints and $11$ pints exactly. She had to bring back exactly $2$ pints of water. What is the smallest possible number of transactions necessary? A transaction is filling a vessel, emptying it, or pouring from one vessel to another. In other words, you cannot transfer a proportion of the vessel. You must transfer all of it.
questions.hint: Keep track of the $7$-pint and $11$-pint vessels. What sequence of transactions gets you to $2$ pints in one of the vessels?
hints: You have 7- and 11-pint vessels and want exactly 2 pints, minimizing fill/empty/pour transactions.
Since gcd(7,11)=1, 2 is reachable; repeatedly pour 7 into 11 and reset to step the remainder.
Track states (a,b) and count moves to first reach a 2 in some vessel.

questions.title: Cheese Lover I
questions.question: Jon loves cheese. He decides to make $100$ blocks of cheese. The distribution of the weight (in grams) of each block he makes follows IID Exp$\left(\dfrac{1}{250}\right)$ distribution. Let $W_i$ denote the weight of the $i$th block of cheese, and $T_{100}$ represent the total weight of the $100$ blocks of cheese. Using Markov's Inequality, what is an upper bound on $\mathbb{P}[T_{100} > 26000]$?
questions.hint: Markov's Inequality states that for a non-negative random variable $X$ and $c > 0$, $\mathbb{P}[X > c] \leq \dfrac{\mathbb{E}[X]}{c}$
questions.genSolution: Each block has mean $250$, so $\mathbb{E}[T_{100}]=25000$. By Markov's inequality, $P(T_{100}>26000)\le\dfrac{25000}{26000}=\dfrac{25}{26}.$

questions.title: Die Multiple I
questions.question: A fair $6-$sided die is rolled until the sum of all upfaces is even. Find the expected number of rolls performed.
questions.hint: Condition on the parity of the first roll.
hints: Stop when the running sum is even; condition on the first roll's parity.
Even first (1/2): done in 1. Odd first (1/2): need a later odd to flip parity, Geom(1/2) more rolls (mean 2).
questions.genSolution: If the first roll is even (prob $\tfrac12$) the sum is even and we stop. If odd, we need a subsequent odd roll to make the sum even, which takes a further $\mathrm{Geom}(\tfrac12)$ rolls (mean $2$). So $E=\tfrac12(1)+\tfrac12(1+2)=2.$

questions.title: Lapping
questions.question: Lauda and Hunt are in NASCAR. Lauda can drive a lap in $6$ minutes, while Hunt takes $9$ minutes to drive a lap. After how many minutes will Lauda lap Hunt i.e. catch back up to Hunt a lap ahead of him?
questions.hint: We can think about this in terms of number of laps completed per Alice lap. For every lap Alice completes, Bob completes $2/3$ of a lap.
hints: Lap rates: 1/6 and 1/9 laps per minute.
Lapping = gaining a full lap at the relative rate 1/6 − 1/9 = 1/18.
Time = 1/(1/18) = 18 minutes.
questions.genSolution: Lauda gains on Hunt at $\tfrac16-\tfrac19=\tfrac1{18}$ lap per minute. To gain a full lap takes $\dfrac{1}{1/18}=18$ minutes.

questions.title: Company Purchase II
questions.question: Quant Questions Inc. generates $\$100$ of cash flow per year, and its cash flow is expected to grow at $5\%$ per year until you sell. Although, you can invest in a similar company and earn $10\%$ per year. How much would you pay to buy Quant Questions Inc.?
questions.hint:
$ 
\text{Company Value} = \frac{\text{Cash Flow}}{\text{Discount Rate - Cash Flow Growth Rate}}$ where $\text{Cash Flow Growth Rate < Discount Rate}$.
hints: This is a growing perpetuity: value = CF/(r − g).
questions.genSolution: A growing perpetuity is worth $\dfrac{\text{CF}}{r-g}=\dfrac{100}{0.10-0.05}=\dfrac{100}{0.05}=2000.$

questions.title: Optimal Bidders I
questions.question: Carter has come into contact with a bounty of gold. He takes it to an auction shop. The auction shop says that each person that bids will place a bid that is uniformly distributed between $\$500$ and $\$1000$. They also state that they can recruit people to bid for a price of $\$5$ per person. Assuming Carter selects the optimal number of people to bid on his gold, what is his expected payout?
questions.hint: Write the expected payout as a function of $n$, the number of people that they recruit. Try to maximize it with respect to $n$.
hints: Maximize: derivative 500/(n+1)² − 5 = 0 ⇒ (n+1)²=100 ⇒ n=9.
questions.genSolution: With $n$ bidders, $\mathbb{E}[\max]=500+\tfrac{500n}{n+1}$, so the payout is $f(n)=1000-\tfrac{500}{n+1}-5n$. Setting $f'(n)=\tfrac{500}{(n+1)^2}-5=0$ gives $n+1=10$, $n=9$, and $f(9)=1000-50-45=905.$

questions.title: Stranded at Sea
questions.question: You are boating through the Mediterranean Sea when your motor breaks down. You remember learning from your divemaster that the probability of seeing at least one boat within a given hour is $73.7856\%$. Assume that boats are observed as a Poisson process with a fixed rate of observations per time step and that observations are independent in any mutually exclusive time period. What is the probability that you will see at least one boat within the next 10 minutes?
questions.hint: Let $p$ be the probability that you do not see a boat in 10 minutes- you are solving for $1-p$. How can you use complementary probability to define the probability that you do not see a boat within an hour?
hints: For a Poisson process, P(none in 10 min)=P(none in hour)^{1/6}=0.8.
questions.genSolution: $P(\text{no boat in an hour})=1-0.737856=0.262144=0.8^6$. Over $10$ minutes ($\tfrac16$ hour) the no-boat probability is $0.8^{6\cdot(1/6)}=0.8$, so $P(\ge1\text{ boat in 10 min})=1-0.8=0.2.$

questions.title: Greedy Bob
questions.question:
Alice and Bob are playing a game. Alice spins a spinner and wins $X$ dollars, with
mean 12 and variance 2. Bob flips a coin, and if the coin lands on heads, then Bob takes from Alice whatever she won, else Bob wins nothing.  What is $\text{Var}(Y)$, where Y denotes the amount Bob wins?
questions.hint: Define an indicator variable for if Bob gets a head. How can this be used to define Y, and what laws of variance can be utilized to solve for $\text{Var}(Y)$?
hints: Y=I·X with I~Bernoulli(1/2) (heads) and X independent (mean 12, var 2).
questions.genSolution: $Y=IX$ with $I\sim\mathrm{Bernoulli}(\tfrac12)$ independent of $X$ ($\mathbb{E}[X]=12$, $\mathrm{Var}(X)=2$). Then $\mathbb{E}[Y]=\tfrac12\cdot12=6$ and $\mathbb{E}[Y^2]=\tfrac12\,\mathbb{E}[X^2]=\tfrac12(2+144)=73$, so $\mathrm{Var}(Y)=73-36=37.$

questions.title: Stack Double
questions.question: $7$ men labeled $A-G$ play a coin flip game that starts with player $A$. At each turn, the player will flip a coin. If it appears heads, then the player must double the stack of each other player that plays the game, taking the money from their own stack. In other words, the payout to each of the other $6$ players is the value of their current stack. Otherwise, nothing happens. The sequence $HHHHHHH$ is observed and each of the $7$ players ends up with $\$1.28$ as their final stack. If $x_1,\dots,x_7$ represent the amount that each player started with, find the value of $10000(x_1^2 + \dots + x_7^2)$. For example, if player $A$ starts with $\$5.31$, then $x_1 = 5.31$.
questions.hint: If the player is the $i$th to flip the coin, his money will be doubled $7-i$ times after he flips. Therefore, after obtaining his heads and paying out the sum to each of the other players, the $i$th player to flip must have $\dfrac{1.28}{2^{7-i}}$ in the bank. Furthermore, note that there is always $\$1.28 \cdot 7 = \$8.96$ in the game at all turns.
hints: Total money is always 8.96. The i-th flipper, just after paying out, holds 1.28/2^{7−i}; before flipping he holds 4.48+0.64/2^{7−i}.
Earlier heads double his initial, so x_i=(4.48+0.64/2^{7−i})/2^{i−1}.
questions.genSolution: The pot is always $\$8.96$. Player $i$ (the $i$-th to flip), right after paying everyone, holds $\tfrac{1.28}{2^{7-i}}$, so before flipping he holds $S_i=4.48+\tfrac{0.64}{2^{7-i}}$. Since the earlier $i-1$ heads doubled his initial stake, $x_i=S_i/2^{i-1}$, giving $x=(4.49,2.25,1.13,0.57,0.29,0.15,0.08)$. Then $\sum x_i^2=26.9374$, so $10000\sum x_i^2=269374.$

questions.title: 9 Appearance
questions.question: How many times does the digit $9$ occur when counting $1$ to $1000$? Note that the number $919$, for example, would count as $2$ $9$s.
questions.hint: The first thing is to count the number of times $9$ appears in $1-99$. Can you use this to account for the different cases of the starting digit?
hints: Count digit 9 over 000–999 (1000 numbers, 3 positions).
Each position shows 9 in 1/10 of numbers: 100 times per position.
questions.genSolution: Writing numbers $000$–$999$, each of the $3$ digit positions is a $9$ in exactly $\tfrac1{10}$ of the $1000$ numbers, i.e. $100$ times. Total $3\cdot100=300$, and $1000$ contributes no $9$s, so $300.$

questions.title: 20-30 Die Split II
questions.question: Alice and Bob have fair $30-$sided and $20-$sided dice, respectively. The goal is to obtain the largest possible value. Alice rolls her die one time. However, Bob can roll his die twice and keep the maximum of the two values. In the event of a tie, Bob is the winner. Find the probability Alice is the winner.
questions.hint:
Let $W$ be the event Alice wins. Then we can use Law of Total Probability to condition on whether or not Alice's roll is larger than $20$. Let $A$ be the value Alice rolls. Furthermore, let $B_1$ and $B_2$ be the values that Bob rolls on the first and second roll. We have that 
 $$ 
\mathbb{P}[W] = \mathbb{P}[W \mid A > 20] \mathbb{P}[A > 20] + \mathbb{P}[W \mid A \leq 20] \mathbb{P}[A \leq 20]
 $$
hints: Alice wins iff A > max(B₁,B₂); ties go to Bob. P(max<a)=[P(B<a)]².
Sum (1/30)Σ_{a=1}^{30}[P(B<a)]², with P(B<a)=(a−1)/20 for a≤21 and 1 beyond.
questions.genSolution: Alice wins iff her roll beats both of Bob's, $A>\max(B_1,B_2)$, with $P(\max<a)=\big(P(B<a)\big)^2$. Then $P(W)=\tfrac1{30}\sum_{a=1}^{30}\big(P(B<a)\big)^2$ where $P(B<a)=\tfrac{a-1}{20}$ for $a\le21$ and $1$ for $a>20$. The sum is $\tfrac1{400}\sum_{k=0}^{19}k^2+10=6.175+10=\tfrac{647}{40}$, so $P(W)=\tfrac1{30}\cdot\tfrac{647}{40}=\tfrac{647}{1200}.$

questions.title: Uniform Fix
questions.question: Let $U \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)$ and $X_1,X_2,\dots \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)$ IID. Define $N = \text{min}\{n \in \mathbb{N} : X_n > U\}$. Find $\mathbb{E}[N]$. Enter $-1$ if the answer is infinite.
questions.hint: The key here is to condition on $U$. What is the conditional distribution of $N \mid U = u$?
hints: Condition on U=u: each X_n exceeds u with probability 1−u.
N|U=u ~ Geom(1−u), so E[N|U=u]=1/(1−u).
E[N]=∫₀¹ 1/(1−u) du diverges ⇒ infinite (−1).
questions.genSolution: Given $U=u$, each $X_n>u$ with probability $1-u$, so $N\mid U=u\sim\mathrm{Geom}(1-u)$ with mean $\tfrac1{1-u}$. Then $\mathbb{E}[N]=\int_0^1\tfrac1{1-u}\,du=\infty$, so the answer is $-1.$

questions.title: Crossing the River
questions.question: During the Turkish stampede in Thrace, a small detachment found itself confronted by a wide and deep river. However, they discovered a boat in which two children were rowing about. It was so small that it would only carry the two children, or one grown up person. There are a total of $358$ officers and soldiers. How many times does the boat pass from shore to shore if all the officers and soldiers need to get across, and that the two children are the last ones in joint possession of the boat?
questions.hint: How can you get one soldier across and leave both children with the boat?
hints: The unit operation ferries one soldier across and returns both children to the start.
That cycle is 4 crossings: 2 kids over, 1 back, 1 soldier over, 1 kid back.
358 soldiers × 4 = 1432 crossings (children end with the boat).
questions.genSolution: To move one soldier across while keeping both children available takes $4$ crossings: both children row over, one returns, a soldier crosses, the other child returns. Repeating for all $358$ soldiers gives $358\times4=1432$ crossings, leaving the two children with the boat.

questions.title: Missing Million II
questions.question: You are on a game show with $3$ doors in front of you. One of the doors has $\$1$ million inside, while the other two are empty. In the final round, the host lets you spin a wheel two times that may reveal which door the $\$1$ million is in.  On each spin, this wheel will tell you the location of the $\$1$ million $3/5$ of the time, independent between spins. Otherwise, it tells you nothing about the location of the money. After two spins, if the wheel has not revealed to you the location of the $\$1$ million, you must guess uniformly at random. What is the probability you locate the $\$1$ million door?
questions.hint: We do not locate the door with $\$1$ million exactly when the wheel does not tell us any information both times.
hints: You miss the location only if neither spin reveals it: (2/5)²=4/25.
Otherwise you know it; if not, you guess 1/3.
questions.genSolution: The wheel fails both times with probability $(\tfrac25)^2=\tfrac4{25}$. So $P(\text{locate})=\big(1-\tfrac4{25}\big)+\tfrac4{25}\cdot\tfrac13=\tfrac{63}{75}+\tfrac4{75}=\tfrac{67}{75}.$

questions.title: Unfriendly
questions.question: Jimmy has $8$ friends. He will only invite $5$ to his party. However, two of his friends don't get along and they refuse to attend together. How many possible combinations of guests are possible given this constraint?
questions.hint: Calculate the total number of ways to invite people and then remove those that invite both.
hints: Total ways to pick 5 of 8 minus those with both enemies.
C(8,5)=56; both enemies in ⇒ choose 3 of remaining 6 = C(6,3)=20.
questions.genSolution: Total invitations: $\binom85=56$. Those including both enemies: $\binom63=20$. So $56-20=36.$

questions.title: Coin Pair IV
questions.question: Four fair coins appear in front of you. You flip all four at once and observe the outcomes of the coins. After seeing the outcomes, you may flip any pair of tails again. You may not flip a single coin without flipping another. You can iterate this process as many times as there are at least two tails to flip. Find the expected number of coin flips needed until you are unable to better your position.
questions.hint: Let $T$ be the total number of coin flips needed and $X$ be the total number of heads that appear on the first flipping of the coins. Then $\mathbb{E}[T] = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[T \mid X]] = \sum_{k=0}^{4}\mathbb{E}[T \mid X =k]\mathbb{P}[X = k]$ by Law of Total Expectation. $X \sim \text{Binom}\left(4,\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$ because $X$ counts the number of heads appearing in $4$ flips of a fair coin.
hints: Track tails t; each round flips a pair of tails (2 flips), t→t−2,t−1,t with prob 1/4,1/2,1/4.
Add the 4 initial flips and average over t~Binom(4,½): total = 176/27.
questions.genSolution: Let $E_t$ be the expected additional flips with $t$ tails. Flipping a pair: $E_t=2+\tfrac14E_{t-2}+\tfrac12E_{t-1}+\tfrac14E_t$, giving $E_2=\tfrac83,E_3=\tfrac{40}9,E_4=\tfrac{176}{27}$. The initial $4$ flips plus the average over $t\sim\mathrm{Binom}(4,\tfrac12)$ is $4+\tfrac{68}{27}=\tfrac{176}{27}.$

questions.title: Better in Red V
questions.question: The surfaces of a $3 \times 3 \times 3$ cube (initially white) are painted red. The large cube is then cut up into $27$ $1 \times 1 \times 1$ small cubes. One of the small cubes is selected uniformly at random and is rolled twice. The face appearing is red both times. What is the probability the cube selected was a corner cube?
questions.hint: Given that we have new information entering this problem (both rolls of the selected mini cube yielded a red face shown), use Bayes Theorem to update the probability that the chosen cube was a corner.
hints: Cube types: corner (8, 3 red faces), edge (12, 2), face (6, 1), center (1, 0). P(two reds)=(red/6)².
P(2 red): corner 1/4, edge 1/9, face 1/36, center 0.
questions.genSolution: By type, $P(\text{two reds})$ is $\tfrac14$ (corner), $\tfrac19$ (edge), $\tfrac1{36}$ (face), $0$ (center), with priors $\tfrac{8,12,6,1}{27}$. Total $=\tfrac1{27}(8\cdot\tfrac14+12\cdot\tfrac19+6\cdot\tfrac1{36})=\tfrac{7}{54}$, and corner contributes $\tfrac{8}{27}\cdot\tfrac14=\tfrac2{27}$. So $P(\text{corner}\mid\text{2 red})=\dfrac{2/27}{7/54}=\dfrac47.$

questions.title: Dollar Break
questions.question: What's the maximum amount of money in coins you can have in your pocket AND not be able to make change for a dollar? $$$$ The coin denominations in the USA are pennies ($0.01$ dollars), nickels ($0.05$ dollars), dimes ($0.10$ dollars), and quarters ($0.25$ dollars).
questions.hint: What is the maximum amount of money in coins you can have in your pocket and not be able to make change for a dollar?
hints: You want the largest coin total with no subset summing to exactly 100¢.
Avoid being able to reach $1: 3 quarters + 4 dimes + 4 pennies.
questions.genSolution: With $3$ quarters ($75$), $4$ dimes ($40$), and $4$ pennies ($4$) you hold $\$1.19$, yet no subset sums to exactly $100$ cents (a $4$th quarter or proper dime combination is unavailable). Any more coins would enable change, so the maximum is $\$1.19.$

questions.title: Hidden Prisms
questions.question: You are presented with a clear $5$x$5$x$5$ cube. How many unique rectangular prisms can you see within the cube? Uniqueness does not include orientation. For example, there are $8$ unique $3$x$3$x$3$ cubes within a $4$x$4$x$4$ cube.
questions.hint: How can prisms be formed as intersections of the three axes?
hints: A sub-box is fixed by choosing 2 of the 6 grid planes along each axis.
Count = [C(6,2)]³ for a 5×5×5.
questions.genSolution: A rectangular prism is determined by choosing $2$ of the $6$ parallel cutting planes along each of the three axes: $\binom62=15$ per axis. So the count is $15^3=3375.$

questions.title: Particle Reach V
questions.question: Consider a particle that performs a random walk on the integers starting at position $0$. At each step, the particle moves from position $i$ to position $i+1$ with probability $p$, while the probability it moves from $i$ to $i-1$ is $1-p$. If $p = 1/3$, find the expected number of steps until the particle reaches $1$. If the answer is infinite, answer $-1$.
questions.hint: From Particle Reach I, we know that the probability that the particle ever reaches position $1$ is strictly less than $1$ for $p < 1/2$
hints: For p<1/2 the particle reaches +1 with probability <1 (here 1/2).
With positive probability it never arrives.
So the expected number of steps is infinite ⇒ −1.
questions.genSolution: For $p=\tfrac13<\tfrac12$, the walk reaches $+1$ only with probability $\tfrac12<1$; with the remaining probability it drifts to $-\infty$ and never arrives. Hence the expected number of steps is infinite, so the answer is $-1.$

questions.title: Binomial Pricing a Binary Call
questions.question: We want to find the fair-value of a European binary call on an underlying $S$ with initial value $S_0 = 4$. At each time step, the underlying can either grow by $90\%$ or shrink by $30\%$. At expiry ($T = 2$), the call will pay $1$ if $S > 2$ and $0$ otherwise. Round to $3$ decimal points.
questions.hint: Find the risk-neutral probabilities and iterate backwards.
hints: Find the risk-neutral up-probability from u=1.9, d=0.7.
Enumerate the 2-step terminal prices and which exceed 2.
Price = risk-neutral probability that S_T > 2 (discounted).

questions.title: Bowl Distributions
questions.question: How many ways can $7$ white balls and $8$ black balls be distributed into $5$ bowls such that each bowl receives exactly $3$ balls?
questions.hint: Consider the complementary event.
hints: Each bowl gets 3 balls; let w_i be its whites (0–3), with Σw_i=7 (then blacks are forced).
Count solutions of w₁+…+w₅=7 with 0≤w_i≤3.
questions.genSolution: Each bowl's white count $w_i\in\{0,1,2,3\}$ with $\sum w_i=7$ (blacks $3-w_i$ then sum to $8$). The number of capped solutions is $\binom{11}{4}-5\binom{7}{4}=330-175=155.$

questions.title: 1 Glove Off
questions.question: You have $5$ pairs of gloves that each have a distinct number $1-5$. The $10$ gloves are randomly paired up. Find the probability that the gloves are paired up such that the values of any pair differ by at most $1$.
questions.hint: Note that there are $\displaystyle \dfrac{(2n)!}{2^n \cdot n!}$ ways to pair up the $2n$ gloves, as there are $(2n)!$ total arrangements, $n!$ ways to re-label the pairs, and then $2$ ways to switch around the order in each pair. Let $g_n$ be the number of arrangements that satisfy our condition. Derive a recurrence relation for $g_n$ based on conditioning with one of the gloves labelled $n$.
hints: Total pairings of 10 gloves: 10!/(2⁵5!)=945. Count valid ones (partners differ by ≤1).
Condition on the two value-n gloves: pair them (f(n−1)) or match both to the two (n−1)'s (2·f(n−2)).
questions.genSolution: There are $\dfrac{10!}{2^5 5!}=945$ pairings. Let $f(n)$ count valid pairings of two copies each of $1..n$. The two $n$'s either pair together (leaving $f(n-1)$) or both pair with the two $(n-1)$'s in $2$ ways (leaving $f(n-2)$): $f(n)=f(n-1)+2f(n-2)$ with $f(1)=1,f(2)=3$. So $f(5)=21$ and $P=\tfrac{21}{945}=\tfrac1{45}.$

questions.title: Exponential Maximum Asymptotic
questions.question: Let $X_1,X_2,\dots$ be IID Exp$(1)$ random variables and $M_n = \text{max}\{X_1,\dots,X_n\}$. Define $p_n$ as $\mathbb{P}[M_n \leq \ln(n)]$. Evaluate $\displaystyle \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} p_n$. Given your answer should be in the form $\dfrac{b}{e}$, where $b$ is some constant and $e$ is Euler's constant, what is $b$?
questions.hint: If the maximum is at most $\ln(n)$, what does that tell usabout the value of each random variable? Recall the limit $\displaystyle \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} \left(1 + \dfrac{x}{n}\right)^n = e^x, \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$ .
hints: P(M_n ≤ ln n)=P(all X_i ≤ ln n)=(1−e^{−ln n})ⁿ.
questions.genSolution: $P(M_n\le\ln n)=\prod P(X_i\le\ln n)=(1-e^{-\ln n})^n=(1-\tfrac1n)^n\to e^{-1}=\tfrac1e$. So $b=1.$

questions.title: Uniform Triangle
questions.question: Let $X,Y,Z \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)$ IID. Find the probability that a triangle of side lengths $X,Y,$ and $Z$ can be formed.
questions.hint: For the triangle to not be valid, one of $X > Y + Z, Y >X + Z,$ or $Z > X + Y$ must hold. Note that these three events are also mutually disjoint. Why?
hints: A triangle fails iff one side exceeds the sum of the other two; the three failure events are disjoint.
Each has probability 1/6 by symmetry (e.g. P(X>Y+Z)=1/6).
questions.genSolution: No triangle forms iff one length exceeds the other two combined; these three events are disjoint and each has probability $\tfrac16$ (e.g. $P(X>Y+Z)=\tfrac16$). So $P(\text{triangle})=1-3\cdot\tfrac16=\tfrac12.$

questions.title: Bivariate Covariance
questions.question:
Let $(X,Y)$ follow a Bivariate Normal distribution with $X$ and $Y$ marginally standard normal and $\text{Corr}(X,Y) = \rho$. Compute 
 $\text{Cov}(X,Y^2)$.
questions.hint: If $Z_1, Z_2 \sim N(0,1)$ IID, we know that the joint transformation that would yield $(X,Y)$ is $X = Z_1$, $Y = \rho Z_1 + \sqrt{1-\rho^2}Z_2$.
hints: Write X=Z₁, Y=ρZ₁+√(1−ρ²)Z₂ with Z₁,Z₂ IID N(0,1).
Cov(X,Y²)=E[Z₁Y²]; expand Y² — all terms involve odd moments of Z₁ (or E[Z₂])=0.
Cov(X,Y²)=0.
questions.genSolution: With $X=Z_1$, $Y=\rho Z_1+\sqrt{1-\rho^2}Z_2$: $\mathbb{E}[XY^2]=\rho^2\mathbb{E}[Z_1^3]+2\rho\sqrt{1-\rho^2}\mathbb{E}[Z_1^2 Z_2]+(1-\rho^2)\mathbb{E}[Z_1 Z_2^2]=0$ (each term has $\mathbb{E}[Z_1^3]=0$ or $\mathbb{E}[Z_2]=0$). Since $\mathbb{E}[X]=0$, $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y^2)=0.$

questions.title: 3 Heads 3 Tails I
questions.question: Anna and Brenda are playing a game. They repeatedly toss a coin. Anna wins if 3 heads appear. Brenda wins if 3 tails appear. The heads and tails do not need to be consecutive. What is the expected number of coin tosses for a winner to be determined?
questions.hint: The probability that Anna wins is $\dfrac{1}{2}$ by symmetry. Further, the expected total number of tosses for Anna to win is equal to the expected total number of tosses for Brenda to win by symmetry. By the law of total expectation, our desired answer is simply double the expected total number of tosses for Anna to win.
hints: By symmetry the answer is twice the expected tosses for Anna (3 heads) to win.
Anna wins in 3,4,5 tosses with probabilities 1/8, 3/16, 6/32.
questions.siteSolution:
The probability that Anna wins is $\dfrac{1}{2}$ by symmetry. Further, the expected total number of tosses for Anna to win is equal to the expected total number of tosses for Brenda to win by symmetry. By the law of total expectation, our desired answer is simply double the expected total number of tosses for Anna to win. Anna wins in 3 total tosses with probability $\dfrac{1}{8}$. Anna wins in 4 total tosses with probability $\dfrac{3}{16}$ (there are 3 arrangements of 3 heads and 1 tail such that the last flip is heads). Anna wins in 5 total tosses with probability $\dfrac{6}{32}$ (there are 6 arrangements of 3 heads and 2 tails such that the last flip is tails). Putting it all together, we find the expected number of tosses for Anna to win is 
 $$ 
\dfrac{1}{8} \cdot 3 + \dfrac{3}{16} \cdot 4 + \dfrac{6}{32} \cdot 5 = \dfrac{66}{32}
 $$ 
 Multiplying by 2 gives us $\dfrac{33}{8}$.

questions.title: 30 Side Difference
questions.question: Let $X$ and $Y$ be the upfaces of two independent rolls of fair $30-$sided dice with values $1-30$ on the sides. Find $\mathbb{E}[|X-Y|]$.
questions.hint: Let $Z = |X-Y|$. Compute the PMF of $Z$ directly. If the absolute difference between the rolls is exactly $k$, then what must the two dice rolls look like? How can you use this to calculate the probability $P[Z=k]$?
hints: For two IID uniforms on 1..n, E[|X−Y|]=(n²−1)/(3n).
Plug n=30.
questions.genSolution: For two independent uniform rolls on $1,\dots,n$, $\mathbb{E}[|X-Y|]=\dfrac{n^2-1}{3n}$. With $n=30$ this is $\dfrac{899}{90}.$

questions.title: Red-Blue Die Match
questions.question: There are three fair $6-$sided dice in front of you. Two of the dice are blue, while one of the dice is red. Find the probability exactly one of the blue dice matches the red die in value.
questions.hint: Simplify the problem by setting the red die value to some random value $x \in (1-6)$
hints: Fix the red value; each blue matches it independently with probability 1/6.
Exactly one of two blues matches: C(2,1)(1/6)(5/6).
questions.genSolution: Given the red value, each blue die matches it with probability $\tfrac16$. Exactly one of the two blues matching is $\binom21\tfrac16\cdot\tfrac56=\tfrac{10}{36}=\tfrac{5}{18}.$

questions.title: Ramen Bowl
questions.question: There are 100 noodles in your bowl of ramen. You take the ends of two noodles uniformly at random and connect the two, putting the connected noodle back into the bowl and continuing until there are no ends left to connect. On average, how many circles will you create? Round to the nearest whole number.
questions.hint: How many circles do you create when there is only one noodle? Two noodles? $n$ noodles?
hints: With n noodles (2n ends), each join closes a loop with probability 1/(2k−1) when 2k ends remain.
questions.genSolution: When $2k$ ends remain, picking a first end, the chance its partner is its own other end (closing a loop) is $\tfrac1{2k-1}$. By linearity $\mathbb{E}[\text{circles}]=\sum_{k=1}^{100}\tfrac1{2k-1}\approx3.28$, which rounds to $3.$

questions.title: Option Dice I
questions.question: Pretend you have a simple options chain on the expected value of the product of two dice rolls. What would the call option at $30$ be priced at in this market?
questions.hint: When does our options contract make money?
hints: Underlying = product of two dice; call pays max(X−30,0).
Only X=36 (6×6) exceeds 30, paying 6.
