questions.genSolution: The call on the product pays $\max(X-30,0)$, nonzero only when $X=36$ (the roll $6,6$), paying $6$. So the price is $6\cdot\tfrac1{36}=\tfrac16.$

questions.title: Crossing Pairs
questions.question: Suppose $6$ points are chosen uniformly at random along the perimeter of the circle. The $6$ points are then divided into three pairs, and a chord is drawn between every pair of points. What is the probability none of the chords intersect?
questions.hint: Imagine we pick the pairs sequentially, and say we pick a random point of the six. Which of the remaining 5 points can we pair with the chosen one? Condition on the selected pair.
hints: Count non-crossing perfect matchings of 6 points vs all matchings.
Non-crossing matchings = Catalan C₃ = 5; total matchings = 5!! = 15.
questions.genSolution: The number of non-crossing perfect matchings of $6$ points on a circle is the Catalan number $C_3=5$, out of $5!!=15$ total matchings. So $P=\tfrac{5}{15}=\tfrac13.$

questions.title: Circular Delete
questions.question: On the circumference of a circle, the numbers $1,2,\dots, 2000$ are put down in clockwise increasing order. Starting at the integer $1$, you delete it, then you, move clockwise to $2$ and keep it. You are going to repeat alternating between deleting integers and keeping integers until you reach the last integer of a given rotation. Afterwards, you start again by deleting the first integer in the new cycle, keeping the second, etc. Find the last integer to be deleted from the circle.
questions.hint: At the first step, you are keeping all integers $n$ with $n \equiv 0 \hspace{3pt} \text{mod} \hspace{3pt} 2$. In the second step, can you form a similar function?
hints: After the first pass, the survivors are the even numbers; the pattern repeats on a halved set.
The last number deleted is the highest power of 2 within the range.
Largest power of 2 ≤ 2000 is 1024.
questions.genSolution: Each pass deletes every other number, so after the first pass only multiples of $2$ remain, then multiples of $4$, and so on. The number surviving longest (last deleted) is the largest power of $2$ in range, $2^{10}=1024.$

questions.title: The Perfect Hedge II
questions.question:
You have two assets. We will call them asset $1$ and asset $2$. Asset $1$ has an expected return of $4\%$ and a variance of $15\%$. Asset $2$ has an expected return of $2\%$ and a variance of $4\%$. They have a correlation $\rho = -1$. 


 $$ $$ 


We want to create a risk-free portfolio using assets $1$ and $2$. We will denote $w_1$ and $w_2$ as the weights of asset $1$ and $2$ in the portfolio respectively. What is the expected return of this portfolio? Round the answer to three significant figures.
questions.hint: What is the weighting needed to achieve a perfect hedge when we have a correlation of $\rho = -1$? What is the expected return of the portfolio?
hints: Use the hedge weights from ρ=−1: w₁=σ₂/(σ₁+σ₂)≈0.341, w₂≈0.659.
Portfolio return = 0.04w₁ + 0.02w₂.
questions.genSolution: The risk-free weights are $w_1=\dfrac{\sigma_2}{\sigma_1+\sigma_2}\approx0.341$, $w_2\approx0.659$. The portfolio's expected return is $0.04(0.341)+0.02(0.659)\approx0.0268$, i.e. $2.68\%.$

questions.title: Variance of Uniform
questions.question: Let $X \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)$. Find $\text{Var}(X)$.
questions.hint: We can quickly see that the mean of $X$ is $\dfrac{1}{2}$, as $X$ is uniformly distributed over the interval $(0,1)$, so the mean is right in the center of the interval due to the uniform distribution of probability density. Use LOTUS to compute $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$.
hints: E[X]=1/2; compute E[X²]=∫₀¹x²dx=1/3.
questions.genSolution: $\mathbb{E}[X]=\tfrac12$ and $\mathbb{E}[X^2]=\int_0^1x^2dx=\tfrac13$, so $\mathrm{Var}(X)=\tfrac13-\tfrac14=\tfrac1{12}.$

questions.title: 12-8 Showoff
questions.question: Suppose you have a fair $8-$sided die and roll it once. You can either keep the value showing on that die or roll a fair $12-$sided die once. You receive the payout of the last die you roll. Find your expected payout under optimal play.
questions.hint: What is the expected value of the $12-$sided die? Using this, when will we want to re-roll?
hints: The 12-sided die averages 6.5, so keep the d8 only if it beats 6.5 (a 7 or 8).
Keep 7,8 (prob 2/8); else reroll for 6.5.
questions.siteSolution:
The expected value of the $12-$sided die is $\dfrac{1+12}{2} = \dfrac{13}{2}$. Therefore, we would want to keep our original roll if it is a $7$ or $8$. Otherwise, we will roll the $12-$sided die. Therefore, our expected value is 
 $$ 
\dfrac{7+8}{8} + \dfrac{3}{4} \cdot \dfrac{13}{2} = \dfrac{27}{4}
 $$ 
 as we will roll the $12-$sided die with probability $\dfrac{3}{4}$.

questions.title: Silly SDE
questions.question:
Let $W_t$ be a standard Brownian Motion. Let $X_t$ be a process satisfying the SDE 
 $$ 
dX_t = \kappa(\theta-X_t)dt - \sigma\sqrt{X_t}dW_t
 $$ 
 with $X_0 = x > 0$. It can be shown (you do not need to do this) that if $\dfrac{2\kappa}{\theta} > \sigma^2$, $X_t > 0$ with probability $1$, so $\sqrt{X_t}$ is defined almost surely. For $T > 0$, $\mathbb{E}[X_T]$ can be written as a function of $x,\kappa,\theta,\sigma,$ and $T$. Evaluate this function when $T = 10, \kappa = 0.2, \theta = 2, x = 5,$ and $\sigma = 0.1$. The answer will be in the form $A + Be^C$ for integers $A,B,$ and $C$. Find $ABC$.
questions.hint: Calculate $d(e^{\kappa t}X_t)$ via Ito's Formula. Afterwards, express $e^{\kappa T}X_T - X_0$ as a sum of Lebesgue and Ito integrals. A "sufficiently nice" integrand inside an Ito integral has mean $0$.
hints: Apply Itô to e^{κt}X_t; the drift becomes κθe^{κt}dt and the noise is a mean-zero Itô integral.
Taking expectations: E[X_T]=θ+(x−θ)e^{−κT}.
questions.genSolution: By Itô, $d(e^{\kappa t}X_t)=e^{\kappa t}(\kappa\theta\,dt-\sigma\sqrt{X_t}\,dW_t)$. Taking expectations (the Itô integral has mean $0$): $e^{\kappa T}\mathbb{E}[X_T]-x=\theta(e^{\kappa T}-1)$, so $\mathbb{E}[X_T]=\theta+(x-\theta)e^{-\kappa T}$. With the given values, $2+3e^{-2}$, giving $A=2,B=3,C=-2$ and $ABC=-12.$

questions.title: Make a Market II
questions.question: Let's say you have product $A$ in which you are quoting $4 \text{ @ } 5$ and product $B$ in which you are quoting $10 \text{ @ } 12$. $X \text{ @ } Y$ means that $X$ is our bid and $Y$ is our ask. We want to make a market on the product $A - B$. What is the bid-ask spread you will quote? Give the answer in the format of $Y^2 - X^2$
questions.hint: What is the difference between the bid and ask?
hints: For A−B: your bid = bid_A − ask_B, your ask = ask_A − bid_B.

questions.title: Sequence Terminator
questions.question:
A fair $6-$sided die is rolled repetitively, forming a sequence of values, under the following rules: If any even value is rolled, add it to the current sequence. If a $3$ or $5$ is rolled, discard the entire sequence and don't add the $3$ or $5$ to the start of the new sequence. If a $1$ is rolled, add the $1$ to the current sequence and end the game. Find the expected length of the sequence at the end of the game.


 $$ 
 $$ 


For example if the sequence of rolls is $34265241$, we would reset to an empty sequence at the first roll, reset to an empty sequence at the $5$, and our final sequence would be $241$, which is of length $3$.
questions.hint:
An incorrect solution goes as follows: We will have no $3$s or $5$s appearing before the first $1$, as they reset the sequence and are not added. Therefore, our sample space is really $1,2,4,$ and $6$. On average, the $1$ will appear after $4$ throws of the die, so the answer is $4$.


 $$ 
 $$ 


This above solution is incorrect because it more implicitly assumes that we just "ignore" $3$s and $5$s that appear. Therefore, this is not the correct assumption we want to make. Instead, we can reframe this problem as follows: At some point, the last $3$ or $5$ will appear before the first $1$. As the die is memoryless (i.e. is not affected by what has already appeared), given that the first $1$ appears before both the first $3$ and first $5$, what is the expected number of rolls needed to obtain the $1$?
hints: The final length is a run of evens ending in a 1, conditioned on no 3 or 5 occurring in that run.
P(length=k, success)=(1/2)^{k−1}(1/6); P(success)=1/3.
questions.genSolution: The final sequence is the successful run: evens then a terminating $1$, with no $3$/$5$. $P(\text{length}=k,\text{success})=(\tfrac12)^{k-1}\tfrac16$ and $P(\text{success})=\tfrac13$. So $\mathbb{E}[\text{length}\mid\text{success}]=\dfrac{\tfrac16\sum_k k(\tfrac12)^{k-1}}{1/3}=\dfrac{(1/6)\cdot4}{1/3}=2.$

questions.title: Number Concatenate
questions.question: Write out the decimal expansions of $2^{1000}$ and $5^{1000}$ adjacent to one another. Concatenate them to form a new number, say $n$. Find the amount of digits in $n$.
questions.hint: Given an integer $m$, the number of digits in $m$ is $\lfloor \log_{10}(m) \rfloor + 1$.
hints: Digits(m)=⌊log₁₀ m⌋+1. Find digit counts of 2^1000 and 5^1000.
questions.genSolution: The digit count is $\lfloor\log_{10}m\rfloor+1$: $2^{1000}$ has $\lfloor1000\log_{10}2\rfloor+1=302$ digits and $5^{1000}$ has $699$. Concatenated, $n$ has $302+699=1001$ digits (matching $2^{1000}5^{1000}=10^{1000}$).

questions.title: Soccer Practice
questions.question: You're practicing soccer by taking $100$ penalty kicks. Assume that you have made the first goal but missed the second. For each of the following kicks, the probability that you score is the fraction of goals you've made thus far. For example, if you made 17 goals out of the first 30 attempts, then the probability that you make the 31st goal is $\frac{17}{30}$. After 100 attempts, including the first two, what is the probability that you score exactly $66$ penalty kicks?
questions.hint: Break down the problem. What is the probability of making exactly 1 successful kick? What is the probability of making exactly 2 successful kicks? What is the probability of making exactly $n$ successful kicks?
hints: This is a Pólya urn: starting 1 made, 1 missed, the success rule makes the made-count uniform.
The final number of goals is uniform over its 99 possible values (1 to 99).
P(exactly 66)=1/99.
questions.genSolution: Starting with one make and one miss, the rule 'score with probability = current fraction made' is a Pólya urn, under which the final number of goals is uniformly distributed over its possible values $1,\dots,99$. Hence $P(\text{exactly }66)=\tfrac1{99}.$

questions.title: Coin Pair III
questions.question: Four fair coins appear in front of you. You flip all four at once and observe the outcomes of the coins. After seeing the outcomes, you may flip any pair of tails again. You may not flip a single coin without flipping another. You can iterate this process as many times as there are at least two tails to flip. Find the expected number of heads that we end up with after this process is complete.
questions.hint: Think about the total number of heads and the amount that'll appear in the first flipping of coins
hints: Track expected final tails h_t; h_t=¼h_{t−2}+½h_{t−1}+¼h_t with h_0=0,h_1=1.
h_2=2/3, h_3=7/9, h_4=20/27; average over t~Binom(4,½): E[final tails]=20/27.
questions.genSolution: Let $h_t$ be expected final tails from $t$ tails: $h_t=\tfrac14h_{t-2}+\tfrac12h_{t-1}+\tfrac14h_t$, giving $h_2=\tfrac23,h_3=\tfrac79,h_4=\tfrac{20}{27}$. Averaging over $t\sim\mathrm{Binom}(4,\tfrac12)$ gives $\mathbb{E}[\text{final tails}]=\tfrac{20}{27}$, so $\mathbb{E}[\text{heads}]=4-\tfrac{20}{27}=\tfrac{88}{27}.$

questions.title: Modified Deck Pair
questions.question: Marissa has a deck of 40 cards: 4 1's, 4 2's, $\ldots$, 4 10's. A matching pair—2 cards with the same number—is removed from the deck without replacement. If Marissa randomly draws 2 more cards, what is the probability that she draws another matching pair?
questions.hint: You can get a matching pair by getting one of the ranks that didn't have a pair removed or by selecting the rank that did have the pair removed.
hints: After removing a pair: one rank has 2 cards, nine ranks have 4 each (38 total).
Favorable pairs: 1 (depleted rank) + 9·C(4,2)=54.
questions.genSolution: After removing a matched pair, the deck has $38$ cards: one rank with $2$, nine ranks with $4$. The number of matching-pair draws is $\binom22+9\binom42=1+54=55$ out of $\binom{38}{2}=703$, so $P=\tfrac{55}{703}.$

questions.title: Least Multiple of 15
questions.question: What is the least positive multiple of 15 whose digits consist of 1's and 0's only?
questions.hint: In order for a number to be a multiple of 15, it must be perfectly divisible by both 3 and 5. How can you use 1's and 0's to build a number that is perfectly divisible by both?
hints: Multiple of 15 ⇒ divisible by 5 (ends in 0) and by 3 (digit sum divisible by 3).
Digits are 0/1, so the digit sum (number of 1s) must be a multiple of 3 — at least three 1s.
Smallest: 1110.
questions.genSolution: It must end in $0$ (divisible by $5$) and have digit sum divisible by $3$; with only $1$s and $0$s that means at least three $1$s. The smallest such number is $1110.$

questions.title: Precise N Sum
questions.question: Suppose $N > 0$ is an integer. Let $X_1,X_2,X_3$ be independently selected uniformly at random from the set $\{0,1,\dots,N\}$. Let $p_N = \mathbb{P}[X_1 + X_2 + X_3 = N]$. Find $\displaystyle \lim_{N \rightarrow \infty} Np_N$. If this limit does not exist, enter $-1$.
questions.hint: For $X_1+X_2+X_3 = N$ to occur, we would need $X_1 + X_2 \leq N$ and $X_3 = N - X_1 - X_2$ to occur simultaneously. Condition on the first event happening to compute the probability the second event happens.
hints: Count solutions of X₁+X₂+X₃=N with each in {0,…,N}: C(N+2,2) (caps don't bind).
questions.genSolution: The number of ways to write $X_1+X_2+X_3=N$ with each in $\{0,\dots,N\}$ is $\binom{N+2}{2}$, so $p_N=\dfrac{\binom{N+2}{2}}{(N+1)^3}=\dfrac{N+2}{2(N+1)^2}$. Then $Np_N\to\dfrac12.$

questions.title: Colorful Line
questions.question: How many distinct ways can you arrange $3$ red balls, $7$ blue balls, and $9$ green balls in a line?
questions.hint: Think about this in terms of anagrams, where every arrange corresponds to a string of red, green, and blue.
hints: Arrangements of a multiset = 19!/(3!·7!·9!).
Compute the multinomial coefficient.
questions.genSolution: The number of distinct arrangements is the multinomial $\dfrac{19!}{3!\,7!\,9!}=11085360.$

questions.title: Multinomial Sum
questions.question: Calculate the sum of all multinomial coefficients $\displaystyle \binom{7}{b_1,b_2,b_3,b_4}$, where $b_1 + \dots + b_4 = 7$ and every $b_i \geq 0$ is an integer.
questions.hint: Interpret this combinatorially, ask yourself, what is this counting?
hints: Σ over compositions of n into k parts of the multinomial = k^n (assign each of n items to one of k bins).
Here k=4, n=7.
questions.genSolution: Summing all multinomials $\binom{7}{b_1,b_2,b_3,b_4}$ counts all ways to place $7$ distinct items into $4$ boxes, which is $4^7=16384.$

questions.title: Egg Drop I
questions.question: You have two identical eggs in a 100-story building, you don't know the floor the eggs will break on. If an egg is dropped at an elevation under story $X$, then the egg will survive; else, the egg breaks. What is the minimum number of drops required to determine $X$ in the worst-case scenario?
questions.hint: Tip #1: You can't use binary search here, remember you only have 2 eggs.  $$$$ Tip #2: Assume we design a strategy with $N$ maximum drops. If the first egg is dropped once, then the second egg can cover $N-1$ floors. If the first egg is dropped twice, then the second egg can cover $N-2$ floors. $$$$
hints: With a max of N drops, the first egg's drops cover N + (N−1) + … floors.
Smallest such N is 14.
questions.siteSolution: Suppose that we have a strategy with a maximum number of $N$ drops. For the first drop of the first egg, we can try the $N$-th floor. If the egg breaks, we can start to try the second egg from the first floor and increase the floor number by one until the second egg breaks. At most, there are $N-1$ floors to tests. $$$$ If the first egg dropped from the $N$-th floor does not break, then we have $N-1$ drops left.  $$$$ This time we can only increase the floor number by $(N-1)$ for the first egg since the second egg can only cover $(N-2)$ floors if the first egg breaks. If the egg dropped from the $(2N-1)$th floor does not break, then we have $N-2$ drops left. Therefore, we can only increase the floor number by $N-2$ for the first egg since the second egg can only cover $N-3$ floors if the first egg breaks.  $$$$ From this we can see a pattern emerges on the number of floors we can cover with the two eggs, the number of floors that these two eggs can cover with a maximum of N drops is: $$ N+(N-1) + ... 1 \geq 100  $$$$  \frac{N(N+1)}{2} \geq 100 $$$$ N = 14 $$  $$$$ So the minimum number of drops in the worst-case scenrio for finding floor $X$ is $14$ drops. $$$$

questions.title: Upface Correlation
questions.question: Suppose that you roll a standard fair $6-$sided die $n$ times. Let $X$ be the random variable representing the number of times you roll a $1$ and $Y$ be the random variable representing the number of times you roll a $5$. Calculate $\text{Corr}(X,Y)$.
questions.hint: Consider $\text{Var}(X+Y)$ for a quick solution. Otherwise, use indicators.
hints: X,Y are multinomial counts; Cov(X,Y)=−n p₁p₅, Var(X)=n p(1−p).
Cov=−n/36, Var=5n/36.
questions.genSolution: For multinomial counts, $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y)=-n p_1 p_5=-\tfrac{n}{36}$ and $\mathrm{Var}(X)=\mathrm{Var}(Y)=n\cdot\tfrac16\cdot\tfrac56=\tfrac{5n}{36}$. So $\mathrm{Corr}(X,Y)=\dfrac{-n/36}{5n/36}=-\dfrac15.$

questions.title: Square Billy
questions.question: Billy is walking on a cartesian grid start at $(1, 2)$, she begins a 2D symmetric random walk.  For making a decision on where to go, Billy has a $\frac{1}{4}$ chance each of going up, down, left, or right. There is a square formed with vertices $(0, 0), (0, 4), (4, 4), (4, 0)$. Billy ends his random walk when he hits the square. What is the probability that Billy ends up on a vertical side of the square?
questions.hint: Find the positions where the probabilities that he ends up on a horizontal or vertical side should equal one another.
hints: P(exit on a vertical side) is harmonic: average of neighbours, =1 on x=0,4 and 0 on y=0,4.
Use the symmetries x↔4−x and y↔4−y to reduce to a few unknowns.
Solving gives the center =1/2 and u(1,2)=5/8.
questions.genSolution: Let $u(x,y)=P(\text{exit on a vertical side})$, harmonic with $u=1$ on $x\in\{0,4\}$ and $0$ on $y\in\{0,4\}$. Using the reflection symmetries, the interior unknowns reduce to a small system whose solution gives $u(2,2)=\tfrac12$ and $u(1,2)=\tfrac58.$

questions.title: Smallest Probability
questions.question: Assume that we want to simulate an event $X$ with $\mathbb{P}[X] = p$. We only have two rolls of a fair die and 1 flip of a fair coin. Find the smallest value of $p$ for which we can simulate $X$.
questions.hint: Think about the sizes of the sample spaces.
hints: The combined experiment has 6·6·2 equally likely outcomes.
The smallest nonzero probability is a single outcome.
questions.genSolution: Two die rolls and a coin flip give $6\cdot6\cdot2=72$ equally likely outcomes, so the smallest simulable nonzero probability is $\tfrac1{72}.$

questions.title: Chess Tournament II
questions.question: A chess tournament has 128 players, each with a distinct rating. Assume that the player with the higher rating always wins against a lower rated opponent and that the winner proceeds to the subsequent round. Since the tournament's structure resembles that of a knockout bracket, 7 total rounds are played, including the final. What is the probability that the highest rated and the third-highest rated players will meet in the final?
questions.hint: Divide the knockout tournament into two subgroups of size $64$ each. The top 2 players must be in the same group while the 3rd highest player must be in the opposite group.
hints: #1 always reaches the final; #3 reaches it iff #1 and #2 are both in the other half.
P(#3 in the half opposite #1)=64/127; then P(#2 also in #1's half)=63/126=1/2.
questions.genSolution: #1 always reaches the final. For #3 to reach it, #3 must be the strongest in its half — i.e. #1 and #2 are both in the other half. $P(\#3\text{ opposite }\#1)=\tfrac{64}{127}$, and then $P(\#2\text{ in }\#1\text{'s half})=\tfrac{63}{126}=\tfrac12$. So $P=\tfrac{64}{127}\cdot\tfrac12=\tfrac{32}{127}.$

questions.title: Make Your Martingale III
questions.question: Let $X_1,X_2,\dots \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$ IID. Define the process $\{M_n\}_{n \geq 0}$ by $M_0 = 1$ and $M_n = M_{n-1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}e^{\frac{\lambda}{2}X_n}$ with the natural filtration $\mathcal{F}_n = \sigma(X_1,\dots,X_n)$. If $0 < a < p < b$ for some real values $a$ and $b$, then $\{M_n^p\}_{n \geq 0}$ is a sub-martingale. Find $a + b$.
questions.hint: Compute $\mathbb{E}[M_{n+1}^p \mid M_n^p]$ and find the range where the coefficient is larger than $1$.
hints: Submartingale ⟺ E[((1/2)e^{(λ/2)X})^p] ≥ 1, with X~Exp(λ).
This equals 2^{1−p}/(2−p) ≥ 1, i.e. 2^{1−p} ≥ 2−p (and p<2 for convergence).
Holds for p∈[1,2): a=1, b=2, a+b=3.
questions.genSolution: $M_n^p$ is a submartingale iff $\mathbb{E}\big[(\tfrac12 e^{\frac{\lambda}2 X})^p\big]\ge1$. With $X\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda)$, this is $(\tfrac12)^p\cdot\dfrac{2}{2-p}=\dfrac{2^{1-p}}{2-p}\ge1$, i.e. $2^{1-p}\ge2-p$, which holds for $p\in(1,2)$ (needing $p<2$ to converge). So $a=1,b=2$ and $a+b=3.$

questions.title: Power Grid
questions.question: There is a $3 \times 3$ grid of light bulbs, where each light bulbs are turned on with probability $\dfrac{1}{2}$. Find the probability that no two adjacent (grid cells that share a common side) light bulbs are powered on.
questions.hint: Start break up the problem into cases based on whether or not the middle light bulb is on or off.
hints: Count independent sets of the 3×3 grid graph (no two on-bulbs adjacent).
There are 63 such configurations.
questions.genSolution: The valid patterns are exactly the independent sets of the $3\times3$ grid graph, of which there are $63$. Each of the $2^9$ configurations is equally likely, so $P=\dfrac{63}{512}.$

questions.title: Lowest Target
questions.question: An archer is shooting at targets. There are 4 columns of identical targets, each with $2,3,3,$ and $4$ targets stacked vertically, respectively. The archer first selects a column and then shoots the lowest-hanging target in that column that is not broken. Once a column is out of targets, it is no longer able to be selected. In how many ways can the archer break all the targets?
questions.hint: There are $12$ total targets and $4$ columns. Label the columns $1-4$. How can you identify each unique breaking pattern?
hints: Each breaking order is an interleaving of the 4 columns' forced bottom-up sequences.
questions.genSolution: Within a column the order is forced, so a full breaking sequence is an interleaving of the columns: $\dfrac{12!}{2!\,3!\,3!\,4!}=277200.$

questions.title: Square Cross
questions.question: A square of side length $20$ is formed in front of you. You select a point uniformly at random from the interior of the square. You then proceed to form a circle of radius $R \sim \text{Unif}(0,10)$, independent of the point selected. Find the probability that the circle you create does not intersect the square at any point.
questions.hint: Condition on the radius $R = r$. What is the region the center would need to lie in?
hints: The circle stays inside iff the center is ≥r from each side: a (20−2r)² region.
P(inside | R=r)=(20−2r)²/400; average over r~U(0,10).
questions.genSolution: Given radius $r$, the circle avoids the square's sides iff its center lies in the inner $(20-2r)^2$ region, probability $\tfrac{(20-2r)^2}{400}$. Averaging over $R\sim U(0,10)$: $\tfrac1{10}\int_0^{10}\tfrac{(20-2r)^2}{400}dr=\tfrac1{4000}\cdot\tfrac{4000}{3}=\tfrac13.$

questions.title: Option Arbitrage
questions.hint: For this to be an arbitrage, there must be a $0$ probability that we lose money. In other words, our constructed portfolio must have a non-negative payoff everywhere $\textbf{and}$ we must pay at most $0$ to enter the position.
hints: Call prices must be convex in strike; check C(1000)−2C(1010)+C(1020).
= 4 − 7 + 2.75 = −0.25 < 0, so a long butterfly costs −0.25 (a credit) with payoff ≥ 0.
Guaranteed profit = 0.25.
questions.genSolution: Call prices should be convex in strike, but $C(1000)-2C(1010)+C(1020)=4-7+2.75=-0.25<0$. Buying that butterfly (long the $1000$ and $1020$, short two $1010$s) gives a $0.25$ credit with a payoff that is always $\ge0$, so you are guaranteed $\$0.25.$

questions.title: Matching Socks II
questions.question: 6 distinct pairs of socks are in a drawer. You're in a hurry to pack for a trip, so you draw out 8 socks uniformly at random from the drawer. Find the expected number of pairs of socks that remain in the drawer.
questions.hint: Set up indicators representing the events that each pair of socks is in the drawer after drawing out $8$ socks and apply linearity of expectation.
hints: A pair remains iff both its socks are among the 4 left; use an indicator per pair.
P(both of a pair among the remaining 4)=C(10,2)/C(12,4)=1/11.
questions.genSolution: After drawing $8$, four socks remain. A given pair survives iff both its socks are among those $4$: $\dfrac{\binom{10}{2}}{\binom{12}{4}}=\dfrac{45}{495}=\dfrac1{11}$. By linearity over $6$ pairs, $\mathbb{E}=\dfrac{6}{11}.$

questions.title: Matching Jar Colors
questions.question: You have $2$ jars in front of you. Jar $A$ has $3$ blue and $4$ red balls. Jar $B$ has $6$ blue and $4$ red balls. If one ball is selected uniformly at random from each jar, what is the probability that the two selected balls match in color?
questions.hint: We have a success if both balls are red or both balls are blue.
hints: Match = both blue or both red; jars independent.
questions.genSolution: $P(\text{match})=P(\text{both blue})+P(\text{both red})=\tfrac37\cdot\tfrac6{10}+\tfrac47\cdot\tfrac4{10}=\tfrac{18+16}{70}=\tfrac{17}{35}.$

questions.title: Flip Again
questions.question: 4 fair coins are laid out in front of you and are flipped. You receive the amount of dollars equal to the number of heads that appear. However, you also have the option to re-flip all 4 coins at once one time for $\$1$. If you re-flip whenever you get $0$ or $1$ heads on the initial flip, what is the fair value of this game?
questions.hint: 4 fair coins are laid out in front of you and are flipped. You receive the amount of dollars equal to the number of heads that appear. However, you also have the option to re-flip all 4 coins for $\$1$. If you re-flip whenever you get $0$ or $1$ heads on the initial flip, what is the fair value of this game?
hints: Keep H heads if H≥2; reflip (net value 2−1=1) if H≤1.
E=Σ_{H≥2}H·P(H) + P(H≤1)·1, with H~Binom(4,½).
questions.genSolution: With $H\sim\mathrm{Binom}(4,\tfrac12)$: keep $H$ if $H\ge2$, else reflip for net $2-1=1$. So $\mathbb{E}=\big(2\cdot\tfrac6{16}+3\cdot\tfrac4{16}+4\cdot\tfrac1{16}\big)+\tfrac5{16}\cdot1=\tfrac{28}{16}+\tfrac5{16}=\tfrac{33}{16}.$

questions.title: Head-Tail Product
questions.hint: If $X$ heads are flipped out of a total of $n$ coin tosses, then $Y = n - X$ tails are flipped.
hints: Y=n−X, so E[XY]=nE[X]−E[X²] with X~Binom(n,½).
questions.siteSolution:
If $X$ heads are flipped out of a total of $n$ coin tosses, then $Y = n - X$ tails are flipped. Let's substitute this expression in for $Y$, simplify, and apply the linearity of expectation. 
\[\begin{aligned}
    \mathbb{E}[XY] &= \mathbb{E}[X(n - X)] \\
    &= n \cdot \mathbb{E}[X] - \mathbb{E}[X^2]
\end{aligned}\]
Note that $X \sim \text{Binom}(n, 0.5)$. Recall that for some $Z \sim \text{Binom}(n, p)$, $\mathbb{E}[Z] = np$ and $\text{Var}(Z) = npq$, where $q = 1 - p$. So, 
\[\begin{aligned}
    \mathbb{E}[X] &= \frac{n}{2} \\
    \text{Var}[X] &= \frac{n}{4}
\end{aligned}\]
We can compute $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$ as follows:
\[\begin{aligned}
    \text{Var}[X] &= \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2 \\
    &= \frac{n}{4} \\
    \Rightarrow \mathbb{E}[X^2] &= \frac{n}{4} + \left( \frac{n}{2} \right)^2 \\
\end{aligned}\]
Returning to our original expression of interest,
\[\begin{aligned}
    \mathbb{E}[XY] &= \mathbb{E}[X(n - X)] \\
    &= n \cdot \mathbb{E}[X] - \mathbb{E}[X^2] \\
    &= \frac{n^2}{2} - \frac{n^2}{4} - \frac{n}{4} \\
    &= \frac{n^2 - n}{4}.
\end{aligned}\]
Our solution is therefore $1 - 1 + 4 = 4$.

questions.title: Picky Primes
questions.question: 4 distinct integers are drawn from the set of the first $16$ positive prime integers. Find the probability that the sum is even.
questions.hint: All primes besides $2$ are odd.
hints: Only the prime 2 is even; a sum of four primes is even iff 2 is NOT among them (then all four are odd).
P(2 not chosen)=C(15,4)/C(16,4).
questions.genSolution: Among the first $16$ primes only $2$ is even. The sum of four chosen primes is even iff $2$ is excluded (leaving four odds). So $P=\dfrac{\binom{15}{4}}{\binom{16}{4}}=\dfrac{12}{16}=\dfrac34.$

questions.title: Fibonacci Sum
questions.question: Evaluate $\displaystyle \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{F_k}{10^{k+1}}$, where $F_k$ is the $k$th Fibonacci number defined by $F_0 = 0$, $F_1 = 1$, and $F_{k} = F_{k-1} + F_{k-2}$ for $k \geq 2$.
questions.hint:
Define $S$ as the sum in equation. We can expand out the first few terms of the sum as 
 $$ 
\displaystyle \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{F_k}{10^{k+1}} = \dfrac{F_0}{10} + \dfrac{F_1}{10^2} + \dfrac{F_2}{10^3} + \dfrac{F_3}{10^4} + \dots = \dfrac{F_0}{10} + \dfrac{F_1}{10^2} + \left(\dfrac{F_0}{10^3} + \dfrac{F_1}{10^3}\right) + \left(\dfrac{F_1}{10^4} + \dfrac{F_2}{10^4}\right) + \dots
 $$
hints: Use the Fibonacci generating function Σ F_k x^k = x/(1−x−x²).
The sum is (1/10)·ΣF_k(1/10)^k with x=1/10.
questions.genSolution: Since $\sum_{k\ge0}F_k x^k=\dfrac{x}{1-x-x^2}$, the sum is $\tfrac1{10}\sum F_k(\tfrac1{10})^k=\tfrac1{10}\cdot\dfrac{1/10}{1-\tfrac1{10}-\tfrac1{100}}=\tfrac1{10}\cdot\dfrac{1/10}{89/100}=\dfrac1{89}.$

questions.title: Minimum Variance Portfolio
questions.question: Suppose you purchase a $1$ share of stock $A$. A share of stock $A$ has mean return $2$ and return variation $4$. You also want to purchase stock $B$. $1$ share of $B$ has mean return $3$ and return variation $9$. Additionally, the returns of $A$ and $B$ are correlated with coefficient $-1/2$. How many shares of stock $B$ should you purchase so that the variance of your portfolio consisting of both $A$ and $B$ is minimum?
questions.hint: Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be the returns of stock $A$ and $B$, respectively. We have that if you purchase $k$ shares of stock $B$, then the return of your portfolio is given by $R_A + kR_B$. Minimize the variance of this as a function of $k$.
hints: Var(R_A+kR_B)=Var_A+k²Var_B+2k·Cov, with Cov=ρσ_Aσ_B.
= 4+9k²−6k; minimize over k.
questions.genSolution: With $\mathrm{Cov}=(-\tfrac12)(2)(3)=-3$, the portfolio variance is $4+9k^2-6k$. Setting the derivative $18k-6=0$ gives $k=\tfrac13$ shares of $B$.

questions.title: Poisson Review V
questions.question: Gabe makes rugs. The number of imperfections follows a Poisson distribution with an average of 4 per square yard. It costs $10$ to repair each imperfection. Find the sum of the mean and variance of the repair cost for a 10-square-yard rug.
questions.hint: What is the distribution of imperfections per $10$ square yards?
hints: Imperfections in 10 sq yd ~ Poisson(40); cost = 10·N.
questions.genSolution: Imperfections over $10$ square yards are $\mathrm{Poisson}(40)$, and cost $=10N$. So mean $=10\cdot40=400$ and variance $=10^2\cdot40=4000$, summing to $4400.$

questions.title: Put Theta
questions.question: The underlying is currently at price $S_0 = 35$. You buy a put at strike $K = 33$. What will happen to theta if the price of the underlying increases? Answer $-1$ if theta decreases, $0$ if theta stays the same, and $1$ if it increases.
questions.hint: What is the sign of theta when we long an option? Where is theta maximized?
hints: The put is out-of-the-money (S=35>K=33); long-option theta is negative and most negative near ATM.
Raising S moves it further OTM, so |theta| shrinks (theta becomes less negative).
Theta increases ⇒ 1.
questions.genSolution: The put is out-of-the-money and a long option's theta is negative, largest in magnitude near the money. Increasing $S$ pushes the put further OTM, shrinking $|\theta|$ so theta rises toward $0$: the answer is $1.$

questions.title: Bus Wait I
questions.question: You are anticipating the arrival of a bus to reach an interview on time! The bus operates on a consistent schedule, arriving every $10$ minutes. If you show up at a randomly chosen time during the day, what is the expected duration until the next bus arrives (in minutes)?
questions.hint: Consider the distribution of the time until the next bus?
hints: Arriving at a uniform time, the wait to the next bus is Uniform(0,10).
Its mean is the midpoint.
= 5 minutes.
questions.genSolution: A uniformly random arrival makes the wait $\mathrm{Unif}(0,10)$, with mean $\tfrac{0+10}{2}=5$ minutes.

questions.title: Zero Volatility Returns
questions.question: Suppose that $dS_t = \mu S_t dt + \sigma S_t \sqrt{dt}$ with $\sigma = 0$ and $\mu = 1$. Find $\dfrac{S_2}{S_0}$. The answer is in the form $e^a$ for some $a$. Find $a$.
questions.hint: With $\sigma = 0$, $\dfrac{dS_t}{S_t}$ is now non-random.
hints: With σ=0 the SDE is the ODE dS/S = μ dt.
questions.genSolution: With $\sigma=0$, $\dfrac{dS_t}{S_t}=\mu\,dt$ gives $S_t=S_0e^{\mu t}$, so $\dfrac{S_2}{S_0}=e^{2\mu}=e^{2}$ and $a=2.$

questions.title: The Big Three
questions.question: Suppose we roll 5 standard fair dice and sum the upfaces of the largest 3 values showing. Find the probability that the sum is $18$.
questions.hint: What needs to happen to get a sum of $18$ from three standard dice?
hints: The top three sum to 18 only if all three are 6s, i.e. at least three 6s among the 5 dice.
questions.genSolution: A top-three sum of $18$ requires three $6$s, i.e. at least three $6$s among the five dice: $\sum_{k=3}^{5}\binom5k(\tfrac16)^k(\tfrac56)^{5-k}=\dfrac{250+25+1}{7776}=\dfrac{276}{7776}=\dfrac{23}{648}.$

questions.title: 1 Head Up
questions.question:
$ 
3$ fair coins are flipped, and you are told that at least one of the coins showed heads. Find the probability exactly one coin showed heads.
questions.hint: Of the $2^3 = 8$ possible outcomes, all but $TTT$ have at least one head. How many have exactly one head?
hints: Of 8 outcomes, 7 have at least one head.
Exactly one head: HTT, THT, TTH — 3 outcomes.
questions.genSolution: Conditioning on at least one head leaves $7$ equally likely outcomes; exactly one head occurs in $3$ of them, so $P=\tfrac37.$

questions.title: Exact 5 II
questions.question: Abd continually rolls a fair $6-$sided die until he obtains his first $6$. Compute the expected number of times Abd obtains the value $5$ before he stops rolling.
questions.hint:
Let $N$ be the amount of trials needed to obtain the first $6$ and $T$ be the number of times that $5$ appears in total. By Law of Total Expectation, 
 $$ 
\mathbb{E}[T] = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[T \mid N]]
 $$
hints: Only 5s and 6s matter; before the first 6 they are equally likely.
The number of 5s before the first 6 is geometric with mean P(5)/P(6).
questions.genSolution: Ignoring other faces, each relevant roll is a $5$ or $6$ equally likely. The number of $5$s before the first $6$ has mean $\dfrac{P(5)}{P(6)}=\dfrac{1/6}{1/6}=1.$

questions.title: Alternating Sum
questions.question: Find the value of $100^2 - 99^2 + 98^2 - 97^2 + \dots + 2^2 - 1^2$
questions.hint: Use difference of squares on consecutive terms
hints: Pair consecutive terms and use a²−b²=(a+b)(a−b).
Each pair (2k)²−(2k−1)² = 4k−1; or each n²−(n−1)²=2n−1.
Sum of all integers 1..100 = 5050.
questions.genSolution: Each pair $n^2-(n-1)^2=2n-1$, so the expression telescopes to $\sum_{n=1}^{100}n=\dfrac{100\cdot101}{2}=5050.$

questions.title: Cone Combo
questions.question: A point is uniformly at random selected from a cone with radius $1$ and height $2$. Find the probability that the point is at most a height $1$ away from the circular base.
questions.hint: Consider the complement of this event. What does that region look like?
hints: Complement: the point is in the top portion (height >1), itself a small cone.
Top cone has radius 1/2, height 1: volume (1/3)π(1/2)²·1 = π/12, vs full 2π/3.
questions.siteSolution:
From geometry, we know that the volume of a cone with height $h$ and base radius $r$ is $\dfrac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$. Therefore, as $r = 1$ and $h = 2$ here, the volume of this cone is $\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$. We also know that the radius decreases linearly with height from the bottom. In other words, we know that the radius as a function of the height $x$ from the base, say $r(x)$, is linear. This means it is in the form $r(x) = c_0 + c_1 x$. Since $r(0) = 1$ (we are at the base) and $r(2) = 0$ (we are at the tip), we get that $r(x) = 1 - \dfrac{x}{2}$ by solving the resultant linear system. 


 $$ 
 $$ 


Consider the complement of our event, which is that we are above a height $1$ from the base. This region is another conic region. However, the base radius is now $1 - \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ and our height is $1$, so the volume of the conic region that is complementary to our region of interest is $\dfrac{1}{3} \cdot \pi \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 \cdot 1 = \dfrac{\pi}{12}$. Therefore, as our selection of the point is uniform throughout the volume of the cone, the probability that we select a point above height $1$ from the base is $\dfrac{\frac{1}{12}}{\frac{2}{3}} = \dfrac{1}{8}$. Therefore, the probability of the event we are interested in is $\dfrac{7}{8}$.

questions.title: Positively Normal
questions.question: Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ are two IID standard normal random variables. Compute $\mathbb{P}\left[Y > \sqrt{3}X \mid Y > 0\right]$.
questions.hint: The key idea here is that $(X,Y)$ is radially symmetric about the origin. In other words, the density at each point of the circumference of the circle of radius $r > 0$ centered at the origin is constant.
hints: (X,Y) is radially symmetric, so the angle is uniform.
Y>0 is the upper half (180°); Y>√3X within it is the arc from 60° to 180° (120°).
questions.genSolution: By radial symmetry the direction angle is uniform. The event $Y>0$ is the upper half-plane ($180^\circ$), and within it $Y>\sqrt3X$ corresponds to angles from $60^\circ$ to $180^\circ$ ($120^\circ$). So the conditional probability is $\tfrac{120}{180}=\tfrac23.$

questions.title: Lognormal II
questions.question: Suppose that $\ln(X) \sim N(0,1)$. Find $\text{Var}(X^4)$. Your answer will be in the form of $e^a - e^b$ for positive integers $a$ and $b$. Find $a + b$.
questions.hint: Let $Z \sim N(0,1)$. Then we know that $X = e^Z$, so $\text{Var}(X^4) = \mathbb{E}[X^8] - (\mathbb{E}[X^4])^2$ by the definition of variance. Plug in the expression for $X$ above and note that it looks like the MGF of $Z$ with some certain values plugged in.
hints: X=e^Z, so Var(X⁴)=E[e^{8Z}]−(E[e^{4Z}])²; use the normal MGF E[e^{tZ}]=e^{t²/2}.
Var=e³²−e¹⁶ ⇒ a+b=48.

questions.title: Sheep Sharing
questions.question: An Australian farmer dies and leaves his sheep to his three sons. Alfred is to get $20$ percent more than John, and $25$ percent more than Charles. John's share is $3600$ sheep. How many sheep does Charles get?
questions.hint: Create a system of equations
hints: Alfred = 1.2·John and Alfred = 1.25·Charles.
questions.genSolution: Alfred gets $1.2\times3600=4320$. Since Alfred is $25\%$ more than Charles, $\text{Charles}=\dfrac{4320}{1.25}=3456.$

questions.title: Team Winners
questions.question: A tournament consistent of $32$ teams plays in a round-robin style tournament. This means that every single team plays every other team exactly one time. Suppose that every team is equally skilled so that every team is equally likely to beat any other team in a given match. Each team keeps track of how many wins they have. Find the probability that every team has won a distinct number of games at the end of the tournament. The answer will be in the form $\dfrac{a!}{2^b}$ for integers $a$ and $b$, where $b$ is maximal. Find $a + b$.
questions.hint: How many ways are there to assign win counts to the teams? How many total tournament outcomes are there.
hints: Distinct win counts must be 0,1,…,31 (sum 496 = total games).
Such outcomes are the transitive tournaments: 32! of them, out of 2^{C(32,2)}=2^{496}.
questions.genSolution: Distinct win totals must be exactly $0,1,\dots,31$, which forces a transitive ranking — there are $32!$ such outcomes out of $2^{\binom{32}{2}}=2^{496}$. So $P=\dfrac{32!}{2^{496}}$, giving $a+b=32+496=528.$

questions.title: 2 In A Row
questions.question: You are making a biased coin, where you can decide the probability $p$ of obtaining a heads. What value of $p$ maximizes the probability that you obtain exactly two consecutive heads in $4$ flips?  $$$$ For example, $HHHT$ and $HHHH$ are not allowed, but $HHTH$ is allowed. Your answer will be in the form $\dfrac{a-\sqrt{b}}{c}$ for integers $a,b,$ and $c$ with $c$ minimal. Find $a + b + c$.
questions.hint: Consider all the cases where you would have exactly $2$ consecutive heads in $4$ flips, write the probabilites as a functio nfo $p$. Then maximize their probabilty of occurance (with calculus), finding $p$.
hints: List the 4-flip sequences with a maximal H-run of exactly 2: HHTT,THHT,TTHH (2 heads) and HHTH,HTHH (3 heads).
P(p)=3p²(1−p)²+2p³(1−p)=p²(1−p)(3−p); maximize.
questions.genSolution: The qualifying sequences are $HHTT,THHT,TTHH$ (two heads) and $HHTH,HTHH$ (three heads), so $P(p)=3p^2(1-p)^2+2p^3(1-p)=p^2(1-p)(3-p)$. Setting $P'(p)=0$ gives $2p^2-6p+3=0$, so $p=\dfrac{3-\sqrt3}{2}$ and $a+b+c=8.$

questions.title: No Rock
questions.question: You play rock, paper, scissors with an opponent, but your opponent cannot play rock. Every time you win, you receive $\$1$, every time you lose, you lose $\$1$, and every time you draw, nothing happens. Assuming optimal play, what is your expected profit per round?
questions.hint: Note that there is no point in playing paper, as paper doesn't defeat scissors nor paper. Therefore, suppose that you play rock with probability $a$ and scissors with probability $1-a$. Furthermore, suppose that your opponent plays paper with probability $b$ and scissors with probability $1-b$. Write a function $P(a,b)$ that represents your payout as a function of $a$ and $b$. Find the equilibrium.
hints: You never play paper; play rock w.p. a, scissors w.p. 1−a. Opponent plays paper w.p. b, scissors w.p. 1−b.
Payoff P(a,b)=a+b−3ab; find the equilibrium.
a=b=1/3 gives P=1/3.
questions.genSolution: Drop paper; let you play rock w.p. $a$, scissors $1-a$, and the opponent paper w.p. $b$, scissors $1-b$. Your payoff is $P(a,b)=a(1-2b)+(1-a)b=a+b-3ab$. The equilibrium $a=b=\tfrac13$ gives $P=\tfrac13+\tfrac13-3\cdot\tfrac19=\tfrac13.$

questions.title: Bus Wait II
questions.question: Imagine you are waiting on a bus. The bus schedule is fixed, appearing every $10$ minutes. Although, the driver independently between appearances, may want to refill on gas. The probability the driver refills on gas is  $10\%$ probability per trial, independently between trials. Note, if he fills up on gas,  $1$ hour is added to his travel time.  If you arrive at a uniformly random time throughout the day, what is the expected time until the next bus appears, in minutes?
questions.hint: Use Law of Total Expectation to condition on whether or not the bus stops for gas.
hints: Gaps are 10 min normally, 70 min if the driver refills (prob 0.1) — the inspection paradox applies.
questions.genSolution: Inter-bus gaps are $L=10$ w.p. $0.9$ and $70$ w.p. $0.1$. Arriving at a random time, the expected wait (inspection paradox) is $\dfrac{\mathbb{E}[L^2]}{2\mathbb{E}[L]}$. With $\mathbb{E}[L]=16$ and $\mathbb{E}[L^2]=580$, this is $\dfrac{580}{32}=\dfrac{145}{8}.$

questions.title: Placing Dots
questions.question: You place three dots along four edges of a square at random. What is the probability that the dots lie on distinct edges?
questions.hint: Break down the problem by each dot. What is the probability the first dot is put on a distinct edge? What is the probability the second dot is put on a distinct edge? What is the probability the third dot is put on a distinct edge? How can you combine these probabilities to come to a final answer?
hints: Each dot lands on one of 4 edges uniformly; want all distinct.
questions.genSolution: The first dot is anywhere; the second avoids its edge ($\tfrac34$) and the third avoids both ($\tfrac24$): $P=\tfrac44\cdot\tfrac34\cdot\tfrac24=\tfrac38.$

questions.title: Grid Filling III
questions.question: We place the integers $1-9$ (inclusive, no replacement) randomly on a $3\times 3$ grid. What is the probability each row, column and diagonal add up to an odd number?
questions.hint: How many orientations fit our criteria?
hints: Only parities matter: place five odds so every row, column, and diagonal has an odd count of odds.
The unique pattern is the central 'plus' (middle row + middle column).
1 valid placement of odds out of C(9,5)=126 ⇒ 1/126.
questions.genSolution: Each line needs an odd number of odd entries. Placing the five odd numbers in the central 'plus' (the middle row and middle column) makes every row, column, and both diagonals have an odd count — and it's the only such pattern. The odds occupy $1$ of $\binom95=126$ position-sets, so $P=\tfrac1{126}.$

questions.title: Casted Shadow
questions.question: Billy is $6$ft tall, he is walking away from a lamp post at a rate of $40$ft per minute. Given that when Billy is $10$ feet from the lamp post, his shadow is $15$ feet long, find the rate at which his shadow's length is increasing (in feet per minute) when he is $40$ feet from the lamp post.
questions.hint: Let $x$ be the distance from the base of the lamp and let $s$ the shadow length. We know that $x' = 40$ is constant, as the rate of change of his distance, or velocity, is constant at $40$ft/minute. Use similar trianges to find $s'$ when $x = 10$ and $s = 40$.
hints: Similar triangles: lamp height/(x+s) = 6/s. Use the given x=10,s=15 to find the lamp height (10).
Then s = 1.5x, so ds/dt = 1.5·dx/dt.
questions.genSolution: Similar triangles give $\dfrac{H}{x+s}=\dfrac6s$; from $x=10,s=15$, $H=10$. Then $10s=6(x+s)\Rightarrow s=\tfrac32 x$, so $\dfrac{ds}{dt}=\tfrac32\dfrac{dx}{dt}=\tfrac32\cdot40=60$ ft/min (independent of position).

questions.title: Hours of Labor
questions.question: A factory wants to maximize the number of hours their employees are not working each year. If none of the employees has a birthday on a given day of the year, then every employee must come to work all $24$ hours that day. If at least one person has a birthday on a given day, then none of the employees need to work that day. This factory has good work-life balance, so they want to hire the number of people that would maximize the expected total number of hours that employees are NOT working each year. How many employees should the factory hire? If the answer is infinite, answer $-1$.
questions.hint: Let $Y_n$ be the number of hours that employees have off in a year when there are $n$ employees. Then $\mathbb{E}[Y_n] = 24n \mathbb{E}[T_n]$, where $T_n$ is the number of days that the employees have off in a year. To calculate $\mathbb{E}[T_n]$, let $X_1,X_2,\dots, X_n$ be the indicators of the event that the $i$th person, $1 \leq i \leq n$, has a distinct birthday distinct from the other $n-1$ people. Then $T_n = X_1 + \dots + X_n$ gives the total number of distinct birthdays in the year.
hints: Hours off = 24n × (number of days with at least one birthday).
E[off days]=365(1−(364/365)ⁿ) → 365, so E[hours off]≈24n·365 grows with n.
Unbounded in n ⇒ infinite ⇒ −1.
questions.genSolution: On a day with at least one birthday all $n$ employees get $24$ hours off, so expected hours off $=24n\cdot365\big(1-(\tfrac{364}{365})^n\big)$. As $n\to\infty$ the bracket $\to1$ and the expression grows like $24\cdot365\,n\to\infty$. There is no maximizer, so the answer is $-1.$

questions.title: Optimal Marbles II
questions.question: Two players, called $X$ and $Y$, engage in a strategic game:  $$$$ Each has $100$ tokens and secretly decides how many tokens, between $1$ and $100$, to contribute to a shared pool. Once both players have made their decisions, one token is randomly drawn from the pool. If the token belongs to $X$, then assuming $X$ contributed $x$ tokens, $X$ receives $100 - x$ monetary units from $Y$.  $$$$ Similarly, if the token belongs to $Y$, then assuming $Y$ contributed $y$ tokens, $Y$ receives $100 - y$ monetary units from $X$. Both players aim to maximize their respective monetary outcomes and are assumed to play optimally. How many tokens should $X$ choose to contribute?
questions.hint: Define an expected payout function for $X$ based on $x$ and $y$, and find the optimal contributions for both players by solving for equilibrium.
hints: Now it's zero-sum: X's payoff is [x(100−x) − y(100−y)]/(x+y).
At the symmetric equilibrium x=y, ∂/∂x of the numerator gives (100−2x)(2x)=0.
questions.genSolution: X's expected payoff is $U(x,y)=\dfrac{x(100-x)-y(100-y)}{x+y}$ (X gains when its token is drawn, pays when Y's is). At the symmetric equilibrium $x=y$, $\partial_x U=0$ reduces to $(100-2x)(2x)=0$, so $x=50.$

questions.title: Infected Dinner I
questions.question: There are $1000$ people having dinner at a grand hall. One of them is known to be sick, while the other $999$ are healthy. Each minute, each person talks to one other person in the room at random. There is no limit on how many times a person talks to another person. If one is sick and one is healthy, the healthy person is infected and becomes sick. Once a person becomes sick, they are assumed to be sick for the rest of the dinner. Find the minimum amount of time (in minutes) until every person in the hall becomes sick.
questions.hint: The minimum amount of time would correspond to the most people becoming infected the fastest. This would be when each infected person talks to a healthy person at every step.
hints: In the best case the sick count doubles each minute.
Need 2ᵗ ≥ 1000.
2¹⁰=1024 ⇒ 10 minutes.
questions.genSolution: At best every sick person infects a new healthy one each minute, so the count doubles: $1,2,4,\dots,2^t$. We need $2^t\ge1000$, giving $t=10$ (since $2^{10}=1024$).

questions.title: Deviating Sums
questions.question: Two identical sets of cards are in front of you. Each set has $5$ cards labeled $0-4$. You pick a card from each set and add them together. What is the standard deviation of sum of the cards chosen?
questions.hint: What properties of variance can we use to solve this problem?
hints: Each card is uniform on {0,1,2,3,4}: variance (5²−1)/12=2.
Independent sum ⇒ variance adds: 2+2=4.
questions.genSolution: Each card is uniform on $\{0,\dots,4\}$ with variance $\tfrac{5^2-1}{12}=2$. The independent sum has variance $2+2=4$, so the standard deviation is $2.$

questions.title: Rainbow Trains
questions.question:
There are three train cars: a blue one, a red one, and a green one. This train has a conductor whose job is to disconnect the back train cars and put it in front. They can do this as many or as few times as they want.
If the three train cars are randomly ordered, what is the probability that the conductor can rearrange it into rainbow order? (Note: Here, rainbow order means red, then green, then blue).
questions.hint: To be able to be arranged into rainbow order, the cars must be in cycle of $RGB$.
hints: The allowed moves are cyclic rotations, so only the cyclic order matters.
Of the 6 orderings, the 3 in RGB's cyclic class can be rotated to RGB.
questions.genSolution: Moving the back car to the front is a cyclic rotation, which preserves cyclic order. Of the $6$ arrangements, exactly the $3$ in the same cyclic class as $RGB$ can be rotated into rainbow order, so $P=\tfrac36=\tfrac12.$

questions.title: The Last Airbender
questions.question: Four cards labelled water, earth, fire, and air are placed in front of you faced down. You flip each of the cards over one at a time. You win if you flip over both the air and water cards before the fire card. Otherwise, you lose. What is the probability that you win?
questions.hint: The earth card is absent from our sample space and thus does not affect your probability of winning.
hints: The earth card is irrelevant; consider only air, water, fire.
You win iff fire is last among these three.
P(fire last of 3)=1/3.
questions.siteSolution:
The earth card is absent from our sample space and thus does not affect your probability of winning. Imagine ordering the remaining three cards in the order that you flip them- there are a total of $3!$ possible permutations. Of these permutations, favorable outcomes are those where the first two cards are air then water or water then air. Thus, the probability that you win is:

 $$ 
\frac{2}{3!}=\frac{1}{3}
 $$

questions.title: Car Crash
questions.question: $N$ employees are driving their individual cars on their way to work at QuantEssential. All cars are reasonably spaced apart, and they all travel at distinct speeds which are randomly assigned. When a faster car catches up to a slower car, it assumes the slower car's speed. After a very long period of time has passed, all $N$ cars have settled into $K$ clusters traveling at distinct speeds. What is the expected value of $K$ when $N = 10$?
hints: A car leads its own cluster iff it's the slowest among all cars ahead of it (a 'record' from the front).
P(car ranked i-th from front leads)=1/i, so E[K]=Σ_{i=1}^{N} 1/i = H_N.
questions.genSolution: A car heads its own cluster exactly when no car ahead of it is slower — i.e. it is a left-to-right minimum of speeds. The $i$-th car from the front is such a record with probability $\tfrac1i$, so $\mathbb{E}[K]=\sum_{i=1}^{N}\tfrac1i=H_N$. For $N=10$, $H_{10}=\tfrac{7381}{2520}.$

questions.title: Multiple Divisors I
questions.question: Find the probability that a randomly selected integer from the set of positive divisors of $10^{99}$ is divisible by $10^{80}$.
questions.hint: Consider the prime factorization of $10^{99}$. How many divisors are there?
hints: Divisible by 10^80 needs both exponents in {80,…,99}: 20·20=400.
questions.genSolution: $10^{99}=2^{99}5^{99}$ has $100\cdot100=10000$ divisors. Being divisible by $10^{80}=2^{80}5^{80}$ requires each exponent in $\{80,\dots,99\}$: $20\cdot20=400$ divisors. So $P=\tfrac{400}{10000}=\tfrac1{25}.$

questions.title: Exponential Brownian Motion
questions.question: Let $W_t$ be a standard Brownian Motion.  Find the sum of the factorials of the values of $n$ where $X_t = W_t^n$ is a martingale.
questions.hint: Use Ito's lemma with $f(x) = x^n$.
hints: By Itô, d(Wⁿ)=nW^{n−1}dW + (n(n−1)/2)W^{n−2}dt; martingale needs zero drift.
Drift vanishes only for n=0 and n=1.
questions.genSolution: By Itô, $d(W_t^n)=nW_t^{n-1}dW_t+\tfrac{n(n-1)}2 W_t^{n-2}dt$, which is driftless (a martingale) only for $n=0$ and $n=1$. The sum of factorials is $0!+1!=2.$

questions.title: Second Moment
questions.question: Given that $\text{Var}(X) = 25$ and $\mathbb{E}[X] = 3$, find $\mathbb{E}[X^2]$.
questions.hint: Use the relation $\text{Var}(X) = \mathbb{E}[X^2] - (\mathbb{E}[X])^2$
hints: E[X²]=Var(X)+(E[X])².
questions.genSolution: $\mathbb{E}[X^2]=\mathrm{Var}(X)+(\mathbb{E}[X])^2=25+9=34.$

questions.title: Exercise Vega
questions.question: You have $3$ at-the-money options on some different underlyings with strikes $K = 10$, $K = 20$, $K = 30$, all with expiry $T$. Assuming Black-Scholes dynamics, which option (strike) has the largest vega?
questions.hint: Are options with higher or lower strikes more valuable? Assuming you can exercise them.
hints: Vega ∝ S·√T·φ(d₁); for ATM options it grows with the price level.
Higher strike means higher underlying (ATM), hence larger vega.
questions.genSolution: For ATM options, vega $\approx S\sqrt T\,\varphi(d_1)$ scales with the price level $S=K$. The largest strike $K=30$ therefore has the greatest vega.

questions.title: Pairwise Digit Sums I
questions.question: Find the largest value of $n$ such that the there exists a $n$ digit number with all unique pairwise sums of digits. For example, if $n = 3$, $174$ is a valid integer, since $1+7 \neq 1+4 \neq 7 + 4$.
questions.hint: Try creating a $6-$digit integer first.
hints: Distinct pairwise digit sums means the digits form a Sidon (B₂) set within {0,…,9}.
The largest Sidon subset of {0,…,9} has size 5 (e.g. {0,1,2,6,9}).
So n=5.
questions.genSolution: All pairwise sums distinct means the digits form a Sidon set. The largest Sidon subset of $\{0,\dots,9\}$ has size $5$ (for instance $\{0,1,2,6,9\}$ has all $\binom52=10$ pairwise sums distinct), so the largest such number has $n=5$ digits.

questions.title: Acceptance-Rejection Sampling
questions.question: Suppose we want to sample from the PDF of $X \sim \text{Beta}(3,2)$, which has PDF $f(x) = 12x^2(1-x)I_{(0,1)}(x)$. Since you know how to generate uniformly random pairs in the rectangle $R_a = [0,1] \times [0,a]$ for some $a > 0$, you decide to sample from $R_a$. You will count your random point $(X,Y) \in R_a$ as valid for the PDF if it lies in the region bounded by the $x-$axis and $f(x)$. Otherwise, it is rejected. If $a$ is selected to minimize the number of rejected pairs of points per sample, find the expected amount of rejected pairs per accepted pair.
questions.hint: We need to find $a$ such that it is closest to $f(x)$. In other words, $a$ is just going to be the maximum of $f(x)$ on $[0,1]$.
hints: Choose a = max of f on [0,1]; f'(x)=0 at x=2/3 gives a=16/9.
Acceptance probability = (area under f)/(rectangle area)=1/(1·16/9)=9/16.
Expected rejects per accept = (1−p)/p = (7/16)/(9/16) = 7/9.
questions.genSolution: The tightest rectangle uses $a=\max f=f(\tfrac23)=\tfrac{16}{9}$. Each point is accepted with probability $\dfrac{\int_0^1 f}{1\cdot a}=\dfrac{1}{16/9}=\dfrac9{16}$. The number of rejects before an accept is geometric with mean $\dfrac{1-p}{p}=\dfrac{7/16}{9/16}=\dfrac79.$

questions.title: Skewed
questions.question: Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with PDF $f(x) = \dfrac{\lambda}{2}e^{-\lambda |x - \mu |}$ for all some real constant $\lambda$ and $\mu > 0$. Given that $\mu_X$ and $\sigma_X$ are the mean and standard deviation of $X$, compute the skewness of $X$ i.e. $\mathbb{E}\left[\left(\dfrac{X - \mu_X}{\sigma_X}\right)^3\right]$, You may assume without proof the first three moments of $X$ are finite.
questions.hint: Define the LOTUS integral for this, notice any symmetries?
hints: The density is a Laplace distribution, symmetric about μ.
Symmetric distributions have zero third central moment.
Skewness = 0.
questions.genSolution: The density $f(x)=\tfrac{\lambda}2 e^{-\lambda|x-\mu|}$ is symmetric about $\mu$, so all odd central moments vanish. Hence the skewness $\mathbb{E}[((X-\mu_X)/\sigma_X)^3]=0.$

questions.title: Take And Roll II
questions.question: You are playing a game with a total of 100 actions and a fair $20-$sided die. $$$$ The die starts with face of $1$. The two actions you can perform in the game are to roll and to take. Performing a roll re-rolls the current face of the die. Performing a take allows you to cash out the current face of the die. The game does ends when you complete all 100 actions and you must roll the die again before doing another take. Your strategy is to accept any number that is at least some threshold $n$. This $n$ must be decided in advance and is fixed for the entire game. Assuming rational play in selecting $n$, find your expected payout.
questions.hint: Start with a baseline to compare to, suppose we just roll and always take again and again. We will be able to perform this $50$ times (as each is one action) and the expected value per roll is $10.5$ = $\dfrac{(1+2+... +20)}{20}$, so our expected payout would be $50 \cdot 10.5 = 525$ with this strategy.  $$$$ Now write the expected payoff as a function of $n$.
hints: Each take must be preceded by a roll, so a 'cycle' = (rolls until ≥n) + 1 take.
Cycle length 20/(21−n)+1 actions; #cycles=100/(cycle); take value averages (20+n)/2.
Maximize over n: n=6 gives (300/7)·13 = 3900/7.
questions.genSolution: Since each take needs a fresh roll, a cycle is (roll until $\ge n$) then take, of length $\tfrac{20}{21-n}+1$ actions, each yielding a take worth $\tfrac{20+n}2$. Expected payout $=\dfrac{100}{\tfrac{20}{21-n}+1}\cdot\dfrac{20+n}{2}$, maximized at $n=6$: $\dfrac{300}{7}\cdot13=\dfrac{3900}{7}.$

questions.title: Increasing Chains
questions.question: Fix positive integers $n$ and $k$, and suppose $X_1,\dots,X_n \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)$ IID. We say that there is an $\textit{increasing k-chain}$ starting from position $i$ if $X_i < X_{i+1} < \dots < X_{i + (k-1)}$. Find $n$ such that the expected number of increasing $6-$chains among $X_1,\dots,X_n$ is $1$.
questions.hint: Index $n-(k-1)$ is the last spot at which we can have an increasing chain of length $k$. This is because we would need spots $n-(k-1),\dots, n$ to be increasing order. After that spot, there are not $k$ open spots to be increasing. Therefore, let $I_i$ be the indicator of the event that starting that index $i$, $X_i < \dots < X_{i+k-1}$.
hints: There are n−5 possible starting positions for a 6-chain; each is increasing with probability 1/6!.
questions.genSolution: Each of the $n-5$ starting positions gives an increasing $6$-chain with probability $\tfrac1{6!}=\tfrac1{720}$, so $\mathbb{E}=\tfrac{n-5}{720}$. Setting this to $1$ gives $n=725.$

questions.title: No Heads
questions.question: Let $N \sim \text{Poisson}(2)$. We flip a fair coin $N$ times. Find the probability that no heads are obtained by the end of $N$ flips. Your answer should be in the form $e^{a}$ for a rational number $a$. Find $a$.
questions.hint: Condition on the value of $N$. What is the probability of this event given that $N = n$?
hints: Condition on N: P(no heads | N=n)=(1/2)ⁿ.
questions.siteSolution: Let $X$ represent the number of heads obtained. Given $N = n$, we flip the coin $n$ times. The chance that none of the flips result in heads in $n$ attempts is $\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^n$. Thus, this is $\mathbb{P}[X = 0 \mid N = n].$ $$$$ We want to find $\mathbb{P}[X = 0] = \displaystyle \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \mathbb{P}[X = 0 \mid N = n]\mathbb{P}[N = n]$ using the Law of Total Probability. We know that $\mathbb{P}[N = n] = \dfrac{2^n}{n!}e^{-2}$ from the distribution of $N$. We just calculated the other term, so we have $\mathbb{P}[X = 0] = \displaystyle \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{2^n} \cdot \dfrac{2^n}{n!}e^{-2} = e^{-2} \displaystyle \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{n!}$. The remaining sum is the Taylor expansion of $e$, leading to $\mathbb{P}[X = 0] = e^{-1}$. This indicates that $a = -1$.

questions.title: Uniform Equilibrium I
questions.question: Two players, say $1$ and $2$, simultaneously pick real numbers in the interval $[0,1]$. The payoff of Player $1$ (equal to the loss of Player $2$) is the absolute distance between those numbers. Find the number of pure-strategy Nash equilbria.
questions.hint: Suppose that player $1$ picks $0 < a < 1$ and player $2$ picks $0 < b < 1$. Without loss of generality, say $a < b$. Can either player do better?
hints: Player 1 wants the numbers far apart; Player 2 wants them close.
From any pure pair, one player can profitably deviate (P1 to an endpoint, P2 toward P1).
No pure-strategy equilibrium exists ⇒ 0.
questions.genSolution: Player 1 maximizes $|a-b|$ and Player 2 minimizes it. For any fixed $(a,b)$, Player 2 can move toward $a$ to reduce the distance, or Player 1 can move to an endpoint to increase it — so no pair is stable. There are $0$ pure-strategy Nash equilibria.

questions.title: OLS Review III
questions.question: Given the following data, $(x, y)$: $(-2, 0), (-1, 0), (0, 1), (1, 1), (2, 3)$, Bob does a simple linear regression modeling $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \epsilon$, where $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon] = 0$ and $\text{Var}[\epsilon] = \sigma^2$. Estimate $\sigma^2$.
questions.hint:
\[\begin{aligned}
    \hat{\beta}_1 &= \frac{S_{xy}}{S_{xx}}, \\
    \hat{\beta}_0 &= \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}.
\end{aligned}\] Use $s^2$ as an unbiased estimate for $\sigma^2$.
questions.genSolution: Here $\bar x=0,\bar y=1$, $S_{xy}=7$, $S_{xx}=10$, so $\hat\beta_1=0.7,\hat\beta_0=1$. The residuals are $0.4,-0.3,0,-0.7,0.6$ with $\text{SSE}=1.10$, so $s^2=\dfrac{1.10}{5-2}=\dfrac{11}{30}.$

questions.title: Counting Odds
questions.question: How many odd numbers from 1 to 2023, inclusive, are divisible by 3?
questions.hint: Performing an operation (addition, subtraction, etc.) on each term within a finite sequence does not affect the length of the sequence.
hints: Odd and divisible by 3 means ≡3 (mod 6).
questions.genSolution: Numbers that are odd and divisible by $3$ are exactly those $\equiv3\pmod6$: $3,9,\dots,2019$. Their count is $\dfrac{2019-3}{6}+1=337.$

questions.title: Builders
questions.question: Alice and Bob can each build a pipe on their own in $120$ and $100$ minutes, respectively. Instead, they work together on building a pipe. After 40 minutes of building, Charlie assists them in building the pipe. Together, they finish building the pipe $10$ minutes after Charlie joins. In how many minutes could Charlie build the pipe on his own?
questions.hint: Consider the rate at which each person builds and suppose a scenario where 600 units must be built to fully create the pipe. It follows then that Alice builds at $5$ units per minute and Bob builds at $6$ units per minute (indeed, their respective completion times are $120$ and $100$ minutes). How does Charlie affect this process?
hints: Scale to 600 units: Alice 5/min, Bob 6/min. After 40 min they've built 440, leaving 160.
Three together finish 160 in 10 min: (5+6+c)·10=160 ⇒ c=5.
Charlie alone: 600/5 = 120 min.
questions.genSolution: Take the pipe as $600$ units: Alice builds $5$/min, Bob $6$/min. In $40$ min they finish $440$, leaving $160$, done in $10$ more minutes by all three: $(5+6+c)\cdot10=160\Rightarrow c=5$. So Charlie alone needs $600/5=120$ minutes.

questions.title: Basketball Practice II
questions.question: Lebron is shooting free throws. He hits the first shot but misses the second. For $n \geq 3$, the probability of making the $n$th free throw is equal to the proportion of free throws he made during his first $n-1$ attempts.  If Lebron makes 100 attempts, how many freethrows can we expect him to make.
questions.hint: Write a recurrence relation for $E_n$, the expected number of free throws after $n$ total attempts.
hints: Starting 1 make, 1 miss, the made-fraction is a martingale fixed at 1/2 (Pólya urn).
So the expected fraction made over 100 attempts is 1/2.
questions.genSolution: This is a Pólya urn: the running fraction made is a martingale equal to its start $\tfrac12$. Hence the expected number made in $100$ attempts is $100\cdot\tfrac12=50.$

questions.title: Double Data Trouble V
questions.question: Suppose that you run linear regression on some dataset and obtain the coefficients $\hat{\beta}_{OLS}$. Recall that if $X$ is the data and $\sigma^2$ is the variance of the IID normal errors, then $\text{Var}\left(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}\right) = \sigma^2(X^TX)^{-1}$. If you were to run linear regression again on the dataset where you double the values of each point in your original dataset and obtain new coefficients $\hat{\beta}_{OLS}'$, find the constant $c$ such that $\text{Var}\left(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}'\right) = c\text{Var}\left(\hat{\beta}_{OLS}\right)$. If no such constant exists, enter $-1$.
questions.hint: When we double the data, we are just replacing the matrix $X$ with the matrix $X' = 2X$. Accordingly, $X'^TX' = 4X^TX$ by simple matrix multiplication. What happens to $\sigma$?
hints: Doubling data: X'=2X so (X'ᵀX')⁻¹=(1/4)(XᵀX)⁻¹; but the y's (and errors) also double, so σ'²=4σ².
Var(β')=σ'²(X'ᵀX')⁻¹=4σ²·(1/4)(XᵀX)⁻¹.
= σ²(XᵀX)⁻¹ = Var(β) ⇒ c=1.
questions.genSolution: With $X'=2X$, $(X'^TX')^{-1}=\tfrac14(X^TX)^{-1}$. But doubling the data also doubles the responses and errors, so $\sigma'^2=4\sigma^2$. Hence $\mathrm{Var}(\hat\beta')=4\sigma^2\cdot\tfrac14(X^TX)^{-1}=\sigma^2(X^TX)^{-1}=\mathrm{Var}(\hat\beta)$, giving $c=1.$

questions.title: 8 Card Heart
questions.question: $8$ cards are dealt out from a standard deck. Find the variance of the number of hearts in the $8$ cards.
questions.hint: Let $H_i$ be the indicator that the $i$th card dealt is a heart. Then $T = H_1 + \dots + H_8$ is the total number of hearts in the dealing of $8$ cards.
hints: Number of hearts is hypergeometric (n=8, K=13, N=52).
questions.genSolution: The count of hearts is hypergeometric, with $\mathrm{Var}=n\cdot\tfrac{K}{N}\big(1-\tfrac{K}{N}\big)\cdot\tfrac{N-n}{N-1}=8\cdot\tfrac14\cdot\tfrac34\cdot\tfrac{44}{51}=\dfrac{22}{17}.$

questions.title: Stamp Sum
questions.question: You possess an infinite supply of stamps valued at $5$ and $21$. What is the highest value that cannot be represented as a combination of these stamps.
questions.hint: The values $21$, $42$, $63$, and $84$ are the smallest numbers equivalent to $1$, $2$, $3$, and $4$ modulo $5$, respectively. Continue from here...
hints: For two coprime values a,b, the largest non-representable amount is the Frobenius number ab−a−b.
Here a=5, b=21.
questions.genSolution: For coprime denominations $5$ and $21$, the largest value not representable is the Frobenius number $5\cdot21-5-21=105-26=79.$

questions.title: Bolt Variance II
questions.question: A manufacturer produces nuts and bolts. A random sample of $12$ bolts returned a sample variance of $0.0003$, while a random sample of $12$ bolts from a competitor returned a sample variance of $0.0001$.  $$$$ They manufacturer wants to test if there is sufficient data to indicate a smaller variation in bolt diameter from their competitor. Assuming independence, variance homogeneity, and that diameter variance is approximately normally distributed, what is the value of the relevant test statistic?
questions.hint: Using the F test is best-suited here (ANOVA).
hints: Comparing two variances uses the F statistic = ratio of sample variances.
questions.genSolution: The test statistic for comparing two variances is $F=\dfrac{s_1^2}{s_2^2}=\dfrac{0.0003}{0.0001}=3.$

questions.title: Statistical Test Review IV
questions.question: Spotify's competitor, Soundcloud, offers three plans: $A, B, C$. Of the first $1000$ plans $400$ plans of  $A$ were sold. Can one conclude that customers have a preference for plan $A$? Respond $1$ if yes, $0$ if no.
hints: Test H₀:p=1/3 vs p>1/3 with p̂=0.4, n=1000.
Reject H₀ — yes, a preference (1).
questions.genSolution: Testing $p=\tfrac13$ against $p>\tfrac13$: $Z=\dfrac{0.4-\tfrac13}{\sqrt{\tfrac13\cdot\tfrac23/1000}}\approx4.47$, well beyond any standard critical value, so we reject $H_0$ — customers do prefer plan $A$: answer $1.$

questions.title: Car Line
questions.question: Alice, Bob, and Carter are driving down a one-lane road in the same direction. Each car moves at some constant speed. In some instant, Alice is a distance $d$ behind Bob and Carter is a distance $2d$ in front of Bob. Alice passes Bob $7$ minutes after this instant. $5$ minutes after that, Alice passes Carter. How many seconds after Alice passes Carter would Bob pass Carter?
questions.hint: Let $v_A, v_B,$ and $v_C$ be the speeds of Alice, Bob, and Carter, respectively. The idea here is to consider the relative speeds of the cars to one another. Alice starts a distance $d$ behind Bob, so $\dfrac{d}{v_A - v_B} = 7$. Repeat this for other distances. Write the time of interest in terms of $d$ and some of the velocities.
hints: Relative speeds: d/(v_A−v_B)=7 and (Alice is 3d behind Carter) 3d/(v_A−v_C)=12.
Then v_B−v_C=(v_A−v_C)−(v_A−v_B)=d/4−d/7=3d/28; Bob reaches Carter at 2d/(v_B−v_C)=56/3 min.
After Alice passes Carter (12 min): 56/3−12=20/3 min = 400 s.
questions.genSolution: From relative speeds: $v_A-v_B=\tfrac d7$ and $v_A-v_C=\tfrac{3d}{12}=\tfrac d4$, so $v_B-v_C=\tfrac d4-\tfrac d7=\tfrac{3d}{28}$. Bob (initially $2d$ behind Carter) catches Carter at $\tfrac{2d}{3d/28}=\tfrac{56}{3}$ min after the instant, i.e. $\tfrac{56}{3}-12=\tfrac{20}{3}$ min after Alice passes Carter $=400$ seconds.

questions.title: Pizza Munching
questions.question: Garrett loves eating pizza, but enjoys savoring the slices too. The time it takes for him to eat a pizza slice is uniformly distributed between $1$ and $5$ minutes. If he has been eating for $2$ minutes and still isn't done, find the probability that he finishes the slice in the next minute.
questions.hint: Let $T$ be the amount of time that Garrett takes to eat his slice of pizza. We know that $T \sim \text{Unif}(1,5)$ from the question. We know he has spent at least $2$ minutes eating his slice, and we want the probability he finishes in the next minute. What specific probability are we interested in?
hints: T~Unif(1,5); want P(T<3 | T>2).

questions.title: Coin Identifier
questions.question: Brian walks to the convenience store with $\$0.60$ in his pocket. His money is made of solely nickels, dimes, and quarters. If Brian has $N$ coins in his pocket, find the smallest value of $N$ such that the quantities of each type of coin in Brian's pocket can't be uniquely identified.
questions.hint: Note that $\$0.40$ is the smallest denomination that can be written in two different ways using the same amount of coins.
hints: Find the smallest coin count where 60¢ has two different (nickel,dime,quarter) breakdowns.
With 6 coins: (0 q, 6 d, 0 n) and (1 q, 2 d, 3 n) both total 60¢.
So N=6.
questions.genSolution: We need the fewest coins for which $60$¢ is ambiguous. With $6$ coins, both $6$ dimes and $\{1\text{ quarter},2\text{ dimes},3\text{ nickels}\}$ total $60$¢, so the counts can't be determined. No smaller $N$ is ambiguous, so $N=6.$

questions.title: Minimal Shade
questions.question: Consider an array of white unit squares arranged in a rectangular grid with $r$ rows of unit squares and $c$ columns of unit squares. What is the smallest possible value of $c$ such that, if we shade $s$ unit squares in each column black, then there must necessarily be some row with at least $t$ black unit squares? You should receive a function $f(r,t,s)$. Evaluate $f(30,21,14)$.
questions.hint: The total number of unit squares shaded black is $cs$. Meanwhile, if each row has at most $t - 1$ black unit squares, then by the Pigeonhole Principle, at most $r(t - 1)$ unit squares could have been shaded black. Thus, if $cs > r(t - 1)$, then any valid shading configuration must yield a row with at least $t$ black unit squares. Can you do better than this?
hints: Total shaded = cs; if every row had ≤ t−1, then cs ≤ r(t−1).
So c > r(t−1)/s forces some row to reach t: c = ⌊r(t−1)/s⌋ + 1.
questions.genSolution: If no row reaches $t$, every row has $\le t-1$ black squares, so $cs\le r(t-1)$. Thus $c=\big\lfloor\tfrac{r(t-1)}{s}\big\rfloor+1$ forces a row with $\ge t$. For $(30,21,14)$: $\lfloor\tfrac{600}{14}\rfloor+1=42+1=43.$

questions.title: Dollar Draw
questions.question: A box consists of $1$ $\$100$ bill, $3$ $\$20$ bills, and $4$ $\$10$ bills. Given you select $7$ bills out of this bin uniformly at random without replacement, find the expected value of the sum of all the bills you select.
questions.hint: The average value of each bill selected is the sum of the bills divided by the number of bills.
hints: Total value of all 8 bills = 200; each drawn bill averages 200/8=25.
E[sum of 7] = 7·25.
questions.genSolution: The eight bills total $\$200$, so each selected bill averages $\$25$. The expected sum of $7$ is $7\cdot25=\$175.$

questions.title: Coordinate Jumper
questions.question: What is the total number of paths from $(0,0,0)$ to $(3,4,5)$ in 3D space, given that we can move one unit to the right, forward, or up at each step?
questions.hint: All path from $(0,0,0)$ to $(3,4,5)$ will consist of $3,4,$ and $5$ movements in the $x,y,$ and $z-$directions, respectively.
hints: A path is an arrangement of 3 x-moves, 4 y-moves, 5 z-moves.
questions.genSolution: Each path is an ordering of $3+4+5=12$ unit moves: $\dfrac{12!}{3!\,4!\,5!}=27720.$

questions.title: Sum Covariance
questions.question: Let $X,Y,$ and $Z$ be uncorrelated random variables with respective variances $64, 36,$ and $225$. Let $U = X+Y$ and $V = X+Z$. Find $\text{Corr}(U,V)$.
questions.hint: Since $X,Y,$ and $Z$ are uncorrelated, the variance of the sum of any two is just the sum of the variances. Use Bilinearity of Covariance.
hints: Cov(U,V)=Cov(X+Y,X+Z)=Var(X)=64 (others uncorrelated).
questions.genSolution: $\mathrm{Cov}(U,V)=\mathrm{Var}(X)=64$, while $\mathrm{Var}(U)=100$ and $\mathrm{Var}(V)=289$. So $\mathrm{Corr}(U,V)=\dfrac{64}{\sqrt{100\cdot289}}=\dfrac{64}{170}=\dfrac{32}{85}.$

questions.title: Free Sundae
questions.question: You are in line at an ice cream shop when the manager announces that she will give a free sundae to the first person in line whose birthday is the same as someone who has already bought an ice cream. Assuming that you do not know anyone else's birthday and that all birthdays are uniformly distributed across the 365 days in a normal year, what position in line will you choose to maximize your probability of receiving the free sundae?
questions.hint: Think of this as an optimization problem. How can you define the function you are trying to optimize?
hints: Position k wins iff the first k−1 birthdays are distinct AND yours matches one of them.
The optimum is near √365 ≈ 19–20; it peaks at position 20.
questions.genSolution: You win at position $k$ if the first $k-1$ people all have distinct birthdays and yours matches one of them: $P(k)=\Big(\prod_{i=1}^{k-2}\tfrac{365-i}{365}\Big)\cdot\tfrac{k-1}{365}$. Maximizing this discrete product (the optimum sits near $\sqrt{365}\approx19$) gives the peak at position $20.$

questions.title: Fibonacci Limit I
questions.question: Let $F_n$ be the Fibonacci sequence. Compute $\displaystyle \lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} \dfrac{F_{n+1}}{F_n}$. Your answer should be in the form $\dfrac{a+\sqrt{b}}{c}$, where all of $a,b,$ and $c$ are pairwise relatively prime. Find $abc$.
questions.hint: Write $F_{n+1} = F_n + F_{n-1}$ and get a polynomial that the limit solves.
hints: The ratio L=lim F_{n+1}/F_n satisfies L=1+1/L.
questions.genSolution: Dividing $F_{n+1}=F_n+F_{n-1}$ by $F_n$ and taking the limit gives $L=1+\tfrac1L$, i.e. $L^2-L-1=0$, so $L=\tfrac{1+\sqrt5}{2}$. Thus $a=1,b=5,c=2$ and $abc=10.$

questions.title: Rolls in a Row
questions.question: On average, how many rolls of a fair $6$-sided die must be rolled on average to get two $6$s in a row?
questions.hint: Consider the various states leading up to rolling two consecutive $6$s.
hints: Expected rolls for a run of k successes with prob p is (p^{−k}−1)/(1−p).
Here p=1/6, k=2.
questions.genSolution: The expected number of rolls for two consecutive $6$s is $\dfrac{p^{-2}-1}{1-p}=\dfrac{36-1}{5/6}=42.$

questions.title: Doubly 5 II
questions.question: Jenny has a fair $6-$sided die with numbers $1-6$ on the sides. Jenny continually rolls the die and keeps track of the outcomes in the order they appear. Jenny rolls until she sees two $5$s (not necessarily consecutive) OR both $4$ and $6$. Find the probability Jenny stops rolling due to seeing two $5$s.
