The bottom shelf contains gray socks with probability $0.7$ and white socks with probability $0.3$. $$$$ You pick a shelf at random. From that shelf, you pick $2$ gray socks. What is the probability that you selected the top shelf? Assume you have so many socks that taking from either shelf does not change the probability of future socks that are drawn.
questions.hint: What methods can we use to solve conditional probability questions?
hints: P(2 gray | top)=0.4²=0.16; P(2 gray | bottom)=0.7²=0.49.
Both shelves equally likely a priori.
questions.genSolution: By Bayes with equal priors, $P(\text{top}\mid 2\text{ gray})=\dfrac{0.4^2}{0.4^2+0.7^2}=\dfrac{0.16}{0.65}=\dfrac{16}{65}.$

questions.title: Gambling Addiction
questions.question: Gabe has a crippling gambling addiction. As such, he plays a game where the winning number on the $n$th game, where $n \geq 1$, is uniformly at random selected from the set $\{1,2,\dots, n+2\}$. Let $W$ be the random variable representing how many times Gabe plays before he wins. Find $\mathbb{E}[W]$. If the answer is infinite, answer $-1$.
questions.hint: Use the fact that for non-negative integer-valued random variables, $\mathbb{E}[X] = \displaystyle \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \mathbb{P}[X \geq k]$. Let's compute $\mathbb{P}[W \geq k]$ for a fixed $k$. This is equivalent to saying that Gabe loses each of his first $k-1$ games.
hints: P(lose game n)=(n+1)/(n+2); P(W≥k)=Π telescopes to 2/(k+1).
This harmonic series diverges ⇒ infinite ⇒ −1.
questions.genSolution: Losing game $n$ has probability $\tfrac{n+1}{n+2}$, so $P(W\ge k)=\prod_{n=1}^{k-1}\tfrac{n+1}{n+2}=\tfrac{2}{k+1}$. Then $\mathbb{E}[W]=\sum_{k\ge1}\tfrac{2}{k+1}=\infty$, so the answer is $-1.$

questions.title: Student Appointment
questions.question: You have $5$ students that you want to see $3$ times each over the next month. There are $15$ time slots available for the students to select from. However, one of the students can't attend in the first time slot. How many different ways can these students be scheduled?
questions.hint: We can think of this as an anagrams problem. Label the students $1-5$. Each scheduling then corresponds to some anagram of $111222333444555$.
hints: Total schedules = anagrams of 111222333444555 = 15!/(3!)⁵ = 168168000.
Subtract those with the restricted student in slot 1: a fraction 3/15 of the total.
questions.genSolution: Total schedules are anagrams of $111222333444555$: $\dfrac{15!}{(3!)^5}=168168000$. The restricted student occupies slot 1 in a fraction $\tfrac{3}{15}=\tfrac15$ of these, so the count is $168168000\cdot\tfrac45=134534400.$

questions.title: Corner Meet
questions.question: You have a $5 \times 5$ checkerboard. You randomly place a checker in the center $3 \times 3$ grid. The checkers can move in two equally random ways: up to the left, and up to the right. If the checker is on the boundary, then it can only make valid moves. What is the probability that the checker ends up in the top-left or top-right corner?
questions.hint: Look at the probability of reaching a corner from each of the center squares in the $3 \times 3$ grid. Consider the top-left inner square, for example. We see that we have probability $\frac{1}{2}$ of immediately reaching the left corner and probability $\frac{1}{2}$ of reaching the top-center, in which case there's no chance of reaching a corner.
hints: From each inner square, track the random up-left/up-right walk to the top row, reflecting at the side edges.
Compute the corner-reaching probability from each of the 9 starting squares.
Average over the uniform starting square.

questions.title: Egg Drop II
questions.question: You're in a large buliding with $n$ stories holding $3$ identical eggs. If an egg is dropped at an elevation under story $X$, then the egg will survive with no harm done; otherwise, the egg breaks. At all floors on level $(1-n)$ onwards the eggs will break when dropped. What is the maximum value of $n$ such that at most $9$ drops are required to determine $X$ in the worst-case scenario?
questions.hint: In Egg Drop, a simpler variation of this problem, the strategy was to make larger jumps at lower floors in the tower so that you can test every floor afterwards in a given range. Namely, with at most $d$ drops and $2$ eggs, you could test $\dfrac{d(d+1)}{2}$ floors completely. The key for this problem is to note that once one egg breaks we can simplify this problem to the two egg case in egg drop. So, rather than making jumps that decrease linearly (done in the two egg case), make jumps that decrease by the maximum increment of the two egg case.
hints: With k eggs and d drops, the max testable floors satisfy f(d,k)=f(d−1,k−1)+f(d−1,k)+1.
This equals C(d,1)+C(d,2)+C(d,3) for 3 eggs.
Evaluate at d=9.

questions.title: Meek Mill
questions.question: Meek Mill is performing at Spring Fair. The administration does not know where he is, and they estimate there is an $80\%$ chance he is in Philadelphia and $20\%$ chance he is in Baltimore. If he is in Baltimore, there is a $80\%$ chance he will perform. If he is in Philadelphia, there is a $10\%$ chance he will perform. Find the probability that if Meek Mill performs, he came from Philadelphia.
questions.hint: Let $P$ be the event Meek Mill performs, $L$ be the event he is in Philadelphia, and $B$ be the event he is in Baltimore. You want $\mathbb{P}[L \mid P]$.
hints: P(perform & Philly)=0.8·0.1=0.08; P(perform & Baltimore)=0.2·0.8=0.16.
P(Philly | perform)=0.08/(0.08+0.16).
questions.genSolution: By Bayes, $P(\text{Philly}\mid\text{perform})=\dfrac{0.8\cdot0.1}{0.8\cdot0.1+0.2\cdot0.8}=\dfrac{0.08}{0.24}=\dfrac13.$

questions.title: Divisible Dice
questions.question: You roll $10$ fair $6-$sided dice and sum the values. What is the probability that the sum is divisible by $6$?
questions.hint: Let the sum of the first $9$ dice be $x$. What are the possible values of the total sum of the 10 dice?
hints: Condition on the first 9 dice summing to x.
The last die is uniform on 1–6, hitting each residue mod 6 exactly once.
So the total is uniform mod 6 ⇒ 1/6.
questions.genSolution: Whatever the first nine dice sum to, the tenth die is uniform on $\{1,\dots,6\}$, taking each residue mod $6$ exactly once. Hence the total is uniform mod $6$, and the probability of divisibility is $\tfrac16.$

questions.title: ABC Sum
questions.question: Find the sum of all numbers in the form $0.abcabcabc\dots$, where each of $a,b,c$ are distinct integers from $0$ to $9$.
questions.hint: How many such numbers can be formed? What is the average value of each digit?
hints: 0.abcabc… = (100a+10b+c)/999, summed over distinct ordered (a,b,c).
Each digit position sums to 72·(0+…+9)=3240 over the 720 triples.
questions.genSolution: Each repeating decimal equals $\tfrac{100a+10b+c}{999}$. Over the $10\cdot9\cdot8=720$ distinct ordered triples, each position's digits sum to $72(0+1+\cdots+9)=3240$, so the total is $\dfrac{111\cdot3240}{999}=360.$

questions.title: RNG on RNG
questions.question: You generate a uniformly random number in the interval $(0,1)$. You can generate additional random numbers as many times as you want for a fee of $\$0.02$ per generation. This decision can be made with the information of all of the previous values that have been generated. Your payout is the maximum of all the numbers you generate. Under the optimal strategy, find your expected payout of this game.
questions.hint: The optimal strategy will be in the form of a payout when you generate some value $x \geq T$ for a threshold $T$. The threshold here needs to satisfy that the payout if you stop now equals the value that you receive by playing on that doesn't involve stopping.
hints: Stop when a draw exceeds threshold T; T solves the indifference V=E[max(x,V)]−0.02.
0.5V²−V+0.48=0 ⇒ V=0.8 (the threshold).
First draw is free, so payout=∫₀¹max(x,0.8)dx = 0.64+0.18 = 0.82.
questions.genSolution: The continuation value $V$ (with the \$0.02 fee) satisfies $V=\int_0^1\max(x,V)\,dx-0.02$, i.e. $0.5V^2-V+0.48=0$, giving threshold $V=0.8$. Since the first draw is free, the expected payout is $\int_0^1\max(x,0.8)\,dx=0.64+0.18=0.82.$

questions.title: Racecar Speed
questions.question: A racecar is going around a racetrack three times. The first lap was at a speed of $100$ mph, the second lap was at $150$ mph, and the final lap was at a speed of $200$ mph. What was the average speed (in mph) of the racecar throughout all three laps?
questions.hint: Be careful with this problem, we can't simply take the average of the speeds of the laps because the racecar spends different amounts of time around each lap.
hints: Equal-distance laps: average speed is the harmonic mean.
questions.genSolution: With equal lap distances the average speed is the harmonic mean: $\dfrac{3}{\tfrac1{100}+\tfrac1{150}+\tfrac1{200}}=\dfrac{3}{13/600}=\dfrac{1800}{13}.$

questions.title: Significant Others
questions.question: Suppose that we have a set of data $\{(x_1,y_1),\dots, (x_n,y_n)\}$, where both $x_i$ and $y_i$ are real-valued and mean $0$. We fit a linear regression line without intercept to regress $y$ onto $x$. This yields a line $y = \hat{\beta}_0 x + \varepsilon$, $\varepsilon$ being an error term. Given that Corr$(X,Y) = \dfrac{1}{10\sqrt{3}}$, find the smallest value of $n$ so that the results of the hypothesis test $H_0: \beta_0 = 0$ vs. $H_A: \beta_0 > 0$ are significant at the $95\%$ level. You may use the fact that the $95$ and $97.5$ percentile values are approximately $1.645$ and $1.96$.
questions.hint: Recall that under the null hypothesis, the distribution of the $t-$statistic is $t_{n-1}$, where $t = \dfrac{\hat{\beta}_0}{s\left\{\hat{\beta}_0\right\}}$. The variance of $\hat{\beta}_0$ is Var$(\hat{\beta}_0) = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{\sum x_i^2}$ since $x_i$ are assumed to have mean $0$. $\sigma^2$ is the variance of the error term $\varepsilon$.
hints: For no-intercept regression, t = r√(n−1)/√(1−r²) under H₀.
questions.genSolution: The test statistic is $t=\dfrac{r\sqrt{n-1}}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}$. With $r=\tfrac1{10\sqrt3}$, $r^2=\tfrac1{300}$ and $\dfrac{r}{\sqrt{1-r^2}}=\dfrac1{\sqrt{299}}$, so $t=\sqrt{\tfrac{n-1}{299}}$. Requiring $t\ge1.645$ gives $n-1\ge1.645^2\cdot299\approx809.1$, so $n=811.$

questions.title: First Quarter
questions.question: You play a game with a friend. You both take turns to put a quarter on a round table (the table can be any size, but must be of symmetrical shape). The goal is to cover the table up with quarters. Quarters can't overlap with other quarters. A player loses when there's no place for them to put down a quarter. Should you be the first person to play? Answer $1$ if yes and $2$ if no.
questions.hint: What is special about the center?
hints: The table has a unique center point.
Play first at the exact center, then mirror every opponent move through the center.
You always have a reply ⇒ go first (answer 1).
questions.genSolution: Place the first quarter exactly at the center, then mirror each opponent placement through the center. Every move they can make has a symmetric reply, so you never run out first — you should go first, answer $1.$

questions.title: Inheritance Split
questions.question: A father leaves a $\$100$ inheritance to his two kids, Jack and Jill. If one-third of Jack's inheritance is to be taken from one-fourth of Jill's inheritance, the difference would be $\$11$. How much did Jack receive?
questions.hint: Let $n$ be the amount Jack receives. Then $100-n$ is what Jill receives. Write the line in the question algebraically in terms of $x$.
questions.genSolution: With Jack $=n$ and Jill $=100-n$: $\tfrac14(100-n)-\tfrac13 n=11$, i.e. $25-\tfrac{7n}{12}=11$, so $\tfrac{7n}{12}=14$ and $n=24.$

questions.title: Horse Arbitrage
questions.question: There are three horses numbered $1,2,$ and $3$. You have $\$1$ to make bets. You may make bets in fractions of dollars. If horse $1$ wins, you get $\$2$ back for a $\$1$ bet. If horse $2$ wins, you receive $\$4$ back for a $\$1$ bet. If horse $3$ wins, you receive $\$6$ back for a $\$1$ bet. Note that you do not receive your initial bet back. There is an arbitrage here. Find the maximum guaranteed profit that can be made from this arbitrage.
questions.hint: The implied odds of the respect horses are $1/2, 1/4,$ and $1/6$, respectively. These sum to strictly less than $1$, so there is an arbitrage.
hints: Bet bᵢ so payouts equalize: 2b₁=4b₂=6b₃=R, with b₁+b₂+b₃=1.
Profit = R−1 = 1/11.
questions.genSolution: Choose bets so every outcome returns $R$: $b_1=\tfrac R2,b_2=\tfrac R4,b_3=\tfrac R6$. Then $R\big(\tfrac12+\tfrac14+\tfrac16\big)=\tfrac{11}{12}R=1$, so $R=\tfrac{12}{11}$ and the guaranteed profit is $R-1=\tfrac1{11}.$

questions.title: Card Turner
questions.question: Suppose you have $20$ cards with the values $1-10$ each appearing twice in a deck. You draw $2$ cards at a time uniformly at random from the $20$ cards. If they match in value, you remove them from the deck. Otherwise, they are put back into the deck. The game finishes once there are no more cards to draw. Each drawing of two cards is a turn. Find the expected number of turns needed to finish the game.
questions.hint: Let $T$ be the total duration of the game. Then $T = T_1 + T_2 + \dots + T_{10}$, where $T_i$ is the amount of draws needed to go from $i-1$ pairs drawn to $i$ pairs drawn.
hints: With k pairs left (2k cards), a draw removes a pair w.p. 1/(2k−1).
So removing one pair takes a geometric mean of 2k−1 turns.
questions.genSolution: When $k$ pairs remain ($2k$ cards), a random draw of two matches with probability $\dfrac{k}{\binom{2k}{2}}=\dfrac1{2k-1}$, so clearing that pair takes on average $2k-1$ turns. Summing, $\mathbb{E}[T]=\sum_{k=1}^{10}(2k-1)=100.$

questions.title: Animal Farm
questions.question: A farmer goes to the market and spends $\$1000$ on $100$ animals. The farmer buys at least one of each animal. The cost of each cow is $\$50$. The cost of each sheep is $\$10$. The cost of each chicken is $\$0.50$. Let $x_1,x_2,$ and $x_3$ represent the number of cows, sheep, and chickens that the farmer bought, respectively. Find $x_1x_2x_3$.
questions.hint: A farmer goes to the market and spends $\$1000$ on $100$ animals. The cost of each cow is $\$50$. The cost of each sheep is $\$10$. The cost of each chicken is $\$0.50$. Let $x_1,x_2,$ and $x_3$ represent the number of cows, sheep, and chickens that the farmer bought, respectively. Find $x_1x_2x_3$.
hints: Mod 99: 19x₂≡19 ⇒ x₂=1, then x₁=19, x₃=80.
questions.genSolution: Doubling the cost equation and subtracting the count equation gives $99x_1+19x_2=1900$. Reducing mod $99$, $x_2=1$, so $x_1=\tfrac{1881}{99}=19$ and $x_3=80$. Thus $x_1x_2x_3=19\cdot1\cdot80=1520.$

questions.title: Mean Cards
questions.question: Suppose you have a deck of $100$ cards labeled with values $1-100$. The deck is thoroughly shuffled and cards are dealt one-by-one from the top of the deck. For every card that is dealt, you may either keep it or discard it. Discarding a card costs $\$1$. At the end, you are paid out the average value of all the cards you keep. What is the optimal strategy and expected profit on this game?
questions.hint: The optimal strategy will be the in the form of keeping all cards at least $k$ for some threshold $k$. Therefore, as a function of $k$, say $f(k)$, find the expected profit of the strategy.
hints: Threshold strategy: keep all cards ≥ k; f(k)=(100+k)/2 − (k−1).
f(k)=51 − k/2 is decreasing in k.
Best is k=1 (keep everything): f(1)=101/2.
questions.siteSolution:
The optimal strategy will be the in the form of keeping all cards at least $k$ for some threshold $k$. Therefore, as a function of $k$, say $f(k)$, let's find the expected profit of the strategy.


 $$ 
 $$ 


The mean of all of the cards valued at least $k$ is $\dfrac{100+k}{2}$, which would be our payout. Then, we need to factor in the discarding cost, so we would discard all the $k-1$ cards below value $k$. Therefore, $f(k) = \dfrac{100+k}{2} - (k-1) = 51 - \dfrac{k}{2}$ for $1 \leq k \leq 100$. This is a decreasing function as $k$ increases, so the optimal strategy is just to keep all of the cards! The expected payoff from this strategy is $51 - \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{101}{2}$.
questions.genSolution: Keeping all cards $\ge k$ pays the mean $\tfrac{100+k}{2}$ minus the $k-1$ discard costs: $f(k)=51-\tfrac k2$, decreasing in $k$. So keep everything ($k=1$), giving $f(1)=\tfrac{101}{2}.$

questions.title: Binary Option
questions.question:
Consider an underlying with $S_0 = 25$. We have the following derivatives contract: this contract pays $1$ if $S_T \ge 24$ and $-1$ otherwise. We have access to the following binary calls (a contract paying $1$ if $S_T \ge K$ and $0$ otherwise). The calls are given in the format of $(\text{Strike } K, \text{Price } C_0)$


 $$ 
\begin{align*}
(25, 0.6) \\ 
(24, 0.73) \\ 
(23, 0.88) \\ 
\end{align*}
 $$ 


Give the time-$0$ price of the derivatives contract. Bonds pay $1$ at time-$T$ and have time-$0$ price $B_0 = 0.9$.
questions.hint: How can we replicate the payoff using binary calls?
hints: Payoff = 2·1(S≥24) − 1; replicate with 2 binary calls at K=24 minus 1 bond.
questions.genSolution: The payoff is $2\cdot\mathbf 1(S_T\ge24)-1$, replicated by $2$ binary calls struck at $24$ minus one bond. The price is $2(0.73)-0.9=1.46-0.9=0.56.$

questions.title: Distinct Date I
questions.question: Find the next date where all of the digits, when expressed in the form $MM/DD/YYYY$ are distinct. For example, $01/23/4567$ would be a valid date. Express your answer in the form $MMDDYYYY$.
questions.hint: We want to minimize the year, as that will be the biggest influence on how soon it is. Note that the first $M$ is either $0$ or $1$. If the first $M$ is $0$, then the first day must be $1$ or $2$ OR the day is $31$ ($30$ doesn't work since we already have a $0$. If the first $M$ is $1$, then either the second $M$ is a $0$ OR the second $M$ is a $2$ and the day contains a $0$ somewhere.
hints: Minimize the year first; months need a leading 0 or 1 and days need small digits.
Years 20XX consume 0 and 2, blocking valid all-distinct month/day combos.
The first workable year is 2345 (digits 2,3,4,5), with 06/17 → 06/17/2345.
questions.genSolution: All-distinct dates require the four year digits plus a valid month and day with no repeats. Years $20\text{XX}$ tie up $0$ and $2$, leaving no room for a legal month/day, and the same obstruction rules out everything up to $2344$. The first feasible year is $2345$ (digits $2,3,4,5$), and the earliest month/day from the remaining $\{0,1,6,7,8,9\}$ is $06/17$. So the date is $06/17/2345$, i.e. $06172345.$

questions.title: 18 Sides
questions.question: There is a fair 18-sided die where each side is either a 6 or a 3. The expected value of a roll is 4. How many 6s are on the 18-sided die?
questions.hint: If there are $n$ sides that are $6$, what is the expected value of the die?
hints: With n sixes and 18−n threes, EV=(6n+3(18−n))/18.
Set equal to 4: 3n+54=72.
questions.genSolution: $\dfrac{6n+3(18-n)}{18}=4\Rightarrow 3n+54=72\Rightarrow n=6.$

questions.title: Perfect Correlation III
questions.question: Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ are perfectly negatively correlated i.e. $\rho(X,Y) = -1$ with $\sigma_Y > \sigma_X$. Find the correlation of $X+Y$ and $X-Y$.
questions.hint: Suppose that $X$ and $Y$ have respective variances $\sigma_X^2$ and $\sigma_Y^2$. Compute $\text{Cov}(X+Y,X-Y)$ and their respective variances using the common formulae.
hints: Cov(X+Y,X−Y)=Var(X)−Var(Y)=σ_X²−σ_Y².
With ρ=−1: Var(X+Y)=(σ_X−σ_Y)², Var(X−Y)=(σ_X+σ_Y)².
Corr = (σ_X²−σ_Y²)/((σ_Y−σ_X)(σ_X+σ_Y)) = −1.
questions.genSolution: $\mathrm{Cov}(X+Y,X-Y)=\sigma_X^2-\sigma_Y^2$. Since $\rho=-1$, $\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y)=-\sigma_X\sigma_Y$, giving $\mathrm{Var}(X+Y)=(\sigma_X-\sigma_Y)^2$ and $\mathrm{Var}(X-Y)=(\sigma_X+\sigma_Y)^2$. With $\sigma_Y>\sigma_X$, $\mathrm{Corr}=\dfrac{\sigma_X^2-\sigma_Y^2}{(\sigma_Y-\sigma_X)(\sigma_X+\sigma_Y)}=-1.$
